2: This dissertation is the culmination of five wonderful years at UW-Madison. I would not be writing these acknowledgements without all the support from the Biological Systems Engineering family. I am grateful to have such wonderful advisors; Doug Reinemann, who gave me the opportunity to start my Ph.D., and Becky Larson, who gave me the support to finish it. They gave me the freedom to work on my research independently, but were always there to provide guidance. I will always be grateful for all their understanding and for believing in me. I also want to thank the members of my committee Robert Anex, Tracey Holloway, and Troy Runge for all the valuable comments and insights that helped improve my work.

I would like to acknowledge the support of many people who were always available to answer my questions during these years: Debby Sumwalt for always helping me to get the information and resources I was looking for. I will miss our unpredictable talks during lunch; Sue Reinen, Pam Spahn, and Jeremy Wall for facilitating SO much all administrative work; Jeffrey Nelson for the technical support; Brian Holmes and Scott Sanford for providing material and resources when I could not find them anywhere else.

I am thankful for my research team: Thais Passos Fonseca, who was the first person to give me her hand when I arrived to Madison; Julie Sinistore who guided through the process of learning about LCA; Simone Kraatz, Franco Milani, and Astrid Newenhouse, who were courageous enough to collaborate with me; Alysa Bradley, who patiently gathered massive amounts of data; Asly Ozkaynak, Patrick Triscari, Michael Holly, and Hui Wang who put up with me during conference trips; and John Norman, Lou Armentano, and Victor Cabrera, who were fundamental in the completion of the Green Cheese project.

I would like to thank my friends who shared with me unforgettable moments throughout these years: Jasmeet Lamba, Harsh Singh, Jack Buchanan, Sonia Ares, Arturo Gomez, Edgardo Ortiz Reyes, Zong Liu, Craig Slattery, Shashi Dunghel, Kate Zipp, Pilar Jano, Juan Vivanco, Nick Hatt, Chris Kanter, John Liu, Ya-Ting Chuang, Thilina Gunawardhana, Sampath Gunukula, Jordi Francis, Andrew Holstein, Zach Zopp. Thank you for your friendship!

Finally and most importantly, I thank my family for all their support. My father and mother, thank you for unconditionally being there for me and for being the best role models that I could have. My brothers Sergio and Rodrigo and my grandmother Salome, thank you for believing in me. To my sister in law Monica, thank you for your infinite patience, valuable advices, and guidance through this process and beyond. To my wife Pati, my dear companion, my friend, my love. She has always encouraged me to continue growing professionally and personally. Thank your for sharing with me the happiest moments of my life and for holding my hand during the tough times.

5: <header>Abstract </header>

With an increasingly growing and wealthier population that demands more food and fuel, the pressure on the planet's ecosystems threatens to reach levels that cannot be sustained. Animal agriculture presents multiple challenges for sustainability and the dairy sector alone contributes 30% of agricultural greenhouse gas emissions. Bioenergy systems have been implemented to reduce GHG emissions and contribute to energy independence goals. The production of bioenergy must be done with caution to avoid the generation of additional environmental impacts. This dissertation contributes to the debate on the sustainability of agricultural systems and integrates dairy and bio-energy systems to address global warming and energy security using lifecycle techniques. Overall, the results of this comprehensive analysis are intended to inform not only researchers and dairy processors, but guide the development of new climate and energy policies specific to the dairy and bioenergy sectors.

Chapter two evaluates how including dry distillers grains with solubles and soybean meal into the dairy diet affect energy intensity, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and direct land-use of milk production. This is done by conducting a cradle-to-farm gate lifecycle assessment and by expanding the system boundaries of the dairy farm to include ethanol and biodiesel production. Findings highlight that manure management is one of the main contributors to GHG emissions. Results also show that scenarios that maximize dry distillers grains with solubles in the dairy diet achieve lower GHG emissions and energy intensity, but lead to higher land area requirements.

Chapter three develops a novel method combining subdivision and allocation concepts to reduce the variability when partitioning the environmental impacts of a multi-functional system producing cheese, dried whey, and whey cream. GHG emissions and energy intensity are

analyzed to capture the effect of this method on results. After running several sensitivity tests, it is shown that the variability of results is significantly reduced with the new method when compared to the traditional allocation methods.

Chapter four combines process-based and LCA techniques to test the environmental impacts of different manure processing pathways that include anaerobic digestion and solid-liquid separation. Findings suggest that anaerobic digestion has the largest reductions in GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels, but also the largest increases in ammonia emissions. The study highlights the need of using multiple sustainability indicators to evaluate and understand the trade-offs between manure pathways.

Chapter five evaluates different anaerobic digestion pathways to reduce environmental impacts and increase energy production. Pathways are defined based on multiple management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes. Results show that management practices can achieve significant reductions in greenhouse gas and ammonia emissions. Co-digesting manure with corn stover and switchgrass improve sustainability indicators. The results of this study could provide clear guidelines to implement policies or strategies that seek to minimize emissions and maximize energy production from anaerobic digestion systems.

9: <header>Abstract </header>

With an increasingly growing and wealthier population that demands more food and fuel, the pressure on the planet's ecosystems threatens to reach levels that cannot be sustained. Animal agriculture presents multiple challenges for sustainability and the dairy sector alone contributes 30% of agricultural greenhouse gas emissions. Bioenergy systems have been implemented to reduce GHG emissions and contribute to energy independence goals. The production of bioenergy must be done with caution to avoid the generation of additional environmental impacts. This dissertation contributes to the debate on the sustainability of agricultural systems and integrates dairy and bio-energy systems to address global warming and energy security using lifecycle techniques. Overall, the results of this comprehensive analysis are intended to inform not only researchers and dairy processors, but guide the development of new climate and energy policies specific to the dairy and bioenergy sectors.

Chapter two evaluates how including dry distillers grains with solubles and soybean meal into the dairy diet affect energy intensity, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and direct land-use of milk production. This is done by conducting a cradle-to-farm gate lifecycle assessment and by expanding the system boundaries of the dairy farm to include ethanol and biodiesel production. Findings highlight that manure management is one of the main contributors to GHG emissions. Results also show that scenarios that maximize dry distillers grains with solubles in the dairy diet achieve lower GHG emissions and energy intensity, but lead to higher land area requirements.

Chapter three develops a novel method combining subdivision and allocation concepts to reduce the variability when partitioning the environmental impacts of a multi-functional system producing cheese, dried whey, and whey cream. GHG emissions and energy intensity are

analyzed to capture the effect of this method on results. After running several sensitivity tests, it is shown that the variability of results is significantly reduced with the new method when compared to the traditional allocation methods.

Chapter four combines process-based and LCA techniques to test the environmental impacts of different manure processing pathways that include anaerobic digestion and solid-liquid separation. Findings suggest that anaerobic digestion has the largest reductions in GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels, but also the largest increases in ammonia emissions. The study highlights the need of using multiple sustainability indicators to evaluate and understand the trade-offs between manure pathways.

Chapter five evaluates different anaerobic digestion pathways to reduce environmental impacts and increase energy production. Pathways are defined based on multiple management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes. Results show that management practices can achieve significant reductions in greenhouse gas and ammonia emissions. Co-digesting manure with corn stover and switchgrass improve sustainability indicators. The results of this study could provide clear guidelines to implement policies or strategies that seek to minimize emissions and maximize energy production from anaerobic digestion systems.

1: Addressing energy security, climate change, and sustainable agriculture have become top priorities of many national and regional governments. More than 80% of global energy demand is met by diminishing fossil fuel sources, which are concentrated in a few countries (IPCC 2007a). The International Energy Agency has projected that at current consumption rates, oil reserves will last only another 40-45 years, natural gas 55-60 years, and coal 110-120 years. These are optimistic predictions considering that global energy demand is also projected to rise 40% in the next 20 years (IEA 2013).

Combustion of fossil fuels is the leading contributor to climate change. According to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), greenhouse gas emissions (GHG) have increased 70% between 1970 and 2004 and are projected to increase 25% to 90% between 2000 and 2030 (IPCC 2007b). Agriculture contributes nearly 14% of global GHG emissions, with ruminant animal production systems being one of the most important contributors (IPCC 2007c; Mosier et al. 1998). A recent study by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations concluded that the dairy sector alone contributes 4% of global GHG emissions. On-farm sources such as soils, cropping systems, enteric fermentation, and manure management produce 80% of dairy emissions (FAO 2010). Manure disposal constitutes a major environmental concern of dairy farms as it represents to 7% of all N2O and CH4 emissions from the agricultural sector.

In recent decades bio-energy systems have been implemented to accomplish the two-fold goal of addressing both energy independence and global warming. Despite their increased popularity, there have been opposing opinions around the effectiveness of bioenergy systems to achieve positive energy gains and reduce GHG emissions when compared to fossil fuels, given that the production of bioenergy feedstocks is resource-use intensive (Pimentel and Patzek 2005; Farrell et al. 2006). Using agricultural wastes, such as manure, to produce bioenergy in anaerobic digestion (AD) systems has received increased attention due to the availability of manure on the farm. Despite the advantages of AD, little is known about the lifecycle emissions and energy gains.

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is the preferred method to calculate GHG emissions from the lifecycle of a product (Reap et al. 2008). The LCA methods are being increasingly used in the agricultural sector; however, there is still need for improved LCA methods applied to these multi-functional systems. There is an active debate around the sensitivity of LCA results due to assumptions made during the lifecycle inventory stage, the definition of the system boundaries, and decisions regarding the allocation method. Agricultural systems present further challenges as biological processes are more variable when compared to industrial processes.

As population increases and income levels rise around the world, we face the challenge of meeting food and energy needs while reducing the environmental impacts associated with increased consumption (Godfray et al. 2010). The integration of bioenergy systems with agricultural activities offers a unique opportunity to respond to this challenge. Developing lifecycle inventory data and precise LCA methods are needed to effectively quantify and evaluate these integrated systems and to guide energy and environmental policy frameworks.

Despite their increased popularity, there have been opposing opinions around the effectiveness of bioenergy systems to achieve positive energy gains and reduce GHG emissions when compared to fossil fuels, given that the production of bioenergy feedstocks is resource-use intensive (Pimentel and Patzek 2005; Farrell et al. 2006). Using agricultural wastes, such as manure, to produce bioenergy in anaerobic digestion (AD) systems has received increased attention due to the availability of manure on the farm. Despite the advantages of AD, little is known about the lifecycle emissions and energy gains.

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is the preferred method to calculate GHG emissions from the lifecycle of a product (Reap et al. 2008). The LCA methods are being increasingly used in the agricultural sector; however, there is still need for improved LCA methods applied to these multi-functional systems. There is an active debate around the sensitivity of LCA results due to assumptions made during the lifecycle inventory stage, the definition of the system boundaries, and decisions regarding the allocation method. Agricultural systems present further challenges as biological processes are more variable when compared to industrial processes.

As population increases and income levels rise around the world, we face the challenge of meeting food and energy needs while reducing the environmental impacts associated with increased consumption (Godfray et al. 2010). The integration of bioenergy systems with agricultural activities offers a unique opportunity to respond to this challenge. Developing lifecycle inventory data and precise LCA methods are needed to effectively quantify and evaluate these integrated systems and to guide energy and environmental policy frameworks.
5: <header>1.2 LCA: Early Development, Limitations, and Relation with Bioenergy and Policy </header>

The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) defines LCA as "the compilation and evaluation of the inputs and outputs and the potential environmental impacts of a product system during a product's lifetime" (ISO 2006). LCA began as a response to internal policies adopted by the industry sector in the early 1970s with the aim of helping the decision making process and promoting environmental benefits of a product. Some years later, several countries, such as the U.S. and Brazil, started using LCA to analyze energy, environmental, and cost impacts of producing ethanol to respond to oil shortages and price increases during the energy crises of 1973 and 1979 (Svoboda and Yeh 2010).

There are two types of LCA studies: attributional, which describe the flows to and from the lifecycle of a product or service; and consequential, which describe how flows are affected by a decision or proposed change (Finnveden et al. 2009). To date the majority of LCA studies available in the literature have taken the attributional perspective. Every LCA has four components: 1) goal and scope definition of the system boundaries and functional unit; 2) inventory analysis of resources, generation of waste, and emissions; 3) impact assessment; and 4) interpretation of results (ISO 2006).

In recent years, quantifying biofuels' net energy gain and GHG emissions has become a major point of debate in the literature as the application of different methodologies gives different results. The two general options when calculating GHG from a fuel are considering the emissions resulting from direct combustion or considering the emissions resulting from the whole lifecycle. Despite the fact that there has been evidence supporting the latter option, not all policies consider LCA when evaluating GHG emissions. One example is the EPA final version

of the Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Title V Greenhouse Gas Tailoring Rule released on May 2010 (USEPA 2010). This rule aims to address GHG emissions from stationary sources and regulate them upon combustion. The rule has received criticisms from biofuel supporters as its methodology does not include the carbon dioxide (CO2) "recycling" process that takes place through plants photosynthesis, which is one of the main environmental advantages of biofuels.

When calculating GHG emissions from a lifecycle perspective, LCA has become one of the most popular methodologies within the bioenergy sector (Singh et al. 2010). The use of LCA has been strengthened by the establishment of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA) of 2007, which expanded the scope of its Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) requiring an increasing amount of the current transportation fuel consumption to be met with advanced biofuels (EISA 2007). The RFS requires the calculation of lifecycle GHG emissions from each biofuel relative to gasoline or diesel and sets reductions of 60% for cellulosic ethanol, 50% for other advanced biofuels, and 20% for corn ethanol coming from new refineries.

Despite the increased use of LCA to calculate GHG emissions of biofuels, this method has also proven to have limitations. Results are sensitive to the assumptions and practices chosen by the modeler. For example, when comparing corn grain ethanol with gasoline, Farrell et al. (2006), Hill et al. (2006), and Shapouri et al. (2002) report positive net energy gains and decreased GHG emissions, while Pimentel and Patzek (2005) report negative energy gains and increased GHG emissions, and Graboski (2002) report positive net energy gains but increased GHG emissions. Different results may be explained by variability coming from the life cycle inventory, allocation method, and system boundaries.

Building a complete life cycle inventory can be a complicated and expensive process as it involves collecting and validating data, and conducting multiple estimations (Singh et al. 2010). As a result, researchers make assumptions when data are unavailable, incomplete, or difficult to obtain. Among some of the most common assumptions are to consider the same inputs and/or outputs of a reference system and apply them to the system under assessment (e.g. biomass yields, which may be different within regions and climatic conditions) (Davis et al. 2009)

Allocation is the process of assigning the environmental burdens of a system among the product and co-products that were produced (Singh et al. 2010). The ISO recommends avoiding allocation when possible through system subdivision or system expansion of the boundaries to include the processes or co-products that cause the allocation need (ISO, 2006). As system expansion adds complexity and work to the analysis, there are only a few LCA studies that have used it (Björklund 2002; Ekvall and Finnveden, 2001; Farrell et al. 2006). There are different approaches for allocation, being those that include physical relations (e.g. mass, carbon content, energy content) the ones recommended by the ISO. Energy allocation is the most used method for biofuels' studies in the U.S., but this method has raised some issues when the co-products are not valued for their energy contents (Gnansounou et al. 2009). Another approach used in the literature is economic allocation, which partitions the burdens between each product and co- products according to economic indicators (e.g. market prices and production costs) (Hill et al. 2006). Results can shift in magnitude and direction according to which allocation method is applied given that the relations between products and co-products are different in each system.

The boundaries of a system refers to those processes that are needed to create the product under assessment, which means following material and energy flows from both upstream (e.g. production of crops or other biomass feedstocks) and downstream processes (e.g. combustion of

a biofuel in a car) (Rebitzer et al. 2004). The terms cradle-to-gate and cradle-to-grave have been broadly used in LCA studies to refer to the boundaries of a system. Cradle-to-gate systems include all processes from raw materials extraction until the product and co-products reach the factory gate. Cradle-to-grave includes those same processes plus the utilization and disposal of the products. Even when LCAs have the same system boundaries, they may not have the same inputs or outputs in every process. Some of the most commonly analyzed material inputs for crop-based biofuels are fertilizers, pesticides, and energy. In addition, there are other inputs (e.g. machinery and infrastructure) that are not SO clearly identified but that are used in the supply chain. Including or excluding the emissions of these inputs in the analysis can influence the results (Pimentel and Patzek 2005; Hill et al. 2006; Gnansounou et al. 2009). According to Davis et al. (2009) boundaries are not limited only to the start and end points of the biofuel's supply chain. Time, geography, political context, and economic aspects are also factors that need to be described when defining the system boundaries.

Based on the previous description, it is clear that although LCA is an important and widely used tool to evaluate carbon emissions from bioenergy systems, it cannot be applied as a "one size fits all" method. In this context, it seems more appropriate to analyze the environmental impacts of a product or service from a "pathway" perspective (CARB 2009; Gibbs et al. 2008). Each pathway is different from the other and several pathways can be applied to analyze the same product. From a policy design or evaluation point of view, comparing the impacts on the same product of multiple pathways can be a useful exercise as it allows identifying inputs, processes, and technologies that maximize the product (e.g. energy) and minimize the environmental effects.

The relationship between LCA and policy is twofold. First, LCA may be used as a tool included in existing policies. Second, LCA findings and results may be used to develop new policies and regulations. The RFS2 is a clear example of how LCA has been included in this policy by allowing the estimation of GHG emission reductions from biofuels. The results coming from these studies have become valuable inputs for the development of new and more informed policies related to energy independence and GHG emissions, such as the California Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS). The LCFS has shown how different biofuel pathways can be used to guide the development of specific environmental policy frameworks.

In recent years, quantifying biofuels' net energy gain and GHG emissions has become a major point of debate in the literature as the application of different methodologies gives different results. The two general options when calculating GHG from a fuel are considering the emissions resulting from direct combustion or considering the emissions resulting from the whole lifecycle. Despite the fact that there has been evidence supporting the latter option, not all policies consider LCA when evaluating GHG emissions. One example is the EPA final version

of the Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Title V Greenhouse Gas Tailoring Rule released on May 2010 (USEPA 2010). This rule aims to address GHG emissions from stationary sources and regulate them upon combustion. The rule has received criticisms from biofuel supporters as its methodology does not include the carbon dioxide (CO2) "recycling" process that takes place through plants photosynthesis, which is one of the main environmental advantages of biofuels.

When calculating GHG emissions from a lifecycle perspective, LCA has become one of the most popular methodologies within the bioenergy sector (Singh et al. 2010). The use of LCA has been strengthened by the establishment of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA) of 2007, which expanded the scope of its Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) requiring an increasing amount of the current transportation fuel consumption to be met with advanced biofuels (EISA 2007). The RFS requires the calculation of lifecycle GHG emissions from each biofuel relative to gasoline or diesel and sets reductions of 60% for cellulosic ethanol, 50% for other advanced biofuels, and 20% for corn ethanol coming from new refineries.

Despite the increased use of LCA to calculate GHG emissions of biofuels, this method has also proven to have limitations. Results are sensitive to the assumptions and practices chosen by the modeler. For example, when comparing corn grain ethanol with gasoline, Farrell et al. (2006), Hill et al. (2006), and Shapouri et al. (2002) report positive net energy gains and decreased GHG emissions, while Pimentel and Patzek (2005) report negative energy gains and increased GHG emissions, and Graboski (2002) report positive net energy gains but increased GHG emissions. Different results may be explained by variability coming from the life cycle inventory, allocation method, and system boundaries.

Building a complete life cycle inventory can be a complicated and expensive process as it involves collecting and validating data, and conducting multiple estimations (Singh et al. 2010). As a result, researchers make assumptions when data are unavailable, incomplete, or difficult to obtain. Among some of the most common assumptions are to consider the same inputs and/or outputs of a reference system and apply them to the system under assessment (e.g. biomass yields, which may be different within regions and climatic conditions) (Davis et al. 2009)

Allocation is the process of assigning the environmental burdens of a system among the product and co-products that were produced (Singh et al. 2010). The ISO recommends avoiding allocation when possible through system subdivision or system expansion of the boundaries to include the processes or co-products that cause the allocation need (ISO, 2006). As system expansion adds complexity and work to the analysis, there are only a few LCA studies that have used it (Björklund 2002; Ekvall and Finnveden, 2001; Farrell et al. 2006). There are different approaches for allocation, being those that include physical relations (e.g. mass, carbon content, energy content) the ones recommended by the ISO. Energy allocation is the most used method for biofuels' studies in the U.S., but this method has raised some issues when the co-products are not valued for their energy contents (Gnansounou et al. 2009). Another approach used in the literature is economic allocation, which partitions the burdens between each product and co- products according to economic indicators (e.g. market prices and production costs) (Hill et al. 2006). Results can shift in magnitude and direction according to which allocation method is applied given that the relations between products and co-products are different in each system.

The boundaries of a system refers to those processes that are needed to create the product under assessment, which means following material and energy flows from both upstream (e.g. production of crops or other biomass feedstocks) and downstream processes (e.g. combustion of

a biofuel in a car) (Rebitzer et al. 2004). The terms cradle-to-gate and cradle-to-grave have been broadly used in LCA studies to refer to the boundaries of a system. Cradle-to-gate systems include all processes from raw materials extraction until the product and co-products reach the factory gate. Cradle-to-grave includes those same processes plus the utilization and disposal of the products. Even when LCAs have the same system boundaries, they may not have the same inputs or outputs in every process. Some of the most commonly analyzed material inputs for crop-based biofuels are fertilizers, pesticides, and energy. In addition, there are other inputs (e.g. machinery and infrastructure) that are not SO clearly identified but that are used in the supply chain. Including or excluding the emissions of these inputs in the analysis can influence the results (Pimentel and Patzek 2005; Hill et al. 2006; Gnansounou et al. 2009). According to Davis et al. (2009) boundaries are not limited only to the start and end points of the biofuel's supply chain. Time, geography, political context, and economic aspects are also factors that need to be described when defining the system boundaries.

Based on the previous description, it is clear that although LCA is an important and widely used tool to evaluate carbon emissions from bioenergy systems, it cannot be applied as a "one size fits all" method. In this context, it seems more appropriate to analyze the environmental impacts of a product or service from a "pathway" perspective (CARB 2009; Gibbs et al. 2008). Each pathway is different from the other and several pathways can be applied to analyze the same product. From a policy design or evaluation point of view, comparing the impacts on the same product of multiple pathways can be a useful exercise as it allows identifying inputs, processes, and technologies that maximize the product (e.g. energy) and minimize the environmental effects.

The relationship between LCA and policy is twofold. First, LCA may be used as a tool included in existing policies. Second, LCA findings and results may be used to develop new policies and regulations. The RFS2 is a clear example of how LCA has been included in this policy by allowing the estimation of GHG emission reductions from biofuels. The results coming from these studies have become valuable inputs for the development of new and more informed policies related to energy independence and GHG emissions, such as the California Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS). The LCFS has shown how different biofuel pathways can be used to guide the development of specific environmental policy frameworks.

As a result, researchers make assumptions when data are unavailable, incomplete, or difficult to obtain. Allocation is the process of assigning the environmental burdens of a system among the product and co-products that were produced (Singh et al. 2010). The ISO recommends avoiding allocation when possible through system subdivision or system expansion of the boundaries to include the processes or co-products that cause the allocation need (ISO, 2006). As system expansion adds complexity and work to the analysis, there are only a few LCA studies that have used it (Björklund 2002; Ekvall and Finnveden, 2001; Farrell et al. 2006). There are different approaches for allocation, being those that include physical relations (e.g. mass, carbon content, energy content) the ones recommended by the ISO. Energy allocation is the most used method for biofuels' studies in the U.S., but this method has raised some issues when the co-products are not valued for their energy contents (Gnansounou et al. 2009). Another approach used in the literature is economic allocation, which partitions the burdens between each product and co- products according to economic indicators (e.g. market prices and production costs) (Hill et al. 2006). Results can shift in magnitude and direction according to which allocation method is applied given that the relations between products and co-products are different in each system.

The boundaries of a system refers to those processes that are needed to create the product under assessment, which means following material and energy flows from both upstream (e.g. production of crops or other biomass feedstocks) and downstream processes (e.g. combustion of

a biofuel in a car) (Rebitzer et al. 2004). The terms cradle-to-gate and cradle-to-grave have been broadly used in LCA studies to refer to the boundaries of a system. Cradle-to-gate systems include all processes from raw materials extraction until the product and co-products reach the factory gate. Cradle-to-grave includes those same processes plus the utilization and disposal of the products. Even when LCAs have the same system boundaries, they may not have the same inputs or outputs in every process. Some of the most commonly analyzed material inputs for crop-based biofuels are fertilizers, pesticides, and energy. In addition, there are other inputs (e.g. machinery and infrastructure) that are not SO clearly identified but that are used in the supply chain. Including or excluding the emissions of these inputs in the analysis can influence the results (Pimentel and Patzek 2005; Hill et al. 2006; Gnansounou et al. 2009). According to Davis et al. (2009) boundaries are not limited only to the start and end points of the biofuel's supply chain. Time, geography, political context, and economic aspects are also factors that need to be described when defining the system boundaries.

Based on the previous description, it is clear that although LCA is an important and widely used tool to evaluate carbon emissions from bioenergy systems, it cannot be applied as a "one size fits all" method. In this context, it seems more appropriate to analyze the environmental impacts of a product or service from a "pathway" perspective (CARB 2009; Gibbs et al. 2008). Each pathway is different from the other and several pathways can be applied to analyze the same product. From a policy design or evaluation point of view, comparing the impacts on the same product of multiple pathways can be a useful exercise as it allows identifying inputs, processes, and technologies that maximize the product (e.g. energy) and minimize the environmental effects.

The relationship between LCA and policy is twofold. First, LCA may be used as a tool included in existing policies. Second, LCA findings and results may be used to develop new policies and regulations. The RFS2 is a clear example of how LCA has been included in this policy by allowing the estimation of GHG emission reductions from biofuels. The results coming from these studies have become valuable inputs for the development of new and more informed policies related to energy independence and GHG emissions, such as the California Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS). The LCFS has shown how different biofuel pathways can be used to guide the development of specific environmental policy frameworks.
14: <header>1.3 Dairy and Bioenergy in Wisconsin </header>

Dairy is one of the most important sectors in Wisconsin (WI), representing 6% of total state sales (Deller and Williams 2011). WI is the second largest milk producer state in the U.S. and also the second in terms of dairy COWS population. The approximately 1.2 million milking COWS produce more than 11 billion kilograms of milk (NASS 2013). Nearly 85% of this milk is processed into cheese, making WI the leading cheese producer in the U.S. with 26% of the national production. The dairy sector in WI not only provides food to meet regional and national demands, but has also environmental consequences as it releases emissions to air and water, mostly through enteric methane and manure (FAO 2010; Reinemann et al. 2011) The cow population in WI produces approximately 4.7 million dry tons of manure annually. This constitutes a major environmental concern, but it is the most immediate and abundant biomass source for energy generation (Radloff et al. 2012). To date, more than 30 farms in the state are producing their own energy using anaerobic digestion systems.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a microbial conversion process that breaks down organic materials in the absence of oxygen. There are four stages in AD: hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, and methanogenesis, in which methane forming bacteria produce biogas, which is a mix of methane and carbon dioxide. In WI, biogas is mostly combusted to generate electricity, but biogas can also be upgraded and compressed to be used as a transportation fuel, or combusted directly to produce thermal energy. The remaining non-gaseous fraction, known as digestate, is usually separated into solid and liquid streams with a mechanical separator. The liquid fraction is land-applied as fertilizer, as it maintains the nutrients of the incoming feedstock, and the solid fraction is mostly used as animal bedding, but it can also be composted, land-applied, or converted to other products due to its fiber content.

In addition to biogas, corn ethanol is also an important bioenergy source in WI. The state has an installed capacity to produce 1.9 billion liters of ethanol and more than 1 billion kg of Dry Distillers Grains with Solubles (DDGS) per year (Sinistore and Bland 2010). DDGS are a co- product of the ethanol production process and are usually used as animal feed. Hermans et al. (2006) estimate that if DDGS were fed to COWS at its maximum rate, the associated amount of ethanol would replace 15% of gasoline. WI has a well-established agricultural industry that offers valuable synergies to develop and establish bioenergy systems. In addition, its internal energy supply is currently limited due to the scarcity of fossil fuel reserves. WI has a clear competitive advantage for biogas production due to the availability and reliability of manure as a biomass resource, and corn ethanol due to the demand of DDGS by the dairy cow population.

17: <header>1.4 Dissertation Objectives and Structure </header>

This dissertation contributes to the debate on the sustainability of agricultural production systems and its integration with bio-energy systems to address global warming and energy security. In particular, this work proposes innovative strategies to improve the environmental sustainability of dairy systems in Wisconsin. Through enriching collaborations with departments of Environmental Studies, Animal Sciences, and Soil Sciences from UW-Madison, this dissertation combines primary and secondary data and applies LCA techniques to quantify environmental impacts. Throughout the analysis, the concept of pathways is used to capture the influence of different properties of the system under study and different methodological decisions proper for LCA. Overall, the results of this comprehensive analysis are intended to inform researchers and dairy processors, but they also aim to guide the development of climate and energy policies specific to the dairy and bioenergy sectors.

Chapter two addresses the problem that on-farm operations are both major GHG emitters and energy consumers throughout the lifecycle of dairy products. This chapter presents new insights on how to reduce these impacts by integrating dry distillers grains with solubles (DDGS) and soybean meal (SBM) into the dairy diet without affecting the nutritional content of the produced milk. An innovative approach of expanding the system boundaries of the dairy farm is adopted to model the dairy and bioenergy integrated system. A cradle-to-farm gate LCA is conducted to quantify the effects of five dairy diets with varying amounts of DDGS and SBM on energy intensity, GHG emissions, and direct land-use of milk production. Given that manure is an important contributor to environmental impacts, a preliminary assessment of anaerobic digestion is also conducted. The results of this chapter have been translated into an online tool that is
currently providing farmers, dairy processors, and policy makers with information on the environmental impacts of dairy farms.

Chapter three focuses on the dairy manufacturing process and analyzes GHG emissions and energy intensity of producing cheese and whey at the dairy plant level. Besides offering novel environmental impact calculations, as this type of study has not been conducted in the U.S., this work also proposes methodological improvements to some of the LCA allocation limitations. In particular, this chapter responds to the ongoing debate on how to partition the environmental impacts in systems that produce more than one product by proposing a two-step approach that combines the traditional allocation strategies, based on economic values or physical characteristics of products, with a sub-division strategy. The results of this chapter were published in Transactions of ASABE (2012).

Chapter four builds on previous conclusions that manure is a major contributor to environmental impacts in dairy systems, and evaluates how different manure processing pathways can improve these impacts. In particular, it evaluates how solid-liquid separation (SLS) and anaerobic digestion (AD) affect depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), nutrient balances, and GHG and ammonia (NH3) emissions, when compared to a base-case scenario of applying manure directly to the land. Process-based and LCA techniques are used to maximize the representativeness of the model to the local conditions of WI. In addition, attributional lifecycle concepts are used to evaluate each individual pathway and consequential lifecycle concepts are used to compare the marginal changes between pathways. This is one of the first studies conducting a comprehensive assessment on manure, which highlights the need for using multiple sustainability indicators to evaluate and understand the trade-offs between manure pathways. The results of this chapter were recently published in Biofuels, Bioproducts and Biorefining (2014).

Given the opportunity of AD systems to reduce GHGs and DFF, Chapter five evaluates different AD pathways that seek to increase energy production and minimize environmental impacts. This chapter is particularly interested in understanding the effect of management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes on GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, and energy return on investment ratios (EROI). Management practices include manure collection, digestate separation, digestate land application, and solid digestate use as cow bedding. Co-digestion strategies include mixing manure with corn stover and switchgrass. Energy conversion processes include electricity production with heat recovery and upgrading and compressing biogas to be used as a transportation fuel. The range of AD pathways coming from this chapter could help current dairy farmers with AD systems to adopt new strategies or help new adopters to define a specific pathway according to their priorities and needs.

0: <title>Green cheese: Partial life cycle assessment of greenhouse gas emissions and energy intensity of integrated dairy and bioenergy systems </title>
H.A. Aguirre-Villegas, T. H. Passos-Fonseca, D. J. Reinemann, L. E. Armentano, M. A. Wattiaux, V. E. Cabrera, J. M. Norman, and R. Larson
1: The objective of this study is to evaluate the effect of integrating diary and bioenergy systems on land use, net energy intensity (NEI), and GHG emissions. A reference dairy farm system representative of Wisconsin (WI) is compared to a system that produces dairy and bioenergy products. This integrated system simulates five lactating cow diet scenarios supplemented with co-products from corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel production, and two manure management scenarios. The diets are characterized by including varying amounts of dry distillers grains with solubles (DDGS) and soybean meal (SBM), and are balanced with different types of forages. The manure management scenarios include manure land-application, which is the most common manure disposal method in WI, and manure anaerobic digestion (AD) to produce biogas. A partial life cycle assessment (LCA) from cradle-to-farm gate is conducted, where the boundaries of the system are expanded to include the production of bio-fuels in the analysis, and the environmental burdens between milk and bioenergy products are partitioned by system expansion. By adopting this approach, ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas are co-products of milk, and their production is scaled to meet the dietary requirements of each selected dairy ration. Results indicate that land use is 1.6 m² 2 NEI is 3.78 MJ, and GHG emissions are 1.02 kg CO2-eq per kg

of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) for the reference system. Within the integrated dairy and bioenergy system, diet scenarios that maximize DDGS and implement AD have the biggest reduction of GHG emissions and NEI, but the biggest increase in land use in comparison to the reference system. Average land use ranges from 1.68 to 2.01 m²/kg FPCM; NEI ranges from - 5.63 to -0.73 MJ/kg FPCM; and GHG emissions range from 0.63 to 0.77 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. AD contributes 65% of the NEI and 77% of the GHG emission reductions.

3: Cropland and pasture for the production of food, fiber, and fuel occupy nearly 40% of the earth's land surface (Foley et al. 2005), use 3-8% of total energy consumption (excluding food processing and transport by agro-industries) (FAO 2000), and emit approximately 13.5% of the total anthropogenic greenhouse gases (GHG) in the world (IPCC 2007). Moreover, agricultural activities account for 85% and 50% of global nitrous oxide (N20) and methane (CH4) emissions, which represents 70% of non-carbon dioxide emissions of the agricultural sector (IPCC 2007). Most N2O emissions originate from soils due to the application of fertilizers and animal manures, and the majority of CH4 emissions come from livestock enteric fermentation, making of animal agriculture an important target of emission reduction strategies.

Land use, energy intensity, and GHG emissions can be assessed by environmental indicators derived from input-output accounting methods that focus only on on-farm impacts, but more comprehensive estimates derive from life cycle assessment (LCA) indicators (Thomassen and de Boer 2008). LCA has arisen as the preferred methodology to estimate the environmental impacts of agricultural related systems (Reap et al. 2008). A recent LCA study by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations estimates that the dairy sector alone contributes

4% (+-26%) of total global anthropogenic GHG emissions (FAO 2010). An analysis of 60 studies by the International Dairy Federation concludes that about 80% of the GHG emissions from dairy production originate from on-farm sources; mainly soils, crop production, cows' enteric fermentation, and manure (IDF 2009). This same study estimates that on-farm processes represent 40% of the total energy consumption of dairy products manufacturing from a lifecycle perspective.

Numerous LCAs report varying results of GHG emissions from milk production depending on milk yield per cow, dairy diets, and manure management (e.g., Cederberg and Stadig 2003; Rotz et al. 2010; Mc Geough et al. 2012; Thoma et al. 2013). According to these studies, CH4 from gastro-enteric fermentation is the largest single contributor to GHG emissions, followed by CH4 from manure management and N2O from soils where dairy feeds are grown. Consequently, different studies have evaluated adjustments in dairy diet compositions to reduce enteric CH4 emissions from cows (Johnson and Johnson 1995; Johnson et al. 2007; Martin et al. 2008). The composition of the dairy diet plays an important role in GHG emissions, not only by affecting enteric emissions from COWS (direct effect) and changing the quantities or types of crops grown (indirect effect), but often times by interacting with bioenergy production (synergistic effect).

Effectiveness of GHG mitigation methods during manure management has also been evaluated (Boadi et al. 2004; Del Prado et al. 2010). For example, GHG emissions from dairy can be reduced when manure is land applied for crops or pasture fertilization given that manure displaces synthetic fertilizers that require high fossil energy inputs. Dairy manure can also be processed in anaerobic digesters to generate biogas. This renewable gas is 50% to 65% CH4 and can be collected and used for direct burn applications, conversion to electricity, or compressed fuel (Appels et al. 2011). Following digestion, the manure or digestate can still be land-applied

as fertilizer and emits less CH4 than non-digested manure during storage as it contains less carbon (C) and volatile solids (VS) (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014). Moreover, the combustion of CH4 (otherwise emitted from stored liquid manure) reduces GHG emissions by converting CH4 into carbon dioxide (CO2) and by displacing fossil fuels used to produce heat or electricity.

This study focuses on Wisconsin (WI), the second largest milk producer state in the United States (US) and also the second in terms of dairy cows population (USDA-NASS 2013). As a result, the majority of the state's agricultural emissions occur in the form of enteric CH4, unlike emissions from agricultural practices in other Midwest states, which occur mostly in the form of N2O from corn production and crop management (WRI 2007). The Wisconsin's Strategy for Reducing Global Warming includes proposals that increase the capture and use of animal CH4 for electricity or heat; reduce current CH4 emissions; reduce the application of nitrogen and the overall use of chemical fertilizers; and mandate the adoption of nutrient management practices (WI Global Warming Task Force 2008). Despite these efforts, little is still known about the life cycle environmental impacts and benefits of implementing these strategies in a dairy farm.

Dairy diets in WI typically combine corn silage, alfalfa silage, corn grain, and cotton seed with soybean meal (SBM), which is the co-product from the extraction of soy oil that is typically used for food or biodiesel production. More recently, dry distillers grains with solubles (DDGS), a co- product of the corn ethanol industry, has also been added to the dairy diet of confined herds due to the immediate availability of this resource. The corn ethanol industry in WI has an installed capacity to produce 1.9 billion liters of ethanol and more than 1 billion kg of DDGS per year. Hermans et al. (2006) estimated that about 15% of the gasoline used in WI could be replaced by ethanol based on maximum allowable DDGS consumption by the state's 1.2 million dairy cows. Similarly, biodiesel from soy oil could replace approximately 6% of the locally consumed

petroleum diesel based on maximum allowable SBM consumption by the state's dairy cows. The inclusion of locally produced SBM and DDGS in the dairy diet could further reduce GHG emissions and fossil energy consumption as they do not need to be transported from out-of-state crushing facilities.

In the case of a multi-functional system where corn ethanol and DDGS are produced, the environmental burdens should be assigned to both of these products, given that DDGS provide benefits to the system when used as animal feed. Bremer et al. (2010) indicate that assigning GHG credits to DDGS can represent 19 to 38% of total life cycle GHG emissions of corn ethanol production. Defining how much of the inputs and environmental burdens should be attributed to the main product and how much to the co-products is one of the most controversial topics in the LCA debate as it can have an enormous influence on the results (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2012). The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) provides the following guidance to deal with multi-functional systems (ISO 2006): i) avoid allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes into sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products; ii) when allocation cannot be avoided, identify physical relationships between the multiple products and functions to partition the environmental impacts; and iii) when a physical relationship cannot be established, inputs and outputs should be allocated by identifying other relationship between products and co-products (e.g. energy ratio and economic value).

Defining the system boundaries is also a methodological challenge when conducting LCA studies that integrate agriculture and bioenergy systems given that these systems respond to different societal needs and priorities. Dairy is a well-established industry and one of the most important economic activities in WI. The main purpose of a dairy farm is the production of milk, thus, it is highly unlikely that crop production for cow feed would be replaced by energy crops

for bio-fuel production. In this context, it is important to evaluate alternative ways in which the production of biofuel feedstocks can co-exist or provide synergies for dairy feed production. The objective of this LCA is to estimate how different dairy diets with various concentrations of DDGS and SBM, and manure management practices with and without anaerobic digestion (AD) affect direct land use, GHG emissions and net energy intensity of WI dairy production systems. To achieve this goal, a partial LCA of milk production is conducted by applying system expansion and boundary expansion approaches to account for the interactions that exist between the dairy and bioenergy sectors in the state.

The Wisconsin's Strategy for Reducing Global Warming includes proposals that increase the capture and use of animal CH4 for electricity or heat; reduce current CH4 emissions; reduce the application of nitrogen and the overall use of chemical fertilizers; and mandate the adoption of nutrient management practices (WI Global Warming Task Force 2008). Despite these efforts, little is still known about the life cycle environmental impacts and benefits of implementing these strategies in a dairy farm.

Dairy diets in WI typically combine corn silage, alfalfa silage, corn grain, and cotton seed with soybean meal (SBM), which is the co-product from the extraction of soy oil that is typically used for food or biodiesel production. More recently, dry distillers grains with solubles (DDGS), a co- product of the corn ethanol industry, has also been added to the dairy diet of confined herds due to the immediate availability of this resource. The corn ethanol industry in WI has an installed capacity to produce 1.9 billion liters of ethanol and more than 1 billion kg of DDGS per year. Hermans et al. (2006) estimated that about 15% of the gasoline used in WI could be replaced by ethanol based on maximum allowable DDGS consumption by the state's 1.2 million dairy cows. Similarly, biodiesel from soy oil could replace approximately 6% of the locally consumed

petroleum diesel based on maximum allowable SBM consumption by the state's dairy cows. The inclusion of locally produced SBM and DDGS in the dairy diet could further reduce GHG emissions and fossil energy consumption as they do not need to be transported from out-of-state crushing facilities.

In the case of a multi-functional system where corn ethanol and DDGS are produced, the environmental burdens should be assigned to both of these products, given that DDGS provide benefits to the system when used as animal feed. Bremer et al. (2010) indicate that assigning GHG credits to DDGS can represent 19 to 38% of total life cycle GHG emissions of corn ethanol production. Defining how much of the inputs and environmental burdens should be attributed to the main product and how much to the co-products is one of the most controversial topics in the LCA debate as it can have an enormous influence on the results (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2012). The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) provides the following guidance to deal with multi-functional systems (ISO 2006): i) avoid allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes into sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products; ii) when allocation cannot be avoided, identify physical relationships between the multiple products and functions to partition the environmental impacts; and iii) when a physical relationship cannot be established, inputs and outputs should be allocated by identifying other relationship between products and co-products (e.g. energy ratio and economic value).

Defining the system boundaries is also a methodological challenge when conducting LCA studies that integrate agriculture and bioenergy systems given that these systems respond to different societal needs and priorities. Dairy is a well-established industry and one of the most important economic activities in WI. The main purpose of a dairy farm is the production of milk, thus, it is highly unlikely that crop production for cow feed would be replaced by energy crops

for bio-fuel production. In this context, it is important to evaluate alternative ways in which the production of biofuel feedstocks can co-exist or provide synergies for dairy feed production. The objective of this LCA is to estimate how different dairy diets with various concentrations of DDGS and SBM, and manure management practices with and without anaerobic digestion (AD) affect direct land use, GHG emissions and net energy intensity of WI dairy production systems. To achieve this goal, a partial LCA of milk production is conducted by applying system expansion and boundary expansion approaches to account for the interactions that exist between the dairy and bioenergy sectors in the state.

Moreover, agricultural activities account for 85% and 50% of global nitrous oxide (N20) and methane (CH4) emissions, which represents 70% of non-carbon dioxide emissions of the agricultural sector (IPCC 2007). Most N2O emissions originate from soils due to the application of fertilizers and animal manures, and the majority of CH4 emissions come from livestock enteric fermentation, making of animal agriculture an important target of emission reduction strategies.

Land use, energy intensity, and GHG emissions can be assessed by environmental indicators derived from input-output accounting methods that focus only on on-farm impacts, but more comprehensive estimates derive from life cycle assessment (LCA) indicators (Thomassen and de Boer 2008). LCA has arisen as the preferred methodology to estimate the environmental impacts of agricultural related systems (Reap et al. 2008). A recent LCA study by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations estimates that the dairy sector alone contributes

4% (+-26%) of total global anthropogenic GHG emissions (FAO 2010). An analysis of 60 studies by the International Dairy Federation concludes that about 80% of the GHG emissions from dairy production originate from on-farm sources; mainly soils, crop production, cows' enteric fermentation, and manure (IDF 2009). This same study estimates that on-farm processes represent 40% of the total energy consumption of dairy products manufacturing from a lifecycle perspective.

Numerous LCAs report varying results of GHG emissions from milk production depending on milk yield per cow, dairy diets, and manure management (e.g., Cederberg and Stadig 2003; Rotz et al. 2010; Mc Geough et al. 2012; Thoma et al. 2013). According to these studies, CH4 from gastro-enteric fermentation is the largest single contributor to GHG emissions, followed by CH4 from manure management and N2O from soils where dairy feeds are grown. Consequently, different studies have evaluated adjustments in dairy diet compositions to reduce enteric CH4 emissions from cows (Johnson and Johnson 1995; Johnson et al. 2007; Martin et al. 2008). The composition of the dairy diet plays an important role in GHG emissions, not only by affecting enteric emissions from COWS (direct effect) and changing the quantities or types of crops grown (indirect effect), but often times by interacting with bioenergy production (synergistic effect).

Effectiveness of GHG mitigation methods during manure management has also been evaluated (Boadi et al. 2004; Del Prado et al. 2010). For example, GHG emissions from dairy can be reduced when manure is land applied for crops or pasture fertilization given that manure displaces synthetic fertilizers that require high fossil energy inputs. Dairy manure can also be processed in anaerobic digesters to generate biogas. This renewable gas is 50% to 65% CH4 and can be collected and used for direct burn applications, conversion to electricity, or compressed fuel (Appels et al. 2011). Following digestion, the manure or digestate can still be land-applied

as fertilizer and emits less CH4 than non-digested manure during storage as it contains less carbon (C) and volatile solids (VS) (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014). Moreover, the combustion of CH4 (otherwise emitted from stored liquid manure) reduces GHG emissions by converting CH4 into carbon dioxide (CO2) and by displacing fossil fuels used to produce heat or electricity.

To achieve this goal, a partial LCA of milk production is conducted by applying system expansion and boundary expansion approaches to account for the interactions that exist between the dairy and bioenergy sectors in the state.
11: <header>2.3.1 Goal and Scope </header>

The goal of this partial LCA is to evaluate how integrating bioenergy and dairy systems affect energy intensity, GHG emissions, and land use. The first opportunity for this integration happens at the dairy herd feed level, where corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel industries can be paired with the dairy industry to produce DDGS and SBM according to animal feed requirements. This approach expands the boundaries of current dairy systems in WI into more comprehensive dairy and bioenergy multifunctional systems. The second opportunity to integrate dairy with bioenergy happens at the manure management level, where dairy manure can be used to generate biogas through AD for on-farm use or sale.

Two systems are analyzed: 1) a traditional dairy system that produces milk and meat, and 2) a dairy system that integrates bioenergy into its boundaries by producing milk, meat, corn ethanol, soybean biodiesel, and biogas. In the second integrated dairy and bioenergy system, the corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel industries can be seen as pre-processing operations that produce

DDGS and SBM to feed dairy cows, with bio-fuels as co-products. Five lactating cow diets with varying amounts of DDGS and SBM, and two manure management scenarios with and without AD of dairy manure are modeled.

The decision tree for the model begins with the production of milk from a dairy farm under typical conditions of WI. The amounts of crops grown to supply the feed ration and manure excretion are calculated based on the characteristics of the herd, components of the diet, and manure management practices. Dietary requirements are used to calculate the inputs and outputs for crop production, prioritizing the use of dairy manure over purchased fertilizers. The functional unit is one kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) to 3.5% fat and 3.0% protein and is calculated with Equation 1 (NRC 2001). The time frame of the analysis is one year and the system boundaries are defined as milk production from cradle-to-farm gate. The processes included in these boundaries are: crops production for both dairy feed and bio-fuels (corn ethanol and soy biodiesel); dairy herd and diets composition; manure handling and biogas generation; corn-ethanol and soybean biodiesel production; and manufacturing of nutrients, chemicals, machinery, and energy inputs (Figure 1).
From NRC (2001), where:
FPCM = fat and protein corrected milk (kg)
MP = milk production (kg)
MF = milk fat (%)
MP = milk protein (%)

15: <header>2.3.2 Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) </header>

The impact categories depletion of abiotic resources and climate change are assessed. The indicators used to measure depletion of abiotic resources are direct land use (measured in m²) and net energy intensity (measured in MJ). Net Energy Intensity (NEI) is defined as the net energy from activities related to milk and bioenergy production and is calculated as the difference between the energy inputs (required energy) and energy outputs (supplied energy). The avoided energy from bioenergy production is discounted when system expansion is applied to partition the burdens between products and co-products (Equation 2). Avoided energy is determined based on how much fossil fuels (gasoline, petro-diesel, and natural gas) is displaced by renewable fuels (ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas) based on its energy content. Negative NEI indicates that the system produces more energy than it consumes, whereas positive NEI indicates an energy loosing system.
NEI = net energy intensity (MJ/kg FPCM)

E1 = energy inputs to the system, including on-farm and off-farm inputs for crops and bio- fuels production (MJ) E0 = energy generated in form of bio-fuel or biogas (MJ) FPCM = fat and protein corrected milk (kg)

Climate change is measured through GHG emissions, which are characterized in kg of CO2 equivalents (CO2-eq) based on the CML2001 - Dec. 07 method over 100 years (CML 2001). It is assumed that biotic CO2 (emitted from biomass sources) is re-captured by plants within the time frame of the LCA; therefore, biotic CO2 emissions are not included in the analysis. The characterization factor for biotic CH4 is adjusted to 22 instead of the 25 factor used in the CML2001 method. This adjustment is done because the carbon released in the form of biotic CH4 through bacterial activity, originally comes from carbon uptake by plants through photosynthesis in the form of CO2. Given that the molecular masses of CO2 and CH4 are different, the CH4 emission factor has to be reduced by 2.75 to account for the C uptake as CO2 through plant photosynthesis (for simplification purposes this factor is rounded to 22) (Equation 3).
Characterization factor for biotic CH4 25 - COz mass = 22.25 (3)

17: <header>2.3.3 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

Life cycle inventory data are obtained from the literature, mathematical equations, and the US Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI 2012) and Ecoinvent 2.0 U.S. (Ecoinvent 2010) databases accessed through GaBi 4 Professional® software (PE International 2010). Database inventory include nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) fertilizers, diesel, gasoline, natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), and cotton production. The electricity matrix represents the mix of fuels that are part of the electric grid of WI and is presented in Table S1 in the Supplementary Information (SI).

Herd Structure and Nutrition. The modeled dairy farm is located in WI and produces 10,000 kg of milk per day at 3.0% protein, 3.5% fat, and 4.85% lactose concentrations, which are representative of WI (AgSource 2009). To achieve this milk production, the farm houses 286 lactating cows (650 kg body weight), 173 growing heifers (347 kg body weight), 41 mature heifers and dry COWS (285 kg body weight), and 9 calves up to one month old (52 kg body weight) in free-stall barns (Powell et al. 2008). It is assumed that the dry period is 62 days, the calving interval is 14 months, and the annual adult replacement rate including mortality and culling is 35.8%, which are typical values for WI dairies (AgSource 2009). The annual mortality rate is 1.8% for weaned heifers and 7.8% for un-weaned heifers (USDA 2007), and the new born female to male ratio is 0.47 (Del Rio et al. 2007). It is assumed that animals achieve their mature weight at 21 months old and that all heifers needed to maintain the herd size are raised on-farm as replacements. For nutrition purposes, non-lactating animals are divided into three categories: heifers from 1 to 11 months old; heifers from 12 to 21 months old; and heifers older than 21 months old and dry cows. N, P, and K flows from feed to milk and manure are calculated based on dry matter intake (DMI) and nutrient intake values according to NRC (2001) (Table 1). Energy requirements for housing and milking are 0.062 MJ/kg milk and include space heating, ventilation, lighting, milking, milk cooling, and water heating (ECW, 2009).

Diet Scenarios. The reference diet is representative of management practices in WI (Armentano, L. University of Wisconsin-Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication) and is formulated separately for young stock, non-lactating cows, and lactating cows (Table 2). This is done to meet maintenance, growth, pregnancy, and milk production requirements (NRC 2001). Alterations of the reference diet are formulated to represent different forage types as well as

feeding of concentrates derived from biofuels production possesses. It is assumed that calves younger than 1 month do not receive solid feed and are fed milk only at a 4 kg milk/day rate. Heifers are fed harvested alfalfa and corn silage, with the difference that the diet of heifers between 1 and 11 months is supplemented with concentrate. Six diet scenarios are formulated for lactating COWS based on rumen undegradable protein and rumen degradable protein requirements. These scenarios include a reference diet scenario representative of the system that does not integrate dairy and bioenergy production, and five diet scenarios that represent the integrated dairy and bioenergy system: one diet with equal amounts of corn silage and alfalfa silage, and equal amounts of DDGS and SBM (CADS); two diets with more corn silage than alfalfa silage: one maximizing DDGS (CSDG) and one maximizing SBM (CSSB); and two diets with more alfalfa silage than corn silage: one maximizing DDGS (ASDG) and one diet maximizing SBM (ASSB). All lactating cow diets target a crude protein (CP) concentration of 17% based on NRC (2001) recommendations.

Crop Production. Chemicals, energy, fuels, machinery, and nutrients required to produce crops for dairy feed, corn ethanol, and soybean biodiesel are listed in Table 3 and the related primary energy required for their manufacturing is presented in Table S2. Fossil fuels (gasoline, diesel, LPG, natural gas) and renewable fuels (biogas, ethanol and biodiesel) consumption are calculated based on energy density values (Table S3). N, P, and K are applied according to crop requirements, prioritizing the use of dairy manure over purchased fertilizers, which are only included into the system when nutrient targets are not met with manure. It is assumed that all of the P and K from manure applied to soils are available to plants, but just 50% of the applied N (Beegle, 2008). The land area required to produce dairy feed crops is calculated from yield

averages for alfalfa silage (7,865 kg dry matter/ha), corn silage (13,097 kg dry matter/ha), corn grain (7,591 kg dry matter/ha), soybean (2,386 kg dry matter/ha) and cotton seed (1,031 kg dry matter/ha) (USDA-NASS 2009 and 2010). The total area required to produce corn grain and soybeans is considered in integrated bioenergy and dairy scenarios given that the boundaries of the system are expanded to include ethanol and biodiesel production.

Agricultural lime (CaCO3) requirements for crops are calculated as the sum of lime required to: supply calcium removal in harvested material, neutralize the potential acidification rate of the crop (50 g CaCO3/ kmol H+, (Avila et al. 2005), and neutralize the potential acidity from nitrification and mineralization of N fertilizers (2.45 kg of CaCO3/kg N fertilizer (Sinistore, 2008) (Table S4); minus lime displaced due to the potential alkalinity of the manure (1.12 kg of CaCO3 kg/N manure (Sinistore 2008).

Manure Management and Biogas Generation. Manure excretion and total solids (TS) are calculated for each cow type (Table 4). It is assumed that VS constitute 85% of excreted TS for all cow types (ASABE 2005). Two manure management scenarios are considered in the analysis: directly land-applied manure (no processing) and manure AD. In the first management scenario, manure is collected daily from the free-stall barns with an alley scraper that consumes 0.45 kWh/ton collected manure (calculated from survey data from 19 WI dairy farms (S. Sanford, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication). It is assumed that chopped straw at 10% moisture is used as the bedding material, which is also collected from the barns. The area exposed to manure in the barns is 3.5 m² per animal older than 21 months and 2.35 m² per animal younger than 21 months (Rotz and Jouke 2006). After collection, manure is transported to a 4.5 m deep lined uncovered pond, process that consumes 0.22 kWh/ton

transported manure. It is assumed that manure is stored for periods of 6 months at 8% TS content, which promotes the formation of a natural crust on the surface (Rotz et al. 2009). Agitation is done before emptying the manure storage with an electrical pump at 0.22 kWh/ton manure. Manure is then land applied by surface broadcast two times a year (in spring and fall) in a 179 kW (240 HP) - 35,900 m³ (9,500 gallon) truck in cycles of 0.2 hours (including travel and application) at rates of 0.43 1 diesel/ton of applied manure. This rate is calculated based on field studies conducted in the Great Lakes Region (Hadrich et al. 2009) and the ASABE (2006) diesel consumption equation.

The second manure management scenario considers the addition of a digester, where the collected manure (at 85% VS of TS) and chopped straw (at 95.9% VS of TS,) are processed into biogas and digestate (Moller et al. 2004). Biogas is available to the farm for further production of electricity, heat, or compressed gas; and digestate is stored and land applied by surface broadcast in spring and fall. Biogas is produced at yields of 0.268 n3/kg VS (Berglund and Börjesson 2006) with a 65% CH4 and 35% CO2 composition (Appels et al. 2011). The energy inputs for the digester operation are calculated based on a 28% energy input/output ratio in relation to the energy content of the produced biogas (Berglund and Börjesson 2006).

Bio-fuels Production. Inputs and outputs related to the bio-refining process of corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel are included in the analysis of diet scenarios that integrate dairy and bioenergy. Bio-fuels production volumes are scaled to the amounts of DDGS and SBM used to feed the dairy herd. It is assumed that the production of soybean biodiesel yields 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybeans (Pimentel and Patzek 2005) and 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybeans (Wang et al.,
2010), and the production of corn ethanol yields 0.34 kg ethanol/kg corn (Patzek 2004) and 0.26 kg DDGS/kg corn (Wang et al. 2010).

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions. Greenhouse gases are emitted from biomass (biotic) and fossil sources. Biotic emissions are a result of plant decomposition, animal digestion and respiration, and microbial activity in manure (it us assumed that there are no changes in the carbon content of soils). Fossil emissions come from the combustion of fossil energy sources. GHG emissions from enteric fermentation, crop production, manure handling and biogas generation, and bio-fuels production are presented in Table 5. Enteric CH4 emissions are estimated with the method developed by Moe and Tyrrell (1979), which accounts for the chemical composition change (nonstructural carbohydrate, hemicellulose, and cellulose fractions) in each of the analyzed diet scenarios. GHGs from crops production and manure handling are calculated through emission factors and equations from the literature. For the manure management scenario with AD, it is assumed that all CH4 from biogas is converted to CO2 through combustion. The production of biogas reduces the total amount of VS in manure, decreasing CH4 emissions from manure storage after digestion.
