Biotic emissions are a result of plant decomposition, animal digestion and respiration, and microbial activity in manure (it us assumed that there are no changes in the carbon content of soils). Fossil emissions come from the combustion of fossil energy sources. GHG emissions from enteric fermentation, crop production, manure handling and biogas generation, and bio-fuels production are presented in Table 5. Enteric CH4 emissions are estimated with the method developed by Moe and Tyrrell (1979), which accounts for the chemical composition change (nonstructural carbohydrate, hemicellulose, and cellulose fractions) in each of the analyzed diet scenarios. GHGs from crops production and manure handling are calculated through emission factors and equations from the literature. For the manure management scenario with AD, it is assumed that all CH4 from biogas is converted to CO2 through combustion. The production of biogas reduces the total amount of VS in manure, decreasing CH4 emissions from manure storage after digestion.
19: <header>2.3.3 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

Life cycle inventory data are obtained from the literature, mathematical equations, and the US Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI 2012) and Ecoinvent 2.0 U.S. (Ecoinvent 2010) databases accessed through GaBi 4 Professional® software (PE International 2010). Database inventory include nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) fertilizers, diesel, gasoline, natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), and cotton production. The electricity matrix represents the mix of fuels that are part of the electric grid of WI and is presented in Table S1 in the Supplementary Information (SI).

Herd Structure and Nutrition. The modeled dairy farm is located in WI and produces 10,000 kg of milk per day at 3.0% protein, 3.5% fat, and 4.85% lactose concentrations, which are representative of WI (AgSource 2009). To achieve this milk production, the farm houses 286 lactating cows (650 kg body weight), 173 growing heifers (347 kg body weight), 41 mature heifers and dry COWS (285 kg body weight), and 9 calves up to one month old (52 kg body weight) in free-stall barns (Powell et al. 2008). It is assumed that the dry period is 62 days, the calving interval is 14 months, and the annual adult replacement rate including mortality and culling is 35.8%, which are typical values for WI dairies (AgSource 2009). The annual mortality rate is 1.8% for weaned heifers and 7.8% for un-weaned heifers (USDA 2007), and the new born female to male ratio is 0.47 (Del Rio et al. 2007). It is assumed that animals achieve their mature weight at 21 months old and that all heifers needed to maintain the herd size are raised on-farm as replacements. For nutrition purposes, non-lactating animals are divided into three categories: heifers from 1 to 11 months old; heifers from 12 to 21 months old; and heifers older than 21 months old and dry cows. N, P, and K flows from feed to milk and manure are calculated based on dry matter intake (DMI) and nutrient intake values according to NRC (2001) (Table 1). Energy requirements for housing and milking are 0.062 MJ/kg milk and include space heating, ventilation, lighting, milking, milk cooling, and water heating (ECW, 2009).

Diet Scenarios. The reference diet is representative of management practices in WI (Armentano, L. University of Wisconsin-Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication) and is formulated separately for young stock, non-lactating cows, and lactating cows (Table 2). This is done to meet maintenance, growth, pregnancy, and milk production requirements (NRC 2001). Alterations of the reference diet are formulated to represent different forage types as well as

feeding of concentrates derived from biofuels production possesses. It is assumed that calves younger than 1 month do not receive solid feed and are fed milk only at a 4 kg milk/day rate. Heifers are fed harvested alfalfa and corn silage, with the difference that the diet of heifers between 1 and 11 months is supplemented with concentrate. Six diet scenarios are formulated for lactating COWS based on rumen undegradable protein and rumen degradable protein requirements. These scenarios include a reference diet scenario representative of the system that does not integrate dairy and bioenergy production, and five diet scenarios that represent the integrated dairy and bioenergy system: one diet with equal amounts of corn silage and alfalfa silage, and equal amounts of DDGS and SBM (CADS); two diets with more corn silage than alfalfa silage: one maximizing DDGS (CSDG) and one maximizing SBM (CSSB); and two diets with more alfalfa silage than corn silage: one maximizing DDGS (ASDG) and one diet maximizing SBM (ASSB). All lactating cow diets target a crude protein (CP) concentration of 17% based on NRC (2001) recommendations.

Crop Production. Chemicals, energy, fuels, machinery, and nutrients required to produce crops for dairy feed, corn ethanol, and soybean biodiesel are listed in Table 3 and the related primary energy required for their manufacturing is presented in Table S2. Fossil fuels (gasoline, diesel, LPG, natural gas) and renewable fuels (biogas, ethanol and biodiesel) consumption are calculated based on energy density values (Table S3). N, P, and K are applied according to crop requirements, prioritizing the use of dairy manure over purchased fertilizers, which are only included into the system when nutrient targets are not met with manure. It is assumed that all of the P and K from manure applied to soils are available to plants, but just 50% of the applied N (Beegle, 2008). The land area required to produce dairy feed crops is calculated from yield

averages for alfalfa silage (7,865 kg dry matter/ha), corn silage (13,097 kg dry matter/ha), corn grain (7,591 kg dry matter/ha), soybean (2,386 kg dry matter/ha) and cotton seed (1,031 kg dry matter/ha) (USDA-NASS 2009 and 2010). The total area required to produce corn grain and soybeans is considered in integrated bioenergy and dairy scenarios given that the boundaries of the system are expanded to include ethanol and biodiesel production.

Agricultural lime (CaCO3) requirements for crops are calculated as the sum of lime required to: supply calcium removal in harvested material, neutralize the potential acidification rate of the crop (50 g CaCO3/ kmol H+, (Avila et al. 2005), and neutralize the potential acidity from nitrification and mineralization of N fertilizers (2.45 kg of CaCO3/kg N fertilizer (Sinistore, 2008) (Table S4); minus lime displaced due to the potential alkalinity of the manure (1.12 kg of CaCO3 kg/N manure (Sinistore 2008).

Manure Management and Biogas Generation. Manure excretion and total solids (TS) are calculated for each cow type (Table 4). It is assumed that VS constitute 85% of excreted TS for all cow types (ASABE 2005). Two manure management scenarios are considered in the analysis: directly land-applied manure (no processing) and manure AD. In the first management scenario, manure is collected daily from the free-stall barns with an alley scraper that consumes 0.45 kWh/ton collected manure (calculated from survey data from 19 WI dairy farms (S. Sanford, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication). It is assumed that chopped straw at 10% moisture is used as the bedding material, which is also collected from the barns. The area exposed to manure in the barns is 3.5 m² per animal older than 21 months and 2.35 m² per animal younger than 21 months (Rotz and Jouke 2006). After collection, manure is transported to a 4.5 m deep lined uncovered pond, process that consumes 0.22 kWh/ton

transported manure. It is assumed that manure is stored for periods of 6 months at 8% TS content, which promotes the formation of a natural crust on the surface (Rotz et al. 2009). Agitation is done before emptying the manure storage with an electrical pump at 0.22 kWh/ton manure. Manure is then land applied by surface broadcast two times a year (in spring and fall) in a 179 kW (240 HP) - 35,900 m³ (9,500 gallon) truck in cycles of 0.2 hours (including travel and application) at rates of 0.43 1 diesel/ton of applied manure. This rate is calculated based on field studies conducted in the Great Lakes Region (Hadrich et al. 2009) and the ASABE (2006) diesel consumption equation.

The second manure management scenario considers the addition of a digester, where the collected manure (at 85% VS of TS) and chopped straw (at 95.9% VS of TS,) are processed into biogas and digestate (Moller et al. 2004). Biogas is available to the farm for further production of electricity, heat, or compressed gas; and digestate is stored and land applied by surface broadcast in spring and fall. Biogas is produced at yields of 0.268 n3/kg VS (Berglund and Börjesson 2006) with a 65% CH4 and 35% CO2 composition (Appels et al. 2011). The energy inputs for the digester operation are calculated based on a 28% energy input/output ratio in relation to the energy content of the produced biogas (Berglund and Börjesson 2006).

Bio-fuels Production. Inputs and outputs related to the bio-refining process of corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel are included in the analysis of diet scenarios that integrate dairy and bioenergy. Bio-fuels production volumes are scaled to the amounts of DDGS and SBM used to feed the dairy herd. It is assumed that the production of soybean biodiesel yields 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybeans (Pimentel and Patzek 2005) and 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybeans (Wang et al.,
2010), and the production of corn ethanol yields 0.34 kg ethanol/kg corn (Patzek 2004) and 0.26 kg DDGS/kg corn (Wang et al. 2010).

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions. Greenhouse gases are emitted from biomass (biotic) and fossil sources. Biotic emissions are a result of plant decomposition, animal digestion and respiration, and microbial activity in manure (it us assumed that there are no changes in the carbon content of soils). Fossil emissions come from the combustion of fossil energy sources. GHG emissions from enteric fermentation, crop production, manure handling and biogas generation, and bio-fuels production are presented in Table 5. Enteric CH4 emissions are estimated with the method developed by Moe and Tyrrell (1979), which accounts for the chemical composition change (nonstructural carbohydrate, hemicellulose, and cellulose fractions) in each of the analyzed diet scenarios. GHGs from crops production and manure handling are calculated through emission factors and equations from the literature. For the manure management scenario with AD, it is assumed that all CH4 from biogas is converted to CO2 through combustion. The production of biogas reduces the total amount of VS in manure, decreasing CH4 emissions from manure storage after digestion.

University of Wisconsin-Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication) and is formulated separately for young stock, non-lactating cows, and lactating cows (Table 2). This is done to meet maintenance, growth, pregnancy, and milk production requirements (NRC 2001). Alterations of the reference diet are formulated to represent different forage types as well as

feeding of concentrates derived from biofuels production possesses. It is assumed that calves younger than 1 month do not receive solid feed and are fed milk only at a 4 kg milk/day rate. Heifers are fed harvested alfalfa and corn silage, with the difference that the diet of heifers between 1 and 11 months is supplemented with concentrate. Six diet scenarios are formulated for lactating COWS based on rumen undegradable protein and rumen degradable protein requirements. These scenarios include a reference diet scenario representative of the system that does not integrate dairy and bioenergy production, and five diet scenarios that represent the integrated dairy and bioenergy system: one diet with equal amounts of corn silage and alfalfa silage, and equal amounts of DDGS and SBM (CADS); two diets with more corn silage than alfalfa silage: one maximizing DDGS (CSDG) and one maximizing SBM (CSSB); and two diets with more alfalfa silage than corn silage: one maximizing DDGS (ASDG) and one diet maximizing SBM (ASSB). All lactating cow diets target a crude protein (CP) concentration of 17% based on NRC (2001) recommendations.

Crop Production. Chemicals, energy, fuels, machinery, and nutrients required to produce crops for dairy feed, corn ethanol, and soybean biodiesel are listed in Table 3 and the related primary energy required for their manufacturing is presented in Table S2. Fossil fuels (gasoline, diesel, LPG, natural gas) and renewable fuels (biogas, ethanol and biodiesel) consumption are calculated based on energy density values (Table S3). N, P, and K are applied according to crop requirements, prioritizing the use of dairy manure over purchased fertilizers, which are only included into the system when nutrient targets are not met with manure. It is assumed that all of the P and K from manure applied to soils are available to plants, but just 50% of the applied N (Beegle, 2008). The land area required to produce dairy feed crops is calculated from yield

averages for alfalfa silage (7,865 kg dry matter/ha), corn silage (13,097 kg dry matter/ha), corn grain (7,591 kg dry matter/ha), soybean (2,386 kg dry matter/ha) and cotton seed (1,031 kg dry matter/ha) (USDA-NASS 2009 and 2010). The total area required to produce corn grain and soybeans is considered in integrated bioenergy and dairy scenarios given that the boundaries of the system are expanded to include ethanol and biodiesel production.

Agricultural lime (CaCO3) requirements for crops are calculated as the sum of lime required to: supply calcium removal in harvested material, neutralize the potential acidification rate of the crop (50 g CaCO3/ kmol H+, (Avila et al. 2005), and neutralize the potential acidity from nitrification and mineralization of N fertilizers (2.45 kg of CaCO3/kg N fertilizer (Sinistore, 2008) (Table S4); minus lime displaced due to the potential alkalinity of the manure (1.12 kg of CaCO3 kg/N manure (Sinistore 2008).

Manure Management and Biogas Generation. Manure excretion and total solids (TS) are calculated for each cow type (Table 4). It is assumed that VS constitute 85% of excreted TS for all cow types (ASABE 2005). Two manure management scenarios are considered in the analysis: directly land-applied manure (no processing) and manure AD. In the first management scenario, manure is collected daily from the free-stall barns with an alley scraper that consumes 0.45 kWh/ton collected manure (calculated from survey data from 19 WI dairy farms (S. Sanford, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication). It is assumed that chopped straw at 10% moisture is used as the bedding material, which is also collected from the barns. The area exposed to manure in the barns is 3.5 m² per animal older than 21 months and 2.35 m² per animal younger than 21 months (Rotz and Jouke 2006). After collection, manure is transported to a 4.5 m deep lined uncovered pond, process that consumes 0.22 kWh/ton

transported manure. It is assumed that manure is stored for periods of 6 months at 8% TS content, which promotes the formation of a natural crust on the surface (Rotz et al. 2009). Agitation is done before emptying the manure storage with an electrical pump at 0.22 kWh/ton manure. Manure is then land applied by surface broadcast two times a year (in spring and fall) in a 179 kW (240 HP) - 35,900 m³ (9,500 gallon) truck in cycles of 0.2 hours (including travel and application) at rates of 0.43 1 diesel/ton of applied manure. This rate is calculated based on field studies conducted in the Great Lakes Region (Hadrich et al. 2009) and the ASABE (2006) diesel consumption equation.

The second manure management scenario considers the addition of a digester, where the collected manure (at 85% VS of TS) and chopped straw (at 95.9% VS of TS,) are processed into biogas and digestate (Moller et al. 2004). Biogas is available to the farm for further production of electricity, heat, or compressed gas; and digestate is stored and land applied by surface broadcast in spring and fall. Biogas is produced at yields of 0.268 n3/kg VS (Berglund and Börjesson 2006) with a 65% CH4 and 35% CO2 composition (Appels et al. 2011). The energy inputs for the digester operation are calculated based on a 28% energy input/output ratio in relation to the energy content of the produced biogas (Berglund and Börjesson 2006).

Bio-fuels Production. Inputs and outputs related to the bio-refining process of corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel are included in the analysis of diet scenarios that integrate dairy and bioenergy. Bio-fuels production volumes are scaled to the amounts of DDGS and SBM used to feed the dairy herd. It is assumed that the production of soybean biodiesel yields 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybeans (Pimentel and Patzek 2005) and 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybeans (Wang et al.,
2010), and the production of corn ethanol yields 0.34 kg ethanol/kg corn (Patzek 2004) and 0.26 kg DDGS/kg corn (Wang et al. 2010).

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions. Greenhouse gases are emitted from biomass (biotic) and fossil sources. Biotic emissions are a result of plant decomposition, animal digestion and respiration, and microbial activity in manure (it us assumed that there are no changes in the carbon content of soils). Fossil emissions come from the combustion of fossil energy sources. GHG emissions from enteric fermentation, crop production, manure handling and biogas generation, and bio-fuels production are presented in Table 5. Enteric CH4 emissions are estimated with the method developed by Moe and Tyrrell (1979), which accounts for the chemical composition change (nonstructural carbohydrate, hemicellulose, and cellulose fractions) in each of the analyzed diet scenarios. GHGs from crops production and manure handling are calculated through emission factors and equations from the literature. For the manure management scenario with AD, it is assumed that all CH4 from biogas is converted to CO2 through combustion. The production of biogas reduces the total amount of VS in manure, decreasing CH4 emissions from manure storage after digestion.
23: <header>2.3.3 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

Life cycle inventory data are obtained from the literature, mathematical equations, and the US Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI 2012) and Ecoinvent 2.0 U.S. (Ecoinvent 2010) databases accessed through GaBi 4 Professional® software (PE International 2010). Database inventory include nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) fertilizers, diesel, gasoline, natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), and cotton production. The electricity matrix represents the mix of fuels that are part of the electric grid of WI and is presented in Table S1 in the Supplementary Information (SI).

Herd Structure and Nutrition. The modeled dairy farm is located in WI and produces 10,000 kg of milk per day at 3.0% protein, 3.5% fat, and 4.85% lactose concentrations, which are representative of WI (AgSource 2009). To achieve this milk production, the farm houses 286 lactating cows (650 kg body weight), 173 growing heifers (347 kg body weight), 41 mature heifers and dry COWS (285 kg body weight), and 9 calves up to one month old (52 kg body weight) in free-stall barns (Powell et al. 2008). It is assumed that the dry period is 62 days, the calving interval is 14 months, and the annual adult replacement rate including mortality and culling is 35.8%, which are typical values for WI dairies (AgSource 2009). The annual mortality rate is 1.8% for weaned heifers and 7.8% for un-weaned heifers (USDA 2007), and the new born female to male ratio is 0.47 (Del Rio et al. 2007). It is assumed that animals achieve their mature weight at 21 months old and that all heifers needed to maintain the herd size are raised on-farm as replacements. For nutrition purposes, non-lactating animals are divided into three categories: heifers from 1 to 11 months old; heifers from 12 to 21 months old; and heifers older than 21 months old and dry cows. N, P, and K flows from feed to milk and manure are calculated based on dry matter intake (DMI) and nutrient intake values according to NRC (2001) (Table 1). Energy requirements for housing and milking are 0.062 MJ/kg milk and include space heating, ventilation, lighting, milking, milk cooling, and water heating (ECW, 2009).

It is assumed that VS constitute 85% of excreted TS for all cow types (ASABE 2005). Two manure management scenarios are considered in the analysis: directly land-applied manure (no processing) and manure AD. In the first management scenario, manure is collected daily from the free-stall barns with an alley scraper that consumes 0.45 kWh/ton collected manure (calculated from survey data from 19 WI dairy farms (S. Sanford, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication). It is assumed that chopped straw at 10% moisture is used as the bedding material, which is also collected from the barns. The area exposed to manure in the barns is 3.5 m² per animal older than 21 months and 2.35 m² per animal younger than 21 months (Rotz and Jouke 2006). After collection, manure is transported to a 4.5 m deep lined uncovered pond, process that consumes 0.22 kWh/ton

transported manure. It is assumed that manure is stored for periods of 6 months at 8% TS content, which promotes the formation of a natural crust on the surface (Rotz et al. 2009). Agitation is done before emptying the manure storage with an electrical pump at 0.22 kWh/ton manure. Manure is then land applied by surface broadcast two times a year (in spring and fall) in a 179 kW (240 HP) - 35,900 m³ (9,500 gallon) truck in cycles of 0.2 hours (including travel and application) at rates of 0.43 1 diesel/ton of applied manure. This rate is calculated based on field studies conducted in the Great Lakes Region (Hadrich et al. 2009) and the ASABE (2006) diesel consumption equation.

The second manure management scenario considers the addition of a digester, where the collected manure (at 85% VS of TS) and chopped straw (at 95.9% VS of TS,) are processed into biogas and digestate (Moller et al. 2004). Biogas is available to the farm for further production of electricity, heat, or compressed gas; and digestate is stored and land applied by surface broadcast in spring and fall. Biogas is produced at yields of 0.268 n3/kg VS (Berglund and Börjesson 2006) with a 65% CH4 and 35% CO2 composition (Appels et al. 2011). The energy inputs for the digester operation are calculated based on a 28% energy input/output ratio in relation to the energy content of the produced biogas (Berglund and Börjesson 2006).

Bio-fuels Production. Inputs and outputs related to the bio-refining process of corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel are included in the analysis of diet scenarios that integrate dairy and bioenergy. Bio-fuels production volumes are scaled to the amounts of DDGS and SBM used to feed the dairy herd. It is assumed that the production of soybean biodiesel yields 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybeans (Pimentel and Patzek 2005) and 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybeans (Wang et al.,
2010), and the production of corn ethanol yields 0.34 kg ethanol/kg corn (Patzek 2004) and 0.26 kg DDGS/kg corn (Wang et al. 2010).

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions. Greenhouse gases are emitted from biomass (biotic) and fossil sources. Biotic emissions are a result of plant decomposition, animal digestion and respiration, and microbial activity in manure (it us assumed that there are no changes in the carbon content of soils). Fossil emissions come from the combustion of fossil energy sources. GHG emissions from enteric fermentation, crop production, manure handling and biogas generation, and bio-fuels production are presented in Table 5. Enteric CH4 emissions are estimated with the method developed by Moe and Tyrrell (1979), which accounts for the chemical composition change (nonstructural carbohydrate, hemicellulose, and cellulose fractions) in each of the analyzed diet scenarios. GHGs from crops production and manure handling are calculated through emission factors and equations from the literature. For the manure management scenario with AD, it is assumed that all CH4 from biogas is converted to CO2 through combustion. The production of biogas reduces the total amount of VS in manure, decreasing CH4 emissions from manure storage after digestion.
26: <header>2.3.3 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

Life cycle inventory data are obtained from the literature, mathematical equations, and the US Life Cycle Inventory (USLCI 2012) and Ecoinvent 2.0 U.S. (Ecoinvent 2010) databases accessed through GaBi 4 Professional® software (PE International 2010). Database inventory include nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) fertilizers, diesel, gasoline, natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), and cotton production. The electricity matrix represents the mix of fuels that are part of the electric grid of WI and is presented in Table S1 in the Supplementary Information (SI).

Herd Structure and Nutrition. The modeled dairy farm is located in WI and produces 10,000 kg of milk per day at 3.0% protein, 3.5% fat, and 4.85% lactose concentrations, which are representative of WI (AgSource 2009). To achieve this milk production, the farm houses 286 lactating cows (650 kg body weight), 173 growing heifers (347 kg body weight), 41 mature heifers and dry COWS (285 kg body weight), and 9 calves up to one month old (52 kg body weight) in free-stall barns (Powell et al. 2008). It is assumed that the dry period is 62 days, the calving interval is 14 months, and the annual adult replacement rate including mortality and culling is 35.8%, which are typical values for WI dairies (AgSource 2009). The annual mortality rate is 1.8% for weaned heifers and 7.8% for un-weaned heifers (USDA 2007), and the new born female to male ratio is 0.47 (Del Rio et al. 2007). It is assumed that animals achieve their mature weight at 21 months old and that all heifers needed to maintain the herd size are raised on-farm as replacements. For nutrition purposes, non-lactating animals are divided into three categories: heifers from 1 to 11 months old; heifers from 12 to 21 months old; and heifers older than 21 months old and dry cows. N, P, and K flows from feed to milk and manure are calculated based on dry matter intake (DMI) and nutrient intake values according to NRC (2001) (Table 1). Energy requirements for housing and milking are 0.062 MJ/kg milk and include space heating, ventilation, lighting, milking, milk cooling, and water heating (ECW, 2009).

Diet Scenarios. The reference diet is representative of management practices in WI (Armentano, L. University of Wisconsin-Madison, Wisconsin, personal communication) and is formulated separately for young stock, non-lactating cows, and lactating cows (Table 2). This is done to meet maintenance, growth, pregnancy, and milk production requirements (NRC 2001). Alterations of the reference diet are formulated to represent different forage types as well as

feeding of concentrates derived from biofuels production possesses. It is assumed that calves younger than 1 month do not receive solid feed and are fed milk only at a 4 kg milk/day rate. Heifers are fed harvested alfalfa and corn silage, with the difference that the diet of heifers between 1 and 11 months is supplemented with concentrate. Six diet scenarios are formulated for lactating COWS based on rumen undegradable protein and rumen degradable protein requirements. These scenarios include a reference diet scenario representative of the system that does not integrate dairy and bioenergy production, and five diet scenarios that represent the integrated dairy and bioenergy system: one diet with equal amounts of corn silage and alfalfa silage, and equal amounts of DDGS and SBM (CADS); two diets with more corn silage than alfalfa silage: one maximizing DDGS (CSDG) and one maximizing SBM (CSSB); and two diets with more alfalfa silage than corn silage: one maximizing DDGS (ASDG) and one diet maximizing SBM (ASSB). All lactating cow diets target a crude protein (CP) concentration of 17% based on NRC (2001) recommendations.

Crop Production. Chemicals, energy, fuels, machinery, and nutrients required to produce crops for dairy feed, corn ethanol, and soybean biodiesel are listed in Table 3 and the related primary energy required for their manufacturing is presented in Table S2. Fossil fuels (gasoline, diesel, LPG, natural gas) and renewable fuels (biogas, ethanol and biodiesel) consumption are calculated based on energy density values (Table S3). N, P, and K are applied according to crop requirements, prioritizing the use of dairy manure over purchased fertilizers, which are only included into the system when nutrient targets are not met with manure. It is assumed that all of the P and K from manure applied to soils are available to plants, but just 50% of the applied N (Beegle, 2008). The land area required to produce dairy feed crops is calculated from yield

averages for alfalfa silage (7,865 kg dry matter/ha), corn silage (13,097 kg dry matter/ha), corn grain (7,591 kg dry matter/ha), soybean (2,386 kg dry matter/ha) and cotton seed (1,031 kg dry matter/ha) (USDA-NASS 2009 and 2010). The total area required to produce corn grain and soybeans is considered in integrated bioenergy and dairy scenarios given that the boundaries of the system are expanded to include ethanol and biodiesel production.

28: <header>2.3.4 Assigning Environmental Impacts among Co-products </header>

Both analyzed systems (with and without integration of bioenergy production) are multi- functional, and thus, require a method to assign the environmental burdens among milk and co- - products. In the reference system, allocation is applied to partition the burdens between SBM and oil at the input level, and meat and milk at the output level. In the integrated dairy and bioenergy system, system expansion is applied to assign the burdens among milk and bioenergy products.

System Expansion. Environmental impacts are assigned to corn ethanol, soybean biodiesel, and biogas based on the assumption that the production of these fuels displaces an equal amount of gasoline, petro-diesel, and natural gas.

SBM-Oil Allocation. Allocation is needed to partition the environmental burdens between SBM and oil in the reference system given that the bio-refining process for soybean biodiesel is not included in the system boundaries. A mass allocation approach that assigns 78.6% of the burdens to milk and 21.4% to meat is applied based on Wang (2010).

Milk-Meat Allocation. The percentage of adult animals accounted as culled animal meat suitable for human consumption is assumed to be 19.3% per year (AgSource 2009). Meat is valued based on its nutritional content compared to the nutritional content of milk produced during the same time period. The same logic of FPCM is used to calculate "milk corrected meat" (MCM) to partition the environmental burdens between milk and meat (Equation 4). Boneless meat production is 195 kg/culled adult dairy cow (Cederberg and Stadig 2003) with a fat content of 6.5% and protein content of 20.7% (Franco et al. 2009; Minchin et al., 2009). This method allocates 98.99% of the system inputs and outputs to milk and 1.01% to meat.
(0.0920 * Meat Fat) + (0.0547 * Meat Protein) MCM = Meat * Cows (0.0920 * FPCM fat) + (0.0547 * FPCM protein) (4)
Where:
MCM = milk corrected meat (kg boneless meat per culled cow) Cows = number of culled COWS for human consumption Meat Fat = fat content in milk Meat Protein = protein content in meat
FPCM fat = fat content in fat protein corrected milk
FPCM protein = protein content in fat protein corrected milk

30: <header>2.4.5 Results for the Whole System </header>

Total land use, NEI, and GHG emissions for the reference system with no assignment of environmental impacts to meat are 1.62 m²/kg FPCM, 3.82 MJ/kg FPCM, and 1.03 kg CO2- eq/kg FPCM. These results are comparable to those found in other LCA studies that report both milk and meat production previous to any partition of environmental burdens. Capper et al. (2009) reported US averages of 1.62 m2/kg FPCM for land use and 1.35 kg CO2-eq/kg milk for GHG emissions. Cederberg and Stadig (2003) reported 3 MJ/kg ECM for energy use and 1.05 kg CO2-eq/kg of ECM for GHG emissions; and Mc Geough et al. (2012) reported 0.92 kg CO2 eq/kg FPCM. The integrated dairy and bioenergy system produces milk, meat, corn ethanol, soybean biodiesel, and biogas at rates that depend on the lactating cow diet scenario. The production rate for these co-products and the land use, NEI and GHG emissions per kg of FPCM for each scenario without assigning environmental impacts to milk is presented in Table 6.
31: <header>2.4.6 Results Assigned to Milk </header>

Land Use. The land area required assigned to milk production in the reference system is 1.6 m²/kg FPCM after assigning the environmental impacts among products. Land use increases an average of 9.1% in diet scenarios that include ethanol and biodiesel production when compared to the reference system, going from 1.68 m² 2 (ASSB), 1.70 m ² 2 (CADS), 1.71 m² (CSSB), 1.74 m² (ASDG), to 2.01 m² (CSDG) per kg of FPCM. Land use results are greater when DDGS content

in the diet is maximized within scenarios with similar forage characteristics (i.e. CSDG VS. CSSB, and ASDG VS. ASSB). This is attributed to the higher DDGS content in diet CSDG when compared to the SBM content in diet CSSB (4.1 VS. 1.5 kg DMI/lactating cow), and the lower yields of DDGS/kg of corn than SBM/kg of soybean (0.33 kg DDGS/kg corn VS 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybean). Despite these differences, land use area is not increased in the same proportion given the higher yields of corn compared to soybean.

Net Energy Intensity (NEI). NEI assigned to milk production for the reference system is 3.78 MJ/kg FPCM. This result is within the 1.31 to 6.57 MJ/kg milk range reported by the International Dairy Federation (IDF 2009), and higher than the 2.55 to 2.76 MJ/kg ECM range reported by Cederberg and Stadig (2003). NEI is reduced, when compared to the reference system, in all diet scenarios that integrate corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel production and do not consider AD as a manure management practice. This reduction ranges from -1.75 (CSDG) to 3.17 (ASSB) MJ/kg FPCM (Figure 2). Lower NEI is achieved when maximizing the concentration of DDGS (CSDG) than when maximizing the concentration of SBM (CSSB). Besides the higher DDGS content in diet CSDG than SBM in diet CSSB (which results in overall higher ethanol production than biodiesel); this lower NEI is justified by a higher biofuel yield for corn ethanol (0.33 kg ethanol/kg corn VS 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybean). The inclusion of manure AD makes all of the diet scenarios net energy producers. On average, the production of biogas contributes 1.85 MJ/kg FPCM to the system and avoids 2.05 MJ/kg FPCM due to the displacement of natural gas. The overall NEI of milk production, including bio-fuels and biogas, ranges from -5.63 (CSDG) to -0.73 (ASSB) MJ/kg FPCM. A detailed table with NEI results is presented in the SI (Table S5).

Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. GHG emissions assigned to milk production for the reference system are 1.02 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. The largest single contributor to GHG emissions is enteric CH4 with 41% of total GHG, followed by CH4 from manure storage (27%), CO2-eq from manufacturing the inputs for crops production (12%), N2O from soils (11%), CO2-eq from milking energy use (5%), and N2O from manure (4%). These results are comparable to the 0.84 to 0.92 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM reported by Mc Geough et al. (2012) and the 0.89 to 0.97 kg CO2- eq/kg ECM (depending on the allocation method) reported by Cederberg and Stadig (2003); higher than the 0.70 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM reported by Rotz et al. (2010); and lower than the 1.35 kg CO2-eq/kg of milk reported by Capper et al. (2009) and the 1.23 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM reported by Thoma et al. (2013). Even though all of these results come from cradle-to-farm gate LCA studies, there are methodological differences that need to be considered when conducting comparisons (e.g. allocation and functional unit). Mc Geough et al. (2012) apply two mass allocation approaches to assign 85.6% and 73.4% of the impacts to milk. Rotz et al. (2010) apply economic allocation and assigns 91.7% of the burdens to milk, whereas Cederberg and Stadig (2003) uses economic and biological cause-effect allocation approaches to partition 92% and 85% of the environmental burdens to milk. Another difference is the definition of the functional unit as Mc Geough et al. (2010) adjust FPCM to 3.85% fat and 3.03% protein; Rotz et al. (2010) adjusts FPCM to 3.5% fat and 3.1% protein; and FAO (2010) adjusts FPCM to 4% fat and 3.3% protein.

The average GHG emission reduction for all diet scenarios that integrate corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel production (no AD) is 7% when compared to the reference system. GHG emissions assigned to milk for these diet scenarios range from 0.86 (CSDG) to 1.00 (ASSB) kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM (Figure 3). The effect of bio-fuels production can be assessed by comparing

diet scenarios within the land-applied manure management scenario. Diet CSDG (high in corn silage and DDGS) has the lowest GHG emissions and diet ASSB (high in alfalfa silage and no DDGS) has the highest emissions. This 0.14 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM difference is mainly attributed to the avoidance of gasoline production and combustion in diet scenarios with DDGS. However, diets CSDG and ASDG are high in DDGS but show large differences in overall GHG emissions, which highlight the influence that the forage fraction can have on the final results. The effect of biogas generation can be assessed by comparing manure management scenarios (manure land- application VS. manure AD). The average effect of including AD on the dairy farm reduces GHG emissions from milk production by 0.23 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, which is lower than the 0.32 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM reduction reported by Rotz et al. (2010). 57% of this reduction is attributed to the avoided emissions from natural gas production and combustion and 43% is attributed to the decrease in CH4 emissions from manure storage. Overall, the lowest GHG emissions are obtained when DDGS are maximized in the dairy diet (CSDG) and AD is included as a manure management practice, given that both gasoline and natural gas are partially replaced by the ethanol and biogas produced by the system with the system expansion approach. A detailed table with emission results is presented in the SI (Table S6).
32: <header>2.4.6 Results Assigned to Milk </header>

Land Use. The land area required assigned to milk production in the reference system is 1.6 m²/kg FPCM after assigning the environmental impacts among products. Land use increases an average of 9.1% in diet scenarios that include ethanol and biodiesel production when compared to the reference system, going from 1.68 m² 2 (ASSB), 1.70 m ² 2 (CADS), 1.71 m² (CSSB), 1.74 m² (ASDG), to 2.01 m² (CSDG) per kg of FPCM. Land use results are greater when DDGS content

in the diet is maximized within scenarios with similar forage characteristics (i.e. CSDG VS. CSSB, and ASDG VS. ASSB). This is attributed to the higher DDGS content in diet CSDG when compared to the SBM content in diet CSSB (4.1 VS. 1.5 kg DMI/lactating cow), and the lower yields of DDGS/kg of corn than SBM/kg of soybean (0.33 kg DDGS/kg corn VS 0.79 kg SBM/kg soybean). Despite these differences, land use area is not increased in the same proportion given the higher yields of corn compared to soybean.

Net Energy Intensity (NEI). NEI assigned to milk production for the reference system is 3.78 MJ/kg FPCM. This result is within the 1.31 to 6.57 MJ/kg milk range reported by the International Dairy Federation (IDF 2009), and higher than the 2.55 to 2.76 MJ/kg ECM range reported by Cederberg and Stadig (2003). NEI is reduced, when compared to the reference system, in all diet scenarios that integrate corn ethanol and soybean biodiesel production and do not consider AD as a manure management practice. This reduction ranges from -1.75 (CSDG) to 3.17 (ASSB) MJ/kg FPCM (Figure 2). Lower NEI is achieved when maximizing the concentration of DDGS (CSDG) than when maximizing the concentration of SBM (CSSB). Besides the higher DDGS content in diet CSDG than SBM in diet CSSB (which results in overall higher ethanol production than biodiesel); this lower NEI is justified by a higher biofuel yield for corn ethanol (0.33 kg ethanol/kg corn VS 0.18 kg biodiesel/kg soybean). The inclusion of manure AD makes all of the diet scenarios net energy producers. On average, the production of biogas contributes 1.85 MJ/kg FPCM to the system and avoids 2.05 MJ/kg FPCM due to the displacement of natural gas. The overall NEI of milk production, including bio-fuels and biogas, ranges from -5.63 (CSDG) to -0.73 (ASSB) MJ/kg FPCM. A detailed table with NEI results is presented in the SI (Table S5).

36: <header>2.4.7 Comparing Marginal Changes </header>

Changes of land use, NEI, and GHG emissions of each scenario of the integrated dairy and bio-energy system are compared to the reference system before and after assigning environmental impacts to milk (Figure 4). The reference system produces milk and meat, whereas the integrated system produces ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas in addition to milk and meat. Before system expansion, diet scenarios with more DDGS use more land, emit more GHG,

and have higher NEI per kg of FPCM (Figure 4a). AD contributes to reduce GHG emissions by nearly 10% and makes all scenarios energy producers. After system expansion, diet scenarios with more DDGS (CSDG and ASDG) are the most effective in reducing GHG emissions and NEI when compared to other diets within the same group in terms of forage (Figure 4b).

Including DDGS in the diet has a bigger influence on environmental impacts than SBM given the higher ethanol yields per kg of DDGS dry matter intake when compared to the yields of soybean biodiesel per kg of SBM dry matter intake. AD contributes to reduce GHG emissions by more than 20% and NEI by 100%. Despite these environmental benefits, only 0.2% dairy farms in WI have implemented digesters. Some factors that have been limiting a wider implementation of digesters in the state are the high capital costs and the low density of manure as an energy source in comparison to other biomass feedstocks. Among some of the strategies that dairy farmers are already applying to increase the profit of their digester systems include co-digestion of manure with higher energy sources available to the farm (e.g. corn stover) and increasing the value of the digestate (i.e. digested solids used as fertilizer or soil amendment).

This study provides a comprehensive assessment of milk production systems, but there are some factors that might underestimate environmental impacts. First, even though the impacts related to equipment and buildings for bioenergy production are included in the system boundaries, the impacts related to milking and cropping machinery are not included in the analysis. The inclusion of these operations is not expected to change the results as they have been cited to have a small influence on the total life cycle (Kim et al. 2009). Second, the model assumes no losses throughout crops harvesting, transportation and storage, milk production, and manure management activities; therefore, the related impacts to compensate these losses are not assessed. Third, the effects of AD on NH3 or N2O volatilization after manure application to soils

are not analyzed, which might underestimate NH3 and indirect N2O emissions (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014). Fourth, carbon neutrality was assumed for biotic CO2 emissions, but previous studies suggest that the combustion of biodiesel from soybeans can increase NOx emissions by up to 15%, which could reduce GHG credits by up to 4% (EPA 2002). Finally, it is assumed that biogas displaces the production and combustion of natural gas because it is the most similar primary fossil energy source. However, biogas is generally used to produce electricity in WI, which would affect both NEI due to lower conversion efficiencies, and GHG credits given that electricity in WI comes mainly from coal.
37: <header>2.4.7 Comparing Marginal Changes </header>

Changes of land use, NEI, and GHG emissions of each scenario of the integrated dairy and bio-energy system are compared to the reference system before and after assigning environmental impacts to milk (Figure 4). The reference system produces milk and meat, whereas the integrated system produces ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas in addition to milk and meat. Before system expansion, diet scenarios with more DDGS use more land, emit more GHG,

and have higher NEI per kg of FPCM (Figure 4a). AD contributes to reduce GHG emissions by nearly 10% and makes all scenarios energy producers. After system expansion, diet scenarios with more DDGS (CSDG and ASDG) are the most effective in reducing GHG emissions and NEI when compared to other diets within the same group in terms of forage (Figure 4b).

Including DDGS in the diet has a bigger influence on environmental impacts than SBM given the higher ethanol yields per kg of DDGS dry matter intake when compared to the yields of soybean biodiesel per kg of SBM dry matter intake. AD contributes to reduce GHG emissions by more than 20% and NEI by 100%. Despite these environmental benefits, only 0.2% dairy farms in WI have implemented digesters. Some factors that have been limiting a wider implementation of digesters in the state are the high capital costs and the low density of manure as an energy source in comparison to other biomass feedstocks. Among some of the strategies that dairy farmers are already applying to increase the profit of their digester systems include co-digestion of manure with higher energy sources available to the farm (e.g. corn stover) and increasing the value of the digestate (i.e. digested solids used as fertilizer or soil amendment).

This study provides a comprehensive assessment of milk production systems, but there are some factors that might underestimate environmental impacts. First, even though the impacts related to equipment and buildings for bioenergy production are included in the system boundaries, the impacts related to milking and cropping machinery are not included in the analysis. The inclusion of these operations is not expected to change the results as they have been cited to have a small influence on the total life cycle (Kim et al. 2009). Second, the model assumes no losses throughout crops harvesting, transportation and storage, milk production, and manure management activities; therefore, the related impacts to compensate these losses are not assessed. Third, the effects of AD on NH3 or N2O volatilization after manure application to soils

are not analyzed, which might underestimate NH3 and indirect N2O emissions (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014). Fourth, carbon neutrality was assumed for biotic CO2 emissions, but previous studies suggest that the combustion of biodiesel from soybeans can increase NOx emissions by up to 15%, which could reduce GHG credits by up to 4% (EPA 2002). Finally, it is assumed that biogas displaces the production and combustion of natural gas because it is the most similar primary fossil energy source. However, biogas is generally used to produce electricity in WI, which would affect both NEI due to lower conversion efficiencies, and GHG credits given that electricity in WI comes mainly from coal.

40: <header>2.5 Conclusions </header>

Land use, net energy intensity (NEI), and GHG emissions of a reference dairy system representative of WI management practices are compared to a system that integrates dairy and bioenergy production. The impacts of five different diets and two manure scenarios are evaluated in the integrated system. Diet scenarios that maximize DDGS content require the most land area, but are also the most effective in reducing GHG emissions and NEI, demonstrating the environmental trade-offs that need to be considered when analyzing different diet strategies. Reductions in GHG emissions and NEI come mostly from the credits of avoided emissions and primary energy from displaced fossil fuels after assigning the environmental impacts to milk through system expansion. GHG emissions are further reduced by nearly 35% and NEI by 100% when implementing AD as a manure management practice. Overall, the integration of dairy and bioenergy production has the biggest influence on NEI when comparing marginal changes of environmental impacts with respect to the reference system.

The dairy industry will continue to dominate agricultural activities in WI for the foreseeable future and the emerging bioenergy industry will need to be integrated into existing agricultural systems. System models like this one will help farmers and policy makers identify synergies between dairy production and renewable energy development. The system boundary expansion approach applied in this study reduces ambiguities produced by allocation decisions at the feedstock and co-product levels of integrated dairy and bioenergy systems, and provides a methodology for future studies.
41: <header>2.5 Conclusions </header>

Land use, net energy intensity (NEI), and GHG emissions of a reference dairy system representative of WI management practices are compared to a system that integrates dairy and bioenergy production. The impacts of five different diets and two manure scenarios are evaluated in the integrated system. Diet scenarios that maximize DDGS content require the most land area, but are also the most effective in reducing GHG emissions and NEI, demonstrating the environmental trade-offs that need to be considered when analyzing different diet strategies. Reductions in GHG emissions and NEI come mostly from the credits of avoided emissions and primary energy from displaced fossil fuels after assigning the environmental impacts to milk through system expansion. GHG emissions are further reduced by nearly 35% and NEI by 100% when implementing AD as a manure management practice. Overall, the integration of dairy and bioenergy production has the biggest influence on NEI when comparing marginal changes of environmental impacts with respect to the reference system.

The dairy industry will continue to dominate agricultural activities in WI for the foreseeable future and the emerging bioenergy industry will need to be integrated into existing agricultural systems. System models like this one will help farmers and policy makers identify synergies between dairy production and renewable energy development. The system boundary expansion approach applied in this study reduces ambiguities produced by allocation decisions at the feedstock and co-product levels of integrated dairy and bioenergy systems, and provides a methodology for future studies.

0: <title>Life Cycle Impact Assessment and Allocation Methods Development for Cheese and Whey Processing </title>
H. A. Aguirre-Villegas, F.X. Milani, S. Kraatz, D.J. Reinemann (2012) Transactions of the ASABE 55(2):613-627
1: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methods are used to develop Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) and Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) data to estimate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and Energy Intensity (EI) of cheese and dry whey manufacture in Wisconsin, using a farm gate to plant gate approach. All of the environmental burdens and benefits are assigned to the product (cheese) and none to the land-spread waste stream (whey) for a single-output system typical of small cheese plants in Wisconsin. For a multi-function system, in which both cheese and food- grade whey are produced, the following methods to handle co-product multi-functionality are applied: subdivision, allocation ratios, and a method that combines both subdivision and allocation ratios. Total solids, nutritional content, and economic value are considered as the allocation ratios. Each of these fixed allocation ratios is applied to the entire process and to individual processes unique to each product. The differences in GHG emissions and EI for cheese and dry whey are highly influenced by the choice of method. The EI of cheese ranges from 7.1 MJ kg cheese to 19 MJ kg) cheese and GHG emissions range from 0.46 kg CO2-eq kg cheese to 1.3kg CO2-eq kg` cheese. The main source of these differences is the shift of environmental burdens from cheese to the dry whey co-product resulting from different

allocation strategies. The method that combines subdivision and allocation is presented as the preferred and most accurate method to deal with the multi-functionality of cheese and whey manufacture. Sensitivity analysis shows that GHG emissions and EI are most affected by variations in milk pasteurization, whey evaporation, whey drying, and whey pasteurization. This study demonstrates the importance of the allocation method on LCA analysis and suggests methods to more accurately assess the environmental burdens when more than one product is produced at a dairy plant.
2: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methods are used to develop Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) and Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) data to estimate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and Energy Intensity (EI) of cheese and dry whey manufacture in Wisconsin, using a farm gate to plant gate approach. All of the environmental burdens and benefits are assigned to the product (cheese) and none to the land-spread waste stream (whey) for a single-output system typical of small cheese plants in Wisconsin. For a multi-function system, in which both cheese and food- grade whey are produced, the following methods to handle co-product multi-functionality are applied: subdivision, allocation ratios, and a method that combines both subdivision and allocation ratios. Total solids, nutritional content, and economic value are considered as the allocation ratios. Each of these fixed allocation ratios is applied to the entire process and to individual processes unique to each product. The differences in GHG emissions and EI for cheese and dry whey are highly influenced by the choice of method. The EI of cheese ranges from 7.1 MJ kg cheese to 19 MJ kg) cheese and GHG emissions range from 0.46 kg CO2-eq kg cheese to 1.3kg CO2-eq kg` cheese. The main source of these differences is the shift of environmental burdens from cheese to the dry whey co-product resulting from different

allocation strategies. The method that combines subdivision and allocation is presented as the preferred and most accurate method to deal with the multi-functionality of cheese and whey manufacture. Sensitivity analysis shows that GHG emissions and EI are most affected by variations in milk pasteurization, whey evaporation, whey drying, and whey pasteurization. This study demonstrates the importance of the allocation method on LCA analysis and suggests methods to more accurately assess the environmental burdens when more than one product is produced at a dairy plant.
3: Climate change and its link to fossil energy use have become important environmental priorities of the global community. More sustainable production practices have been strongly encouraged by governments, consumers, and private industries around the world. Government policies have been developed to address energy independence and climate change by promoting technologies to reduce energy use as well as replacement of fossil fuels with alternative fuels that result in lower Global Warming Potential (GWP). As consumers become more aware of the environmental impacts of the goods and services they purchase, the demand for more environmentally sustainable products increases. Consumer preferences have caused private industries to assess the environmental consequences of their production practices and seek more environmentally friendly methods to supply their products.

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a widely accepted tool to estimate the environmental impacts of producing goods and services (Jimenez-Gonzales et al. 2000; Reap et al. 2008; and Thomassen and Dalgaard 2008). LCA is defined by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) as the "compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and the potential

environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle" (ISO 2006a; ISO 2006b). LCA has proven to be a useful tool to evaluate complex systems and is beginning to be applied to food production, but, is still in need of improvement (Finnveden et al. 2009). One of the limitations of applying LCA to food systems is the lack of Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) and Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) data for food production and processing operations. Reap et al. (2008) identified 15 major problem areas of LCA, allocation methods being among the most critical. There are many ways to allocate inputs of a common process to its multiple outputs and the choice of the allocation strategy has been cited as highly influential in LCA results (Curran 2007; Kaufman et al. 2010; Kim and Overcash 2000). Frischknecht (2000) stated that allocation is one of the most controversial and unresolved problems of LCA because of differences in the allocation methodology and the resultant variability introduced in estimating environmental impacts.

Allocation is the process of attributing the relative environmental burdens and benefits of a system to each primary and co-product of this system (Gerber et al. 2010). Allocation strategies are particularly important in food production systems as they usually have multiple product streams. Allocation can be based on physical relationships such as mass, volume, carbon content, energy content or by the economic value of the outputs (Berlin 2002). Whole process allocation can shift the majority of the environmental burdens from one product to the other according to which allocation strategy is applied (Luo et al. 2009). Feitz et al. (2007) noted that the variability of results introduced when applying fixed allocation ratios at a whole dairy plant level is much larger than the variability introduced by poor data quality or by the use of differing system boundaries.
ISO 14044 provides the following guidance on allocation methods (ISO 2006b; Azapagic and Clift 1999b; Reap et al. 2008):

Ekvall and Finnveden (2001) present a review of the different allocation methods recommended by the ISO, and conclude that, in most systems, subdivision cannot completely eliminate allocation but rather reduce the need for it. The system expansion approach involves either direct system enlargement or accounting for avoided burdens from products that the co- products displace (Azapagic and Clift 1999a). Even though system expansion is the preferred approach to deal with allocation problems in LCA, there are several difficulties when applying it. First, system expansion can lead to a more complicated model that requires more data (Reap et al. 2008; Ekvall and Finnveden 2001) and can introduce greater uncertainty because of the increased complexity of the model and lack of precise knowledge of the use of co-products. Second, it is challenging to apply system expansion when there are no comparable substitutes for a co-product. Finally, it is difficult to perform system expansion in attributional LCA studies since these kind of studies assume a static approach (Thomassen and Dalgaard 2008).
Most of the research aimed at quantifying the environmental impacts of the dairy sector has been focused at the dairy farm level as this sector contributes the most to GHG emissions and

i)|Avoid allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes into sub processes, or |using system expansion to include the co-products; ii)|When allocation cannot be avoided, identify physical relationships between the |multiple products and functions to partition the environmental impacts; and iii)|When a physical relationship cannot be established, inputs and/or outputs should be |allocated by identifying other relationship between products and co-products (i.e. |energy ratio, economic value).

Energy Intensity (EI) (Gerber et al. 2010; Rotz et al. 2010; Passos-Fonseca et al. 2010; Thomassen and Dalgaard 2008). Processing milk into its various consumer products represents the second source of GHG emissions and EI in the dairy sector lifecycle after milk production at the farm level (Guinard et al. 2009; Berlin 2002; and Eide 2002). Details of allocation procedures are lacking in several previous studies (Berlin 2002; Guinard et al. 2009) and the most common method cited is whole system economic allocation, although recognized as the least desirable and accurate allocation method (ISO 2006a). One reason for these shortcomings is a lack of detailed data to formulate more preferable allocation strategies (Feitz et al. 2007).

The dairy industry is important in Wisconsin as 6% of the total state sales come from the dairy sector alone (Deller and Williams 2011). Moreover, Wisconsin is the leading cheese producer in the U.S., with mozzarella and cheddar being the most common varieties. About 1.3 million COWS in this state produced more than 11.3 billion kg of milk in 2009 with 85% of this milk used for manufacturing cheese, accounting for 26% of total U.S. cheese production (NASS 2010). A significant number of small dairy plants in Wisconsin discard whey as a waste stream from cheese manufacturing and can be treated as single-output systems. Most large cheese plants, however, process whey into valuable food products and must be treated as multi-functional systems when performing LCA. An understanding of the sub-processes involved in the production of cheese and its co-products can provide a more technically accurate basis to assign environmental impacts by subdivision and other methods that apply allocation decisions. The system expansion strategy is difficult to apply to whey products as there are many sources of protein and carbohydrates that can be replaced by whey.
The objective of this study is to develop LCI and LCIA data that can be used in single-output and multi-functional LCA analyses that involve the production of cheese and its related dry whey

and whey cream co-products. A further objective is to develop allocation methods to assign environmental burdens of cheese and whey manufacturing based on a combination of process subdivision and allocation ratios.

(2007) noted that the variability of results introduced when applying fixed allocation ratios at a whole dairy plant level is much larger than the variability introduced by poor data quality or by the use of differing system boundaries.

Ekvall and Finnveden (2001) present a review of the different allocation methods recommended by the ISO, and conclude that, in most systems, subdivision cannot completely eliminate allocation but rather reduce the need for it. The system expansion approach involves either direct system enlargement or accounting for avoided burdens from products that the co- products displace (Azapagic and Clift 1999a). 11: <header>3.3 Methods </header>

Two dairy systems are analyzed: 1) a single-output system, where cheddar cheese is the only product and whey is land-spread as waste; 2) a multifunctional-system, which produces cheddar cheese as well as dry whey and whey cream as co-products. Both systems are modeled as a typically sized Wisconsin cheese plant. Analysis of the multi-functional system includes three different strategies to assign environmental impacts of co-products: a) subdivision, a methodology that avoids allocation; b) whole-plant allocation, which is the most commonly applied allocation strategy in the literature; and c) a more complex allocation method that uses process subdivision to identify processes that are common to co-products and those that are unique to those co-products, followed by allocation to assign environmental burdens (GHG emissions and EI). Three different allocation ratios (economic, total solids and nutritional value) are applied where allocation is necessary (methods b and c). The environmental impacts are analyzed using ISO compliant LCA methodology (ISO 2006a; ISO 2006b) and GaBi software (GaBi 2008) as the modeling tool. The Ecoinvent (Ecoinvent Centre 2007), and U.S. LCI (NREL 2008) databases are used for some material and energy inputs. New LCI and LCIA data are developed for those inputs that are not available in these databases ¹
12: <header>3.3.1 System boundaries </header>

A "gate-to-gate" approach is used considering all processes from the dairy farm gate to the dairy plant gate: production of inputs, milk transportation from farm to plant, processing, and packaging and storage at the dairy plant (fig. 1). The boundaries also include the energy needed to produce all inputs.
13: <header>3.3.2 Functional unit </header>

The functional unit is defined as one kg of cheddar cheese leaving the dairy plant gate and packaged as an 18.2 kg block wrapped in plastic and cardboard. Cheddar cheese is further classified as a semi-hard cheese with fat and protein contents of 34.8% and 24.8% respectively (on a total mass basis).
14: <header>Greenhouse Gas Emissions </header>

Greenhouse gas emissions are expressed in kg CO2-eq kg-1 of cheese. The 100-year time horizon used for gases other than CO2 is 298 kg CO2-eq for N2O emissions (Forster et al. 2007), 25 kg CO2-eq for CH4 (Forster et al. 2007), 1,430 kg CO2-eq for HCF-134a refrigerant (GaBi 2008) and 0 kg CO2-eq for ammonia (NH3) refrigerant (Agarwal et al. 2005). Refrigerants are used in the milk reception and cheese cold storage processes and contribute to GWP when they leak out of the refrigeration system.
15: <header>Energy Intensity </header>

Energy Intensity (EI) is expressed in MJ kg-1 of cheese produced. The EI includes the energy used directly at the dairy plant and the embedded energy for all inputs. Embedded energy is defined as the amount of energy needed to produce these inputs. EI includes both Renewable Energy Intensity (REI) and Fossil Energy Intensity (FEI). The FEI is expressed in MJ kg` cheese produced and is an indicator in the Resource Depletion impact category (Curran 2006; Thomassen and Dalgaard 2008).
16: <header>3.3.4 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

The dairy plant archetype processes 203 Gg of milk to produce 19.6 Gg of cheddar cheese per year (NASS 2010). All of the inputs and outputs are expressed in terms of the functional unit of 1 kg of cheddar cheese produced. Assumptions about the components of the incoming fluid milk are presented in table 1, along with a mass balance of water, protein, fat, carbohydrates, and ash as the milk moves through the separation and processing operations. In the multi-functional system 0.615 kg of dry whey and 0.1 kg of whey cream are obtained as co-products for each kg of cheese produced. In the single-output system 8.87 kg of sweet whey and 0.47 kg of salty whey are produced for each kg of cheddar cheese and are land-spread as waste products.

Diesel fueled tanker trucks with a load capacity of 21,700 kg are used to transport milk a distance of 60 km from the farm-gate to the dairy plant (Newton 2010). Milk is assumed to be received at the cheese plant and stored at 7°C until processed. The refrigerant HFC-134a is used in four 35,000 gallon refrigerated storage tanks with a total refrigerant charge of 700 kg of HCF-

134a (Cleland et al. 2009; IFC 2005) and annual leakage rate of 10% (Agarwal et al. 2005; Cleland et al. 2009). Milk is pasteurized using the high temperature short time (HTST) process to kill pathogens and is then pumped into 'make' vats where bacterial culture, rennet, and CaCl2 are added to start the coagulation process. Calcium is assumed to stay with the cheese while the chloride and rennet are soluble in water with the majority exiting in the whey stream. Salt is then added in a process known as cheddaring: matting and stacking the curds in the vat to hasten the drainage of whey. After the slabs are drained of whey, they are diced into pieces. The pieces are then salted with 55% of the salt staying with the cheese and the remaining 45% lost with the whey. Addition of salt draws out more whey from the pieces producing 'salty whey'. The pieces are then collected and pressed for up to 24 hours to form 18.2 kg blocks of cheese. The blocks are packaged in polyethylene film and cardboard. Finally, the blocks of cheese are cold stored to allow the curds to fully knit (Sun 2005). Cheddar cheese may be consumed only a few weeks after manufacture or aged for months to years to develop a more complex flavor and texture. A period of 70 days is considered for the aging process (NASS 2010) where ammonia is used as the refrigerant. Pearson (1999) and Pearson (2003) reported an optimum ammonia charge of 0.4 kg NH3 kg Ton Refrigerated (TR), and presented values based on chiller technology: 0.1 kg NH3 kg-1 TR for a gravity feed evaporator chiller, 1.77 kg NH3 kg TR for a plate heat exchanger with dry suction, and 3.5 kg NH3 kg TR for an air cooled or evaporator condenser. For the cheddar cheese cold storage, 6,748 kg of ammonia (NH3) are assumed as the annual refrigerant charge (Pearson 1999; Pearson 2003) with a yearly leakage rate of 17% (Agarwal et al. 2005).
There are a number of steps involved with processing whey after it is removed from the 'make' vats. The whey is HTST pasteurized and the whey cream is removed using a continuous

centrifugal cream separator. There is the inevitable loss of fat from cheese and most cheese plants choose to recover this fat due to its high value. The remaining fat-clarified whey, at 6.8% total solids (TS), is subjected to three processes that remove water: reverse osmosis (RO), which increases the TS content to 18%, evaporation further increases TS to 60%, and final spray drying provides a TS content of 96%. Pasteurizers, evaporators, and dryers are cleaned with sodium hydroxide (NaOH 49% concentration) and nitric acid (HNO3 62% concentration) after each processing cycle (Berlin 2002) to reduce contamination risk. Details of the unit processes for cheddar cheese production are illustrated in figure 2 and table 2.

The single-output system does not include any of the steps related to whey processing, except for the unit processes needed to handle whey as waste. Both sweet and salty whey are hauled a distance of 15 miles in 24,500 L diesel fuel tanker trucks (Byrne 2012). After reaching the entrance of the field, sweet and salty whey are land spread by a tanker spreader at an unloading velocity of 14.2 km h1 (Harrigan 2009; Welty et al. 1986) and application rates of 150,000 L Ha for sweet whey and 4,700 L Ha-1 for salty whey (Byrne 2012).

Pasteurizers, evaporators, and dryers are cleaned with sodium hydroxide (NaOH 49% concentration) and nitric acid (HNO3 62% concentration) after each processing cycle (Berlin 2002) to reduce contamination risk. Details of the unit processes for cheddar cheese production are illustrated in figure 2 and table 2.

The single-output system does not include any of the steps related to whey processing, except for the unit processes needed to handle whey as waste. Both sweet and salty whey are hauled a distance of 15 miles in 24,500 L diesel fuel tanker trucks (Byrne 2012). After reaching the entrance of the field, sweet and salty whey are land spread by a tanker spreader at an unloading velocity of 14.2 km h1 (Harrigan 2009; Welty et al. 1986) and application rates of 150,000 L Ha for sweet whey and 4,700 L Ha-1 for salty whey (Byrne 2012).
19: <header>3.3.4 Life Cycle Inventory </header>

The dairy plant archetype processes 203 Gg of milk to produce 19.6 Gg of cheddar cheese per year (NASS 2010). All of the inputs and outputs are expressed in terms of the functional unit of 1 kg of cheddar cheese produced. Assumptions about the components of the incoming fluid milk are presented in table 1, along with a mass balance of water, protein, fat, carbohydrates, and ash as the milk moves through the separation and processing operations. In the multi-functional system 0.615 kg of dry whey and 0.1 kg of whey cream are obtained as co-products for each kg of cheese produced. In the single-output system 8.87 kg of sweet whey and 0.47 kg of salty whey are produced for each kg of cheddar cheese and are land-spread as waste products.

Diesel fueled tanker trucks with a load capacity of 21,700 kg are used to transport milk a distance of 60 km from the farm-gate to the dairy plant (Newton 2010). Milk is assumed to be received at the cheese plant and stored at 7°C until processed. The refrigerant HFC-134a is used in four 35,000 gallon refrigerated storage tanks with a total refrigerant charge of 700 kg of HCF-

134a (Cleland et al. 2009; IFC 2005) and annual leakage rate of 10% (Agarwal et al. 2005; Cleland et al. 2009). Milk is pasteurized using the high temperature short time (HTST) process to kill pathogens and is then pumped into 'make' vats where bacterial culture, rennet, and CaCl2 are added to start the coagulation process. Calcium is assumed to stay with the cheese while the chloride and rennet are soluble in water with the majority exiting in the whey stream. Salt is then added in a process known as cheddaring: matting and stacking the curds in the vat to hasten the drainage of whey. After the slabs are drained of whey, they are diced into pieces. The pieces are then salted with 55% of the salt staying with the cheese and the remaining 45% lost with the whey. Addition of salt draws out more whey from the pieces producing 'salty whey'. The pieces are then collected and pressed for up to 24 hours to form 18.2 kg blocks of cheese. The blocks are packaged in polyethylene film and cardboard. Finally, the blocks of cheese are cold stored to allow the curds to fully knit (Sun 2005). Cheddar cheese may be consumed only a few weeks after manufacture or aged for months to years to develop a more complex flavor and texture. A period of 70 days is considered for the aging process (NASS 2010) where ammonia is used as the refrigerant. Pearson (1999) and Pearson (2003) reported an optimum ammonia charge of 0.4 kg NH3 kg Ton Refrigerated (TR), and presented values based on chiller technology: 0.1 kg NH3 kg-1 TR for a gravity feed evaporator chiller, 1.77 kg NH3 kg TR for a plate heat exchanger with dry suction, and 3.5 kg NH3 kg TR for an air cooled or evaporator condenser. For the cheddar cheese cold storage, 6,748 kg of ammonia (NH3) are assumed as the annual refrigerant charge (Pearson 1999; Pearson 2003) with a yearly leakage rate of 17% (Agarwal et al. 2005).
There are a number of steps involved with processing whey after it is removed from the 'make' vats. The whey is HTST pasteurized and the whey cream is removed using a continuous

centrifugal cream separator. There is the inevitable loss of fat from cheese and most cheese plants choose to recover this fat due to its high value. The remaining fat-clarified whey, at 6.8% total solids (TS), is subjected to three processes that remove water: reverse osmosis (RO), which increases the TS content to 18%, evaporation further increases TS to 60%, and final spray drying provides a TS content of 96%. Pasteurizers, evaporators, and dryers are cleaned with sodium hydroxide (NaOH 49% concentration) and nitric acid (HNO3 62% concentration) after each processing cycle (Berlin 2002) to reduce contamination risk. Details of the unit processes for cheddar cheese production are illustrated in figure 2 and table 2.

The single-output system does not include any of the steps related to whey processing, except for the unit processes needed to handle whey as waste. Both sweet and salty whey are hauled a distance of 15 miles in 24,500 L diesel fuel tanker trucks (Byrne 2012). After reaching the entrance of the field, sweet and salty whey are land spread by a tanker spreader at an unloading velocity of 14.2 km h1 (Harrigan 2009; Welty et al. 1986) and application rates of 150,000 L Ha for sweet whey and 4,700 L Ha-1 for salty whey (Byrne 2012).

21: <header>Energy inputs </header>

Four major energy inputs are considered in this analysis: 1) diesel fuel for transporting milk from the farm to the plant, and transporting and spreading whey, 2) electricity for cold-storage of milk and cheese, lighting, cream and whey separation and other mechanical operations, 3) natural gas as the heat source for pasteurization, evaporation, and cleaning, and 4) embedded energy, or energy used to produce inputs. Diesel requirements for milk transport are assumed to be 0.011 L kg-1 of cheese as calculated from state average raw milk transportation distances (Newton 2010). Reception, cold-storage, and cleaning of the cold-storage tanks are assumed to

use 0.312 MJ kg milk of thermal energy and 0.1961 MJ kg-1 milk of electric energy (Tan et al. 2011). These processes represent nearly 30% of the total energy-use required for cheese production (Riva et al. 1992). Electrical energy used for refrigeration during cold storage is calculated as 10% of the total energy consumed by those processes unique to cheese production (Xu et al. 2009). Ventilation, lighting, and air conditioning are assumed to comprise 20% of the total electricity use (Sun 2005). An efficiency of 75% is assumed for industrial boilers using natural gas for heating applications (IEA ETSAP 2010).

Milk pasteurization requires thermal energy to heat milk to 72°C. Electric energy is required to operate pumps for moving milk and pressurized hot water, and vacuum pumps to recover energy in the regeneration section of the HTST process. After pasteurization, milk is cooled to 31°C before entering the 'make 'vats. The energy requirements for milk using the plate pasteurization and heat recovery process are assumed to be 0.25 MJ kg 1 milk for heat energy and 0.1 MJ kg-1 milk for electrical energy (Ozyurt et al., 2004).

Energy inputs for whey pasteurization are calculated for a temperature change from 55 to 72°C with the same assumptions for electrical energy used for pumping and heat recovery as in the milk pasteurization process. Thermal energy requirements for whey pasteurization are calculated according to the following equation:
Q = mCAT (1)
Where:
Q = heat added (J) m = mass (g) C = specific heat capacity of the product (J g 1 °C-1)
= 3.89 for milk, 2.1 for cheese, and 0.954 for whey (Zehr, 1997)
