24: <header>AT = temperature change (C) </header>

Whey Pasteurization is followed by whey cream separation, reverse osmosis (RO), evaporation, and spray drying to obtain 96% TS dry whey. The separation of whey cream is done by a 30HP centrifugal separator (TetraPak 2003). RO is assumed to require 10 kWh of electricity to remove 1,000 liters of water (Fellows 2009). Evaporation energy is assumed for a 5 stage evaporator using thermal vapor recompression (TVR) that consumes 0.2 kg of steam for each kg of water removed (UNEP 2000). A two-stage spray dryer is assumed to use 4.3 MJ kg-1 of water removed (Ramirez et al. 2006; Fellows 2009). The electricity and thermal energy requirements for cleaning operations are obtained from Ramirez et al. (2006). Cleaning of the pasteurizer is assumed to be 20% of total pasteurization energy; cleaning of the evaporator is 44% of the total evaporation energy, and cleaning of the spray drier is 18% of the total drying energy. The energy requirements for cleaning are assumed to be 20% from electricity and 80% from thermal energy. Packaging is assumed to require 1.5% of the total whey processing energy in the form of electricity (Ramirez et al. 2006).

The values for the embedded energy of natural gas, electricity, diesel, CaCl2, salt, HNO, NaOH, HFC-134a, and NH3 production are obtained from the Eco-Invent Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) library. A GaBi module representing Wisconsin's generation matrix is used (EIA 2010) for electrical energy inputs. The input energy needs to produce one MJ of output energy is 2.9 MJ for electricity, 1.2 MJ for thermal energy, and 1.14 MJ for diesel (GaBi 2008). Diesel consumption to haul and land spread sweet and salty whey in the single-output system is 0.005 L kg-1 cheese and 0.0004 L kg cheese respectively, according to typical state whey transportation distances (Byrne 2012).

26: <header>3.3.5 Handling of Co-Products </header>

Cheese production is analyzed as a single-output system with whey being land-spread as waste, and as a multi-functional system with cheese as the main product and dry whey and whey cream as co-products. In the multi-functional system, some of the unit processes are unique to individual products and others are common to all three products (fig. 3). Assigning GHG emissions and EI burdens to unique unit processes is straightforward, but there are a number of ways that inputs can be allocated for the common processes.
The following allocation and physical relationship subdivision strategies to assign GHG emissions and EI to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream are examined.
27: <header>Handling Whey as a Waste (SINGLE-O): No Allocation needed </header>

This system does not require an allocation strategy as it considers cheese as the only product; therefore, all environmental burdens are assigned to cheese. This is a significant practice of small cheese plants due to cost of entry into whey manufacture. The unit processes that are considered in this system are: fluid milk transportation, reception and storage of fluid milk, milk pasteurization, make vats, cheese cheddaring, cheese packaging and storage, and land-spreading sweet and salty whey.
28: <header>Multi-functional System </header>
System Subdivision (SUB): Avoiding Allocation
This method avoids allocation by subdividing those unit processes unique to dry whey and whey cream and attributing their related environmental burdens to each of those co-products.
After the subdivision step, GHG emissions and EI of the unit processes unique to cheese and all the common processes are attributed to the main product, cheese (fig. 4).
Whole-system Allocation Ratios (AR): Applying Allocation

This method allocates the environmental burdens aggregated across all unit processes using three different allocation ratios (AR); therefore, there are three allocation cases for this method based on: Total Solids (TS), Nutritional Content (NC), which is expressed as the sum of Kcal contained in fat, protein, and carbohydrates, and Economic Value (EV) according to equation 2 and table 3.
NCx (2) NCc + NCdw + NCwc
Where NC can be replaced by TS and EV and subscripts indicate:
C = cheese dw = dry whey WC = whey cream X = desired product: c, dw, or WC
The rationale for the allocation ratios presented in table 3 is as follows:

Total Solids (ARTs): TS are the sum of total protein, fat, carbohydrates, and ash contained in an end product (cheese, dry whey, and whey cream) as compared to the raw material (fluid milk). Therefore, 50.1% of the milk solids are contained in the cheese, 47.3% in the dry whey, and 2.6% in the whey cream (table 1). This is an integral tracking system ubiquitous within the dairy processing industry.

Nutritional Content (ARNC): The specific caloric energy factors for dairy products for this allocation procedure are: 4.27 Kcal g-1 of protein, 8.79 Kcal g-1 of fat and 3.87 Kcal g-1 of carbohydrates (Schakel et al 2009). The caloric energies are related to the mass of each macronutrient component (protein, fat, and carbohydrates) contained in cheese, dry whey, and whey cream (table 1). NC allocation ratios of 68.8% are obtained for the cheese, 27.2% for the dry whey, and 4.1% for the whey cream.

Economic Value (AREV): This value is dynamic and depends on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange price for cheese, whey, and butter. We assume prices of 3.5 $ kg-1 for cheddar cheese, 3.7 $ kg for butter, and 0.5 $ kg for whey, reflective of current cheese yield and revenue potential in U.S. dairy industry. The whey cream butterfat component value is two thirds of the sweet cream market value based on estimation of reported market quality discounts between sellers and buyers. The resulting allocation factors of 87.5%, 9.3%, and 3.2% are calculated for cheese, dry whey, and whey cream respectively.

31: <header>Subdivision + Allocation Ratios (SUB+AR): Applying Allocation </header>

This method uses the subdivision technique (SUB) to differentiate those unit processes unique to each product from those processes common to all products (fig. 5). The allocation ratios for TS, NC and EV described in AR are then applied to all of the common processes (SUB+AR); as a result, there are three allocation cases for this method (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, and SUB+AREV). The goal of this "common + unique" method is to provide a more precise estimation of the environmental impacts associated with each product of the dairy plant system.
Table 4 presents a summary of the four methods used to handle co-products in this study.
32: <header>3.4 Results </header>

Even though the single-output system does not produce any co-products, GHG emissions and EI are analyzed for comparison as it is representative of many small dairy farms of WI. The results are presented according to the complexity of the methods to handle co-product allocation: no allocation, avoiding allocation by subdivision, applying allocation ratios to the whole system, combing sub-division and allocation ratios.

All of the results presented in this paper are expressed in terms of the functional unit of 1 kg of cheddar cheese. In the multi-functional system, these results can be divided by 0.61 to obtain impact results for one kg of dry whey, and divided by 0.1 to obtain results per kg of whey cream. The reader should note that this is a gate-to-gate study SO that GHG emissions and FEI impacts are limited to milk transportation from the farm, production of inputs, and processing activities at the dairy plant and do not include the production of milk at the farm level.
33: <header>3.4.6 Energy Intensity </header>

The EI of the single-output system (SINGLE-O) is 11.92 MJ kg-1 cheese, with liquid whey being land-spread as waste, thus eliminating the dry whey and whey cream processes from the analysis. EI for land-spreading sweet and salty whey is 0.238 MJ kg cheese. In this system, the most EI process is milk pasteurization (55%), followed by cheese packaging and storage (21%), and milk reception (8%). Electricity represents 46% of total EI, followed by thermal energy 37%, and energy required for the production of input materials 18%.

The total EI of the multi-functional system (total energy for packaged cheddar cheese, dry whey, and whey cream) is 22.3 MJ kg cheese (table 5). Net FEI represents the majority of net EI (99.5%) since only 7% of renewable power is used in the Wisconsin electricity matrix (EIA, 2010). Thermal energy represents 53% of the total EI, electricity 38%, and energy required for the production of input materials 9%. The processes unique to dry whey represent 47% of total EI and are dominated by natural gas; processes unique to cheese represent 11% and are dominated by electricity as well as those processes unique to whey cream that represent 1% of EI; common processes represent 41% of total EI and are equally dominated by electricity and thermal energy. Milk pasteurization (30%), whey evaporation (17%) and whey drying (16%) are the most energy intense processes, followed by cheese packaging and storage (11%), and whey pasteurization (10%). Direct energy use in table 2 does not equate to EI in table 5 because of differences in the conversion efficiency, embedded energy inputs (for the production of natural gas, diesel, and electricity), and distribution losses for electricity and natural gas.

Processes related to dry whey and whey cream production are present in the multi-functional system and require a method to assign their environmental impacts. In the subdivision (SUB) method, the EI of dry whey is estimated to be 10.52 MJ kg cheese, which accounts for only those processes unique to dry whey. The EI for whey cream is 0.09 MJ kg) -1 cheese, which only corresponds to processes unique to whey cream processing. Even though SUB and SINGLE-O analyze different systems, it can be seen that the EI for both of them is closely similar, being the EI for SUB lower as it does not include the energy related to hauling and disposing sweet and salty whey.
Allocation methods AR and SUB+AR produce a wide range of EI values for cheese, dry whey, and whey cream across their three allocation cases (TS, NC, and EV). The whole-system

allocation ratios method (AR) place a higher impact burden on cheese than the method that combines subdivision and allocation (SUB+AR). AR assigns a portion of the total EI to each product even if some processes are not related to that product as it is based on a whole plant allocation approach. Therefore, AR burdens those processes that are less energy intense (e.g. cheddaring and make vats) with the impacts of those processes that are energy intense (e.g. whey evaporation and drying). SUB+AR first subdivides those processes unique to each product and then applies allocation ratios based on TS, NC, and EV only to those processes common to all three products. The allocation cases for both AR and SUB+AR place increasing burden on cheese in the following order TS, NC, and EV. The EI results for the SINGLE-O system and the various methods for the multi-functional system are presented in table 7.

37: <header>3.4.7 Greenhouse Gas Emissions </header>

GHG emissions of the SINGLE-O system are 0.8 kg CO2-eq kg cheese. Hauling and disposal of sweet and salty whey represent 2% of this quantity. Electrical energy is the largest contributor to this impact category (51%), followed by thermal energy (35%) and the production of material inputs (14%). The contributions to GHG emissions by unit process follow the same trend as in EI: milk pasteurization 57%, cheese packaging and cold storage 17%, and milk reception 9%.

Total GHG emissions to manufacture 1 kg of packaged cheddar cheese, 0.615 kg of dry whey, and 0.1 kg of whey cream in the multi-functional system are 1.51 kg CO2-eq, where 94.5% of these emissions come from CO2, 4.3% from CH4, 0.7% from N2O, and 0.5% from HFC-134a. Thermal energy represents 50%, electrical energy 42%, and energy related to the production of material and other inputs 8% of total emissions (table 6). Processes unique to dry whey represent 48%, process unique to whey cream 1%, common processes 42%, and processes
unique to cheese 9% of total GHG emissions. Milk pasteurization, evaporation and drying are the largest contributors to GHG emissions at 31%, 17% and 16% respectively.

The results of the four methods to handle co-products produced the same trends for GHG emissions as for EI with some methods assessing the majority of the emissions to cheese and others to dry whey (fig. 6).

39: <header>3.5 Discussion </header>

The choice of method in the multi-functional system has a substantial influence on the estimates of both EI and GHG emissions. EI ranges from 7.1 MJ kg of cheese to 19.5 MJ kg-1 of cheese produced. Guinard et al. (2009) used LCA techniques and applied economic allocation to arrive at an EI value of 6.7 MJ kg-1 cheese produced. Berlin (2002) reported a life-cycle EI value of 7.5 MJ kg` cheese using a 68% economic allocation ratio for a multi-functional system that produced cream, whey, whey powder, and milk powder. Our estimate using an economic allocation ratio is more than double these values, while our estimate using subdivision combined with economic allocation is about 50% higher than these values.

The value reported by Ramirez et al. (2006), 4.3 MJ kg -1 cheese, was an average of country level data and reflected only primary energy consumed to produce cheese. Xu et al. (2009) reported U.S. average end-use energy values that ranged from 8.4 MJ kg` cheese to 9.6 MJ kg-1 cheese considering national production of all cheese varieties; however, end-use energy values ranged from 2.3 MJ kg` cheese to 16.8 MJ kg` -1 cheese when analyzing individual cheese plants. We would expect our EI values to be higher than those calculated for end-use energy alone. This is indeed the case, as our end-use energy for cheese production in the SINGLE-O system, is 5.7 MJ kg-1 cheese compared to an EI value of 11.9 MJ kg-1 of cheese. Allocated end-use energy to

produce one kg of cheese in the multi-functional system according to the different methods in this study are: 5.6 MJ kg- cheese for the SUB method; 6.5 MJ kg-1 cheese, 9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 11.5 MJ kg-1 cheese for the AR method using TS, NC, and EV respectively; and 2.8 MJ kg-1 cheese, 3.9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 5 MJ kg cheese for the SUB+AR method using TS, NC, EV respectively. The results obtained with the SUB+AR method are comparable to the results obtained by Ramirez et al. (2006) and represent an efficient cheese plant of the Xu et al. (2009) study, which is representative of this modeled scenario that considers best practices for current technology in Wisconsin.

The GHG emission estimates for cheese manufacture from Berlin (2002) 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg- of cheese, and Guinard et al. (2009) 0.40 kg CO2-eq kg- cheese are about half of our estimates in the SINGLE-O system SUB method (0.78 kg CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), about one third of our highest estimate using FAR (1.3 CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), and comparable to our estimate using the SUB+ARTS (0.46 CO2-eq kg cheese). In general, the SUB+AR GHG emissions estimations (no matter the type of allocation ratio) are closer to the values reported in Berlin (2002) and Guinard et al. (2009) than the rest of methods.

It was not possible to determine the exact allocation methods used in some previous studies. Since these methods have an important influence on the results, a detailed accounting of the methodology would be useful to the LCA practitioner. Variations in EI and GHG emissions are also influenced by cheese yield, and the final solids and moisture content of the type of cheese being made. SUB+AR provides a method to account for different solids content and unit process energy consumption through the cheese/whey processing cycle, which can provide a basis for a more reliable comparison of environmental impact assessments across cheese plants. There are more advanced technologies for evaporating and spray drying whey than the ones considered in

our study, but they are not typical of current practice in Wisconsin. These processes are major contributors to EI and GHG emissions and differences in them will introduce variation in estimates. Although we have similar estimates of direct energy use as other studies, the ratio of electrical to thermal energy for cheese processing is greater in our study. Since electricity carries more embedded energy and GHG emissions than thermal energy, higher GHG emission estimates are obtained in our study.

Calculating the environmental burden of cheese production is straightforward if the whey is treated as waste (SINGLE-O system), as is the case for many small cheese plants in Wisconsin due to high investments needed to enter the whey processing market. However, whey products have become an important part of the dairy portfolio, and many large dairy plants process whey into some form of food product. This study shows the extreme range of estimates of GHG emissions and EI, for cheese and whey manufacturing, resulting from different methods that handle multi-functionality. The method choice is clearly important in the assignment of environmental burdens to these dairy products. Furthermore, it is important to take into account the method used in order to compare results across studies.

ISO (2006a) recommends avoiding allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes in sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products. System expansion is not practical for whey products as there are a great number of sources of protein and carbohydrates that can be replaced by whey, depending on the specific application as a food ingredient. In addition, there is no alternative method to produce whey or whey cream other than cheese making. If whey and whey cream are not accounted for within our process boundaries, they must be treated as waste products; however, these products do have substantial economic and nutritional value.

We apply three different methods (SUB, AR, and SUB+AR) that produce very different results when partitioning the environmental impacts of our multi-functional system. Subdivision (SUB) assigns all of the processes unique to dry whey and whey cream to the whey product with no reduction in the burdens assigned to cheese for processes common to both products. Allocation ratios (AR) identify physical relationships between the multiple products to partition the environmental impacts for all of the manufacturing operations combined. The SUB+AR method uses a more sophisticated strategy of identifying processes that are common to all products and those that are unique to each product. Subdivision is applied as an alternative method to system expansion to avoid allocation; however, cheese and whey have shared resource inputs from the farm gate through to the separation of the cheese curds and whey; therefore, allocation cannot be avoided. SUB+AR apportion these common processes according to physical characteristics of each product and attribute the environmental burden of the unique processes to each individual end product.

The combination of subdivision with allocation ratios is a rational and accurate approach to partition environmental impacts of multi-functional dairy systems, where subdivision is not precise (processes cannot be entirely subdivided), and allocation ratios produce significant variability (due to the existence of energy intense unit process unique to a single co-product). Even though the choice of allocation ratio (TS, NC, and EV) produces variability within AR and SUB+AR, this variability is less significant in SUB+AR. This reduction provides less need to qualify the environmental impact based on the type of allocation ratio used. The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. For example, recent studies have investigated additional processes such as fractionation of whey components

(whey protein concentrate, lactose and dairy mineral), as well as the use of unprocessed whey as an animal feed or as an energy feedstock (Spajie et al. 2010; Kavacik and Topaloglu 2010). Economic allocation is widely used in the LCA literature because it does not require an understanding of the physical processes involved with dairy product manufacture and economic data are readily available. Economic allocation is, however, recognized as the least accurate and desirable allocation strategy (ISO 2006a) as it could easily fluctuate according to different factors such as prices, demands, taxes, and subsidies of cheese (Feitz et al. 2007), and other products directly (e.g. dairy products) or indirectly (e.g. substitute and complementary goods) related to cheese. Furthermore, the value of food products across the global community is more closely related to its NC than its EV. More environmental burden is placed on cheese than on dry whey with EV allocation compared to the other allocation cases (NC and TS). This does not reflect the relative energy inputs required to process these two products. When applied to other product systems, such as ethanol-corn stover (Luo et al. 2009) and milk-beef (Cederberg and Stadig 2003), economic allocation also places a higher burden on the main product when compared to other allocation ratios. The choice of allocation based on TS, NC, and EV decisions has a larger influence in our study of cheese manufacturing when compared to other studies. Luo et al. (2009) used mass, economic value, and energy content as the basis for co-product allocation when considering first generation ethanol from corn, wheat, and sugar beet, and Curran (2007) applied mass, volume, and market value allocation ratios for corn ethanol.

Cederberg and Stadig (2003) avoided allocation in a milk and beef production system through system expansion and Luo et al. (2009) applied the same method for a corn stover-based ethanol system. Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) compared attributional and consequential LCA of milk production in the Netherlands by applying economic and mass allocation ratios in the former and

system expansion in the latter. Reap et al. (2008) described existing methodologies available in literature that dealt with allocation and the problems associated with them. As this last study is a literature review, no actual calculations were done. None of these studies applied the approach presented in this paper as system expansion avoids allocation and the studies that applied allocation used whole plant allocation ratios. Feitz et al. (2007) addressed the issue of allocation by developing a physico-chemical allocation matrix on a whole factory basis. However, this is an extensive subtraction/substitution method that can only be applied to multi-product dairy plants with similar products and technology levels.

GHG emissions of milk production in the U.S. were estimated as 1kg CO2-eq kg-1 of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) by Gerber et al. (2010) and Guinard et al. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.

The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. For example, recent studies have investigated additional processes such as fractionation of whey components

(whey protein concentrate, lactose and dairy mineral), as well as the use of unprocessed whey as an animal feed or as an energy feedstock (Spajie et al. 2010; Kavacik and Topaloglu 2010). Economic allocation is widely used in the LCA literature because it does not require an understanding of the physical processes involved with dairy product manufacture and economic data are readily available. Economic allocation is, however, recognized as the least accurate and desirable allocation strategy (ISO 2006a) as it could easily fluctuate according to different factors such as prices, demands, taxes, and subsidies of cheese (Feitz et al. 2007), and other products directly (e.g. dairy products) or indirectly (e.g. substitute and complementary goods) related to cheese. Furthermore, the value of food products across the global community is more closely related to its NC than its EV. More environmental burden is placed on cheese than on dry whey with EV allocation compared to the other allocation cases (NC and TS). This does not reflect the relative energy inputs required to process these two products. When applied to other product systems, such as ethanol-corn stover (Luo et al. 2009) and milk-beef (Cederberg and Stadig 2003), economic allocation also places a higher burden on the main product when compared to other allocation ratios. The choice of allocation based on TS, NC, and EV decisions has a larger influence in our study of cheese manufacturing when compared to other studies. Luo et al. (2009) used mass, economic value, and energy content as the basis for co-product allocation when considering first generation ethanol from corn, wheat, and sugar beet, and Curran (2007) applied mass, volume, and market value allocation ratios for corn ethanol.

Cederberg and Stadig (2003) avoided allocation in a milk and beef production system through system expansion and Luo et al. (2009) applied the same method for a corn stover-based ethanol system. Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) compared attributional and consequential LCA of milk production in the Netherlands by applying economic and mass allocation ratios in the former and

system expansion in the latter. Reap et al. (2008) described existing methodologies available in literature that dealt with allocation and the problems associated with them. As this last study is a literature review, no actual calculations were done. None of these studies applied the approach presented in this paper as system expansion avoids allocation and the studies that applied allocation used whole plant allocation ratios. Feitz et al. (2007) addressed the issue of allocation by developing a physico-chemical allocation matrix on a whole factory basis. However, this is an extensive subtraction/substitution method that can only be applied to multi-product dairy plants with similar products and technology levels.

GHG emissions of milk production in the U.S. were estimated as 1kg CO2-eq kg-1 of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) by Gerber et al. (2010) and Guinard et al. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.
41: <header>3.5 Discussion </header>

The choice of method in the multi-functional system has a substantial influence on the estimates of both EI and GHG emissions. EI ranges from 7.1 MJ kg of cheese to 19.5 MJ kg-1 of cheese produced. Guinard et al. (2009) used LCA techniques and applied economic allocation to arrive at an EI value of 6.7 MJ kg-1 cheese produced. Berlin (2002) reported a life-cycle EI value of 7.5 MJ kg` cheese using a 68% economic allocation ratio for a multi-functional system that produced cream, whey, whey powder, and milk powder. Our estimate using an economic allocation ratio is more than double these values, while our estimate using subdivision combined with economic allocation is about 50% higher than these values.

The value reported by Ramirez et al. (2006), 4.3 MJ kg -1 cheese, was an average of country level data and reflected only primary energy consumed to produce cheese. Xu et al. (2009) reported U.S. average end-use energy values that ranged from 8.4 MJ kg` cheese to 9.6 MJ kg-1 cheese considering national production of all cheese varieties; however, end-use energy values ranged from 2.3 MJ kg` cheese to 16.8 MJ kg` -1 cheese when analyzing individual cheese plants. We would expect our EI values to be higher than those calculated for end-use energy alone. This is indeed the case, as our end-use energy for cheese production in the SINGLE-O system, is 5.7 MJ kg-1 cheese compared to an EI value of 11.9 MJ kg-1 of cheese. Allocated end-use energy to

produce one kg of cheese in the multi-functional system according to the different methods in this study are: 5.6 MJ kg- cheese for the SUB method; 6.5 MJ kg-1 cheese, 9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 11.5 MJ kg-1 cheese for the AR method using TS, NC, and EV respectively; and 2.8 MJ kg-1 cheese, 3.9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 5 MJ kg cheese for the SUB+AR method using TS, NC, EV respectively. The results obtained with the SUB+AR method are comparable to the results obtained by Ramirez et al. (2006) and represent an efficient cheese plant of the Xu et al. (2009) study, which is representative of this modeled scenario that considers best practices for current technology in Wisconsin.

The GHG emission estimates for cheese manufacture from Berlin (2002) 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg- of cheese, and Guinard et al. (2009) 0.40 kg CO2-eq kg- cheese are about half of our estimates in the SINGLE-O system SUB method (0.78 kg CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), about one third of our highest estimate using FAR (1.3 CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), and comparable to our estimate using the SUB+ARTS (0.46 CO2-eq kg cheese). In general, the SUB+AR GHG emissions estimations (no matter the type of allocation ratio) are closer to the values reported in Berlin (2002) and Guinard et al. (2009) than the rest of methods.

It was not possible to determine the exact allocation methods used in some previous studies. Since these methods have an important influence on the results, a detailed accounting of the methodology would be useful to the LCA practitioner. Variations in EI and GHG emissions are also influenced by cheese yield, and the final solids and moisture content of the type of cheese being made. SUB+AR provides a method to account for different solids content and unit process energy consumption through the cheese/whey processing cycle, which can provide a basis for a more reliable comparison of environmental impact assessments across cheese plants. There are more advanced technologies for evaporating and spray drying whey than the ones considered in

our study, but they are not typical of current practice in Wisconsin. These processes are major contributors to EI and GHG emissions and differences in them will introduce variation in estimates. Although we have similar estimates of direct energy use as other studies, the ratio of electrical to thermal energy for cheese processing is greater in our study. Since electricity carries more embedded energy and GHG emissions than thermal energy, higher GHG emission estimates are obtained in our study.

Calculating the environmental burden of cheese production is straightforward if the whey is treated as waste (SINGLE-O system), as is the case for many small cheese plants in Wisconsin due to high investments needed to enter the whey processing market. However, whey products have become an important part of the dairy portfolio, and many large dairy plants process whey into some form of food product. This study shows the extreme range of estimates of GHG emissions and EI, for cheese and whey manufacturing, resulting from different methods that handle multi-functionality. The method choice is clearly important in the assignment of environmental burdens to these dairy products. Furthermore, it is important to take into account the method used in order to compare results across studies.

ISO (2006a) recommends avoiding allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes in sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products. System expansion is not practical for whey products as there are a great number of sources of protein and carbohydrates that can be replaced by whey, depending on the specific application as a food ingredient. In addition, there is no alternative method to produce whey or whey cream other than cheese making. If whey and whey cream are not accounted for within our process boundaries, they must be treated as waste products; however, these products do have substantial economic and nutritional value.

We apply three different methods (SUB, AR, and SUB+AR) that produce very different results when partitioning the environmental impacts of our multi-functional system. Subdivision (SUB) assigns all of the processes unique to dry whey and whey cream to the whey product with no reduction in the burdens assigned to cheese for processes common to both products. Allocation ratios (AR) identify physical relationships between the multiple products to partition the environmental impacts for all of the manufacturing operations combined. The SUB+AR method uses a more sophisticated strategy of identifying processes that are common to all products and those that are unique to each product. Subdivision is applied as an alternative method to system expansion to avoid allocation; however, cheese and whey have shared resource inputs from the farm gate through to the separation of the cheese curds and whey; therefore, allocation cannot be avoided. SUB+AR apportion these common processes according to physical characteristics of each product and attribute the environmental burden of the unique processes to each individual end product.

The combination of subdivision with allocation ratios is a rational and accurate approach to partition environmental impacts of multi-functional dairy systems, where subdivision is not precise (processes cannot be entirely subdivided), and allocation ratios produce significant variability (due to the existence of energy intense unit process unique to a single co-product). Even though the choice of allocation ratio (TS, NC, and EV) produces variability within AR and SUB+AR, this variability is less significant in SUB+AR. This reduction provides less need to qualify the environmental impact based on the type of allocation ratio used. The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.
44: <header>3.5 Discussion </header>

The choice of method in the multi-functional system has a substantial influence on the estimates of both EI and GHG emissions. EI ranges from 7.1 MJ kg of cheese to 19.5 MJ kg-1 of cheese produced. Guinard et al. (2009) used LCA techniques and applied economic allocation to arrive at an EI value of 6.7 MJ kg-1 cheese produced. Berlin (2002) reported a life-cycle EI value of 7.5 MJ kg` cheese using a 68% economic allocation ratio for a multi-functional system that produced cream, whey, whey powder, and milk powder. Our estimate using an economic allocation ratio is more than double these values, while our estimate using subdivision combined with economic allocation is about 50% higher than these values.

The value reported by Ramirez et al. (2006), 4.3 MJ kg -1 cheese, was an average of country level data and reflected only primary energy consumed to produce cheese. Xu et al. (2009) reported U.S. average end-use energy values that ranged from 8.4 MJ kg` cheese to 9.6 MJ kg-1 cheese considering national production of all cheese varieties; however, end-use energy values ranged from 2.3 MJ kg` cheese to 16.8 MJ kg` -1 cheese when analyzing individual cheese plants. We would expect our EI values to be higher than those calculated for end-use energy alone. This is indeed the case, as our end-use energy for cheese production in the SINGLE-O system, is 5.7 MJ kg-1 cheese compared to an EI value of 11.9 MJ kg-1 of cheese. Allocated end-use energy to

produce one kg of cheese in the multi-functional system according to the different methods in this study are: 5.6 MJ kg- cheese for the SUB method; 6.5 MJ kg-1 cheese, 9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 11.5 MJ kg-1 cheese for the AR method using TS, NC, and EV respectively; and 2.8 MJ kg-1 cheese, 3.9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 5 MJ kg cheese for the SUB+AR method using TS, NC, EV respectively. The results obtained with the SUB+AR method are comparable to the results obtained by Ramirez et al. (2006) and represent an efficient cheese plant of the Xu et al. (2009) study, which is representative of this modeled scenario that considers best practices for current technology in Wisconsin.

The GHG emission estimates for cheese manufacture from Berlin (2002) 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg- of cheese, and Guinard et al. (2009) 0.40 kg CO2-eq kg- cheese are about half of our estimates in the SINGLE-O system SUB method (0.78 kg CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), about one third of our highest estimate using FAR (1.3 CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), and comparable to our estimate using the SUB+ARTS (0.46 CO2-eq kg cheese). In general, the SUB+AR GHG emissions estimations (no matter the type of allocation ratio) are closer to the values reported in Berlin (2002) and Guinard et al. (2009) than the rest of methods.

It was not possible to determine the exact allocation methods used in some previous studies. Since these methods have an important influence on the results, a detailed accounting of the methodology would be useful to the LCA practitioner. Variations in EI and GHG emissions are also influenced by cheese yield, and the final solids and moisture content of the type of cheese being made. SUB+AR provides a method to account for different solids content and unit process energy consumption through the cheese/whey processing cycle, which can provide a basis for a more reliable comparison of environmental impact assessments across cheese plants. There are more advanced technologies for evaporating and spray drying whey than the ones considered in

our study, but they are not typical of current practice in Wisconsin. These processes are major contributors to EI and GHG emissions and differences in them will introduce variation in estimates. Although we have similar estimates of direct energy use as other studies, the ratio of electrical to thermal energy for cheese processing is greater in our study. Since electricity carries more embedded energy and GHG emissions than thermal energy, higher GHG emission estimates are obtained in our study.

Calculating the environmental burden of cheese production is straightforward if the whey is treated as waste (SINGLE-O system), as is the case for many small cheese plants in Wisconsin due to high investments needed to enter the whey processing market. However, whey products have become an important part of the dairy portfolio, and many large dairy plants process whey into some form of food product. This study shows the extreme range of estimates of GHG emissions and EI, for cheese and whey manufacturing, resulting from different methods that handle multi-functionality. The method choice is clearly important in the assignment of environmental burdens to these dairy products. Furthermore, it is important to take into account the method used in order to compare results across studies.

ISO (2006a) recommends avoiding allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes in sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products. System expansion is not practical for whey products as there are a great number of sources of protein and carbohydrates that can be replaced by whey, depending on the specific application as a food ingredient. In addition, there is no alternative method to produce whey or whey cream other than cheese making. If whey and whey cream are not accounted for within our process boundaries, they must be treated as waste products; however, these products do have substantial economic and nutritional value.

We apply three different methods (SUB, AR, and SUB+AR) that produce very different results when partitioning the environmental impacts of our multi-functional system. Subdivision (SUB) assigns all of the processes unique to dry whey and whey cream to the whey product with no reduction in the burdens assigned to cheese for processes common to both products. Allocation ratios (AR) identify physical relationships between the multiple products to partition the environmental impacts for all of the manufacturing operations combined. The SUB+AR method uses a more sophisticated strategy of identifying processes that are common to all products and those that are unique to each product. Subdivision is applied as an alternative method to system expansion to avoid allocation; however, cheese and whey have shared resource inputs from the farm gate through to the separation of the cheese curds and whey; therefore, allocation cannot be avoided. SUB+AR apportion these common processes according to physical characteristics of each product and attribute the environmental burden of the unique processes to each individual end product.

The combination of subdivision with allocation ratios is a rational and accurate approach to partition environmental impacts of multi-functional dairy systems, where subdivision is not precise (processes cannot be entirely subdivided), and allocation ratios produce significant variability (due to the existence of energy intense unit process unique to a single co-product). Even though the choice of allocation ratio (TS, NC, and EV) produces variability within AR and SUB+AR, this variability is less significant in SUB+AR. This reduction provides less need to qualify the environmental impact based on the type of allocation ratio used. The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. For example, recent studies have investigated additional processes such as fractionation of whey components

(whey protein concentrate, lactose and dairy mineral), as well as the use of unprocessed whey as an animal feed or as an energy feedstock (Spajie et al. 2010; Kavacik and Topaloglu 2010). Economic allocation is widely used in the LCA literature because it does not require an understanding of the physical processes involved with dairy product manufacture and economic data are readily available. Economic allocation is, however, recognized as the least accurate and desirable allocation strategy (ISO 2006a) as it could easily fluctuate according to different factors such as prices, demands, taxes, and subsidies of cheese (Feitz et al. 2007), and other products directly (e.g. dairy products) or indirectly (e.g. substitute and complementary goods) related to cheese. Furthermore, the value of food products across the global community is more closely related to its NC than its EV. More environmental burden is placed on cheese than on dry whey with EV allocation compared to the other allocation cases (NC and TS). This does not reflect the relative energy inputs required to process these two products. When applied to other product systems, such as ethanol-corn stover (Luo et al. 2009) and milk-beef (Cederberg and Stadig 2003), economic allocation also places a higher burden on the main product when compared to other allocation ratios. The choice of allocation based on TS, NC, and EV decisions has a larger influence in our study of cheese manufacturing when compared to other studies. Luo et al. (2009) used mass, economic value, and energy content as the basis for co-product allocation when considering first generation ethanol from corn, wheat, and sugar beet, and Curran (2007) applied mass, volume, and market value allocation ratios for corn ethanol.

Cederberg and Stadig (2003) avoided allocation in a milk and beef production system through system expansion and Luo et al. (2009) applied the same method for a corn stover-based ethanol system. Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) compared attributional and consequential LCA of milk production in the Netherlands by applying economic and mass allocation ratios in the former and

system expansion in the latter. Reap et al. (2008) described existing methodologies available in literature that dealt with allocation and the problems associated with them. As this last study is a literature review, no actual calculations were done. None of these studies applied the approach presented in this paper as system expansion avoids allocation and the studies that applied allocation used whole plant allocation ratios. Feitz et al. (2007) addressed the issue of allocation by developing a physico-chemical allocation matrix on a whole factory basis. However, this is an extensive subtraction/substitution method that can only be applied to multi-product dairy plants with similar products and technology levels.

GHG emissions of milk production in the U.S. were estimated as 1kg CO2-eq kg-1 of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) by Gerber et al. (2010) and Guinard et al. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.

The GHG emission estimates for cheese manufacture from Berlin (2002) 0.44 kg CO2-eq kg- of cheese, and Guinard et al. (2009) 0.40 kg CO2-eq kg- cheese are about half of our estimates in the SINGLE-O system SUB method (0.78 kg CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), about one third of our highest estimate using FAR (1.3 CO2-eq kg-1 cheese), and comparable to our estimate using the SUB+ARTS (0.46 CO2-eq kg cheese). In general, the SUB+AR GHG emissions estimations (no matter the type of allocation ratio) are closer to the values reported in Berlin (2002) and Guinard et al. (2009) than the rest of methods.

It was not possible to determine the exact allocation methods used in some previous studies. Since these methods have an important influence on the results, a detailed accounting of the methodology would be useful to the LCA practitioner. Variations in EI and GHG emissions are also influenced by cheese yield, and the final solids and moisture content of the type of cheese being made. SUB+AR provides a method to account for different solids content and unit process energy consumption through the cheese/whey processing cycle, which can provide a basis for a more reliable comparison of environmental impact assessments across cheese plants. There are more advanced technologies for evaporating and spray drying whey than the ones considered in

our study, but they are not typical of current practice in Wisconsin. These processes are major contributors to EI and GHG emissions and differences in them will introduce variation in estimates. Although we have similar estimates of direct energy use as other studies, the ratio of electrical to thermal energy for cheese processing is greater in our study. Since electricity carries more embedded energy and GHG emissions than thermal energy, higher GHG emission estimates are obtained in our study.

Calculating the environmental burden of cheese production is straightforward if the whey is treated as waste (SINGLE-O system), as is the case for many small cheese plants in Wisconsin due to high investments needed to enter the whey processing market. However, whey products have become an important part of the dairy portfolio, and many large dairy plants process whey into some form of food product. This study shows the extreme range of estimates of GHG emissions and EI, for cheese and whey manufacturing, resulting from different methods that handle multi-functionality. The method choice is clearly important in the assignment of environmental burdens to these dairy products. Furthermore, it is important to take into account the method used in order to compare results across studies.

ISO (2006a) recommends avoiding allocation wherever possible by dividing unit processes in sub processes, or using system expansion to include the co-products. System expansion is not practical for whey products as there are a great number of sources of protein and carbohydrates that can be replaced by whey, depending on the specific application as a food ingredient. In addition, there is no alternative method to produce whey or whey cream other than cheese making. If whey and whey cream are not accounted for within our process boundaries, they must be treated as waste products; however, these products do have substantial economic and nutritional value.

We apply three different methods (SUB, AR, and SUB+AR) that produce very different results when partitioning the environmental impacts of our multi-functional system. Subdivision (SUB) assigns all of the processes unique to dry whey and whey cream to the whey product with no reduction in the burdens assigned to cheese for processes common to both products. Allocation ratios (AR) identify physical relationships between the multiple products to partition the environmental impacts for all of the manufacturing operations combined. The SUB+AR method uses a more sophisticated strategy of identifying processes that are common to all products and those that are unique to each product. Subdivision is applied as an alternative method to system expansion to avoid allocation; however, cheese and whey have shared resource inputs from the farm gate through to the separation of the cheese curds and whey; therefore, allocation cannot be avoided. SUB+AR apportion these common processes according to physical characteristics of each product and attribute the environmental burden of the unique processes to each individual end product.

The combination of subdivision with allocation ratios is a rational and accurate approach to partition environmental impacts of multi-functional dairy systems, where subdivision is not precise (processes cannot be entirely subdivided), and allocation ratios produce significant variability (due to the existence of energy intense unit process unique to a single co-product). Even though the choice of allocation ratio (TS, NC, and EV) produces variability within AR and SUB+AR, this variability is less significant in SUB+AR. This reduction provides less need to qualify the environmental impact based on the type of allocation ratio used. The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. For example, recent studies have investigated additional processes such as fractionation of whey components

(whey protein concentrate, lactose and dairy mineral), as well as the use of unprocessed whey as an animal feed or as an energy feedstock (Spajie et al. 2010; Kavacik and Topaloglu 2010). Economic allocation is widely used in the LCA literature because it does not require an understanding of the physical processes involved with dairy product manufacture and economic data are readily available. Economic allocation is, however, recognized as the least accurate and desirable allocation strategy (ISO 2006a) as it could easily fluctuate according to different factors such as prices, demands, taxes, and subsidies of cheese (Feitz et al. 2007), and other products directly (e.g. dairy products) or indirectly (e.g. substitute and complementary goods) related to cheese. Furthermore, the value of food products across the global community is more closely related to its NC than its EV. More environmental burden is placed on cheese than on dry whey with EV allocation compared to the other allocation cases (NC and TS). This does not reflect the relative energy inputs required to process these two products. When applied to other product systems, such as ethanol-corn stover (Luo et al. 2009) and milk-beef (Cederberg and Stadig 2003), economic allocation also places a higher burden on the main product when compared to other allocation ratios. The choice of allocation based on TS, NC, and EV decisions has a larger influence in our study of cheese manufacturing when compared to other studies. Luo et al. (2009) used mass, economic value, and energy content as the basis for co-product allocation when considering first generation ethanol from corn, wheat, and sugar beet, and Curran (2007) applied mass, volume, and market value allocation ratios for corn ethanol.

Cederberg and Stadig (2003) avoided allocation in a milk and beef production system through system expansion and Luo et al. (2009) applied the same method for a corn stover-based ethanol system. Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) compared attributional and consequential LCA of milk production in the Netherlands by applying economic and mass allocation ratios in the former and

system expansion in the latter. Reap et al. (2008) described existing methodologies available in literature that dealt with allocation and the problems associated with them. As this last study is a literature review, no actual calculations were done. None of these studies applied the approach presented in this paper as system expansion avoids allocation and the studies that applied allocation used whole plant allocation ratios. Feitz et al. (2007) addressed the issue of allocation by developing a physico-chemical allocation matrix on a whole factory basis. However, this is an extensive subtraction/substitution method that can only be applied to multi-product dairy plants with similar products and technology levels.

GHG emissions of milk production in the U.S. were estimated as 1kg CO2-eq kg-1 of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) by Gerber et al. (2010) and Guinard et al. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.
48: <header>3.5 Discussion </header>

The choice of method in the multi-functional system has a substantial influence on the estimates of both EI and GHG emissions. EI ranges from 7.1 MJ kg of cheese to 19.5 MJ kg-1 of cheese produced. Guinard et al. (2009) used LCA techniques and applied economic allocation to arrive at an EI value of 6.7 MJ kg-1 cheese produced. Berlin (2002) reported a life-cycle EI value of 7.5 MJ kg` cheese using a 68% economic allocation ratio for a multi-functional system that produced cream, whey, whey powder, and milk powder. Our estimate using an economic allocation ratio is more than double these values, while our estimate using subdivision combined with economic allocation is about 50% higher than these values.

The value reported by Ramirez et al. (2006), 4.3 MJ kg -1 cheese, was an average of country level data and reflected only primary energy consumed to produce cheese. Xu et al. (2009) reported U.S. average end-use energy values that ranged from 8.4 MJ kg` cheese to 9.6 MJ kg-1 cheese considering national production of all cheese varieties; however, end-use energy values ranged from 2.3 MJ kg` cheese to 16.8 MJ kg` -1 cheese when analyzing individual cheese plants. We would expect our EI values to be higher than those calculated for end-use energy alone. This is indeed the case, as our end-use energy for cheese production in the SINGLE-O system, is 5.7 MJ kg-1 cheese compared to an EI value of 11.9 MJ kg-1 of cheese. Allocated end-use energy to

produce one kg of cheese in the multi-functional system according to the different methods in this study are: 5.6 MJ kg- cheese for the SUB method; 6.5 MJ kg-1 cheese, 9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 11.5 MJ kg-1 cheese for the AR method using TS, NC, and EV respectively; and 2.8 MJ kg-1 cheese, 3.9 MJ kg-1 cheese, and 5 MJ kg cheese for the SUB+AR method using TS, NC, EV respectively. The results obtained with the SUB+AR method are comparable to the results obtained by Ramirez et al. (2006) and represent an efficient cheese plant of the Xu et al. (2009) study, which is representative of this modeled scenario that considers best practices for current technology in Wisconsin.

In addition, there is no alternative method to produce whey or whey cream other than cheese making. If whey and whey cream are not accounted for within our process boundaries, they must be treated as waste products; however, these products do have substantial economic and nutritional value.

We apply three different methods (SUB, AR, and SUB+AR) that produce very different results when partitioning the environmental impacts of our multi-functional system. Subdivision (SUB) assigns all of the processes unique to dry whey and whey cream to the whey product with no reduction in the burdens assigned to cheese for processes common to both products. Allocation ratios (AR) identify physical relationships between the multiple products to partition the environmental impacts for all of the manufacturing operations combined. The SUB+AR method uses a more sophisticated strategy of identifying processes that are common to all products and those that are unique to each product. Subdivision is applied as an alternative method to system expansion to avoid allocation; however, cheese and whey have shared resource inputs from the farm gate through to the separation of the cheese curds and whey; therefore, allocation cannot be avoided. SUB+AR apportion these common processes according to physical characteristics of each product and attribute the environmental burden of the unique processes to each individual end product.

The combination of subdivision with allocation ratios is a rational and accurate approach to partition environmental impacts of multi-functional dairy systems, where subdivision is not precise (processes cannot be entirely subdivided), and allocation ratios produce significant variability (due to the existence of energy intense unit process unique to a single co-product). Even though the choice of allocation ratio (TS, NC, and EV) produces variability within AR and SUB+AR, this variability is less significant in SUB+AR. This reduction provides less need to qualify the environmental impact based on the type of allocation ratio used. The SUB+AR method can be replicated by other dairy and food production systems to obtain more precise environmental impact estimations when compared to whole-plant allocation ratios. For example, recent studies have investigated additional processes such as fractionation of whey components

(whey protein concentrate, lactose and dairy mineral), as well as the use of unprocessed whey as an animal feed or as an energy feedstock (Spajie et al. 2010; Kavacik and Topaloglu 2010). Economic allocation is widely used in the LCA literature because it does not require an understanding of the physical processes involved with dairy product manufacture and economic data are readily available. Economic allocation is, however, recognized as the least accurate and desirable allocation strategy (ISO 2006a) as it could easily fluctuate according to different factors such as prices, demands, taxes, and subsidies of cheese (Feitz et al. 2007), and other products directly (e.g. dairy products) or indirectly (e.g. substitute and complementary goods) related to cheese. Furthermore, the value of food products across the global community is more closely related to its NC than its EV. More environmental burden is placed on cheese than on dry whey with EV allocation compared to the other allocation cases (NC and TS). This does not reflect the relative energy inputs required to process these two products. When applied to other product systems, such as ethanol-corn stover (Luo et al. 2009) and milk-beef (Cederberg and Stadig 2003), economic allocation also places a higher burden on the main product when compared to other allocation ratios. The choice of allocation based on TS, NC, and EV decisions has a larger influence in our study of cheese manufacturing when compared to other studies. Luo et al. (2009) used mass, economic value, and energy content as the basis for co-product allocation when considering first generation ethanol from corn, wheat, and sugar beet, and Curran (2007) applied mass, volume, and market value allocation ratios for corn ethanol.

Cederberg and Stadig (2003) avoided allocation in a milk and beef production system through system expansion and Luo et al. (2009) applied the same method for a corn stover-based ethanol system. Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) compared attributional and consequential LCA of milk production in the Netherlands by applying economic and mass allocation ratios in the former and

system expansion in the latter. Reap et al. (2008) described existing methodologies available in literature that dealt with allocation and the problems associated with them. As this last study is a literature review, no actual calculations were done. None of these studies applied the approach presented in this paper as system expansion avoids allocation and the studies that applied allocation used whole plant allocation ratios. Feitz et al. (2007) addressed the issue of allocation by developing a physico-chemical allocation matrix on a whole factory basis. However, this is an extensive subtraction/substitution method that can only be applied to multi-product dairy plants with similar products and technology levels.

GHG emissions of milk production in the U.S. were estimated as 1kg CO2-eq kg-1 of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) by Gerber et al. (2010) and Guinard et al. (2010); as 0.37 to 0.69 kg of CO2-eq kg-Superscript(1) of energy corrected milk by Rotz et al. (2010), depending on the milk production system; and 0.91 to 1.61 kg CO2-eq kg- of FPCM by Thomassen and Dalgaard (2008) depending on the allocation strategy. This wide range of values is comparable to the range of our GHG emission estimates for 1 kg of cheddar cheese processing which varied in response to the allocation strategy used.
51: <header>3.6 Sensitivity Analysis </header>

A sensitivy analysis of the multi-functional system is performed by varying each of the inputs for individual unit operations by +/- 10% and the resulting change on the environmental impacts of EI and GHG emissions is calculated (figures 7 and figure 8, respectively).
Energy inputs to milk pasteurization, whey evaporation, whey drying, whey pasteurization, and cold storage of cheese are the most sensitive inputs to the process model indicating that the

quality of data on these unit operations is important to achieve accurate whole-plant GHG emissions and EI estimates. Our estimates for these inputs are based on field measurements reported in the literature wherever possible. These field measurements were validated by engineering calculations of the energy required for heat transfer operations along with a check on the reasonableness of the efficiencies of these processes.
52: <header>3.7 Conclusions </header>

We applied a number of methods that handle co-product allocation to an archetypal dairy plant in Wisconsin producing cheddar cheese, dried whey, and whey cream. The data presented provide detail on the inputs to the various unit processes in a dairy plant that can be used for the development of LCI and LCIA and applied in LCA systems that include the production of cheese and its co-products, dried whey, and whey cream. Energy inputs to milk pasteurization, whey evaporation, whey drying, whey pasteurization, and cold storage of cheese are the most sensitive inputs to the process model, indicating that the quality of data on these unit operations are important to achieve accurate GHG emission and EI estimates.

The choice of method has a large influence on the relative environmental burden assigned to cheese and whey. The methods for dealing with allocation decisions are based on the physical processes of pasteurization, cheese making, whey and whey cream separation, and whey drying, which occur in cheese plants that also process whey. This exercise is not an attempt to avoid allocation, but rather to apply allocation in a way that more accurately reflects the inputs and outputs from those processes associated with the main product and co-products. The various methods to handle co-product allocation examined in this study produce a range of estimates of GHG emissions of cheese from 0.46 CO2-eq kg-1 cheese (SUB+ARTS, process subdivision with

allocation based on TS) to 1.3 CO2-eq kg cheese (AREV, whole system allocation based on EV) or a ratio of almost 3:1. Differentiating the processes that are common and unique to each co- product reduces the effect of the choice of allocation method (TS, NC, or EV) by more than one half compared to whole plant allocation based on these same allocation ratios. ISO recommends avoiding allocation by system expansion or subdivision whenever possible. System expansion is difficult to apply and can introduce variability across studies, as there are many possible substitutes for whey protein and carbohydrates and no exact analog. Subdivision alone will not result in a reasonable attribution of the environmental burdens because there are many unit processes shared between the product and co-products. ISO recommends using allocation ratios if allocation cannot be avoided. The variability of results across whole-plant allocation ratios (ARTS, ARNC, and AREV) is considerable. The combination of subdivision and allocation ratios partitions the environmental burden of common processes to both the main product and the co- products, and provides an accurate accounting structure that can be applied to multiple co- product streams without undue complexity. This SUB+AR method reduces the environmental burden on cheese when whey co-products are produced. Furthermore, it reduces the variability introduced by the use of different allocation ratios (TS, NC, EV) when compared to whole-plant allocation. Allocation ratios based on physical properties (TS or NC) are preferred over economic ratios as the relative economic value of cheese and whey can be quite variable across time and region.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.

Table 4. Summary of the modeled methods to handle co-product allocation

Method to handle co- products|System|Decision|Description SINGLE-O|Single- output|No allocation|Cheese is the only product; whey is land spread as waste. SUB|Multi- functional|Subdivision|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. GHG emissions and EI are assigned to dry whey and whey cream only for processes unique to these co-products. Cheese is burdened with the remainder of plant energy use. AR (ARTS, ARNC, AREV)|Multi- functional|Whole system allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is applied to the whole cheese plant based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic value of the products. SUB+AR (SUB+ARTS, SUB+ARNC, SUB+AREV)|Multi- functional|Subdivision + allocation|Cheese, whey, and whey cream are produced. Allocation is done for the common processes (based on total solids, nutritional content, and economic values) and subdivision of energy consumption for the unique unit processes to cheese, dry whey, and whey cream.
