56: <header>4.6.8 Allocated Results </header>

Whole-system allocation and subdivision/allocation ratios are applied to net GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, and DFF. The same whole-system allocation rates are calculated to all sustainability indicators. As shown in Table 8, this approach adds significant variability to the model as it interchanges results in magnitude and direction depending on which allocation strategy (TS or EV) is applied to partition the environmental burdens among co-products of each pathway. For example, the TS strategy assigns 42% of the burdens to manure liquids in the SLS pathway, but when the EV strategy is applied, only 13% of the burdens are assigned to this same product. This same variability happens with the AD+SLS pathways, where 81% of the burdens are assigned to electricity with the EV strategy, but only 30% with the TS strategy.

Subdivision/allocation ratios are calculated for individual sustainability indicators. As shown in Table 9, the variability is significantly reduced when applying this approach, as the partitioning of environmental impacts among co-products is consistent no matter what allocation strategy is applied. With this approach, the contribution of manure liquids to GHG emissions is 90-91% in the SLS pathway and 67-68% in the AD+SLS pathway. Digestate contributes 81% to GHG emissions in the AD pathway. This same trend applies to NH3 emissions and DFF. As shown in Figure 4, the majority of net impacts are related to storage and land-application. The subdivision/allocation approach assigns the burdens of these specific unit-processes to the products that are responsible for them (i.e. digestate storage is not related to electricity production), avoiding overweighting of the environmental impacts to co-products.

This is one of the first LCA studies to focus on the manure management system rather than the milk production system. Thus, there are no similar studies available in the literature to compare results for all pathways and sustainability indicators under the same assumptions. GHG emissions have been the most studied impact in the LCA literature, but manure emissions been aggregated to total results and the functional unit has usually been kilograms of milk. Reinemann et al. (2011) reported 0.19 kg CO2-eq kg- milk attributed to manure handling, which is equivalent to 71 kg CO2-eq ton manure. Thoma et al. (2013) reported a U.S. average of 1.23 kg CO2-eq kg` milk, with manure responsible for 22% of these emissions. This is equivalent to 102.8 kg CO2-eq ton manure if this number expressed in terms of the functional unit of this paper. Although these results can be compared to the 101.2 kg CO2-eq ton -1 manure of the BC pathway, assumptions, management practices, and climatic conditions are different. The Dairy Greenhouse Gas Model V 1.2 was used to simulate the conditions of the BC pathway for WI (Rotz and Chianese 2009). However, results are not comparable because manure GHG emissions

are reported together with barn and cropland emissions in that model. The only unit process that could be compared was storage with results of 80 kg CO2-eq ton manure VS. 74 kg CO2-eq ton- manure from this paper.

Results from the AD and AD+SLS pathways can be compared to biogas LCA studies available in the literature; however, most of these studies consider different practices (i.e. co- digestion with other biomass feedstocks) and report results in terms of energy produced. Poeschl et al. (2012a) reported -23.2 kg CO2-eq ton manure for a reference pathway where biogas was produced from cattle manure. The authors accounted for the credits of avoiding the production and use of fossil electricity and synthetic fertilizers, which precludes the comparison among results. Börjesson and Berglund (2007) reported 15 g CO2-eq MJ of heat and power for animal manure when conducting system expansion to partition the environmental impacts of the system between digestate and energy. After applying the subdivision/allocation approach (and including heat as part of the products to make results comparable) this paper determines that 10 g CO2-eq MJ-1 are emitted to produce electricity.

60: <header>4.7 Senstivity Analysis </header>

A sensitivity analysis is performed by varying each of the parameters used to determine GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF and nutrient availability by +10%. Results for AD+SLS pathway are presented in Figure 8 and in the SI for the remaining pathways (Figures S3-S5). A cut-off criterion is adopted to include those parameters that influence the results by more than + 1%. Sensitivity to nutrient availability is presented for N only as it is the most dynamic nutrient. GHG emissions are most sensitive to changes in CO2(b) emissions after application in all pathways because it is assumed that all carbon in manure is eventually emitted as CO2 after

application, which highlights the importance of future studies that quantify the carbon sequestration of manure application in the long term. CH4(b) emissions during storage have a bigger influence in the BC and SLS pathways since CH4 has been captured and transformed to energy during the digestion process. As a result, N20(b) after land application has a significant influence on GHG emissions in the AD and AD+SLS pathways. GHG emissions sensitivity to NH3 emissions indicates the importance of indirect N2O emissions in this model as well as CH4 leakages in pathways that involve a digester. Oppositely, some other parameters that influence results are negatively related to GHG emissions. For example, an increase of TS in AD pathways results in a decrease in GHG emissions because biogas production is related to TS content. GHG emissions are also negatively related to parameters that increase nutrient availability, such as the number of milking cows and nutrient content (N, P, and K) in excreted manure, because the model considers that any increase in manure nutrients would replace the production of synthetic fertilizers and their related lifecycle impacts. This trend is not linear since manure application is limited due to nutrient application constraints.

A 10% change in nitrogen availability, diesel fuel, and sand recovered would have the biggest influence on DFF, which indicates the amount of primary energy that is embedded in the production of nitrogen fertilizer and diesel fuel, and the significant quantities of sand that are used for cow bedding. DFF is sensitivity to N volatilization because additional synthetic fertilizer is needed to compensate for those N losses. Parameters that increase nutrient availability (i.e. number of cows) reduce DFF because of replacement of synthetic fertilizers. Energy requirements, including electricity for solid-liquid mechanical separation, sand recovery, and diesel for manure collection and application; impact DFF in BC and SLS pathways. These energy requirements are directly related to the amount of manure mass that is processed;

therefore, DFF is sensitive to parameters that increase manure quantities (i.e. water used in sand recovery). In AD pathways, DFF is inversely sensitive to TS (more TS less DFF), especially to those TS that get into the digester because they are directly related to renewable energy production.

NH3 emissions are most sensitive to volatilization after land application in BC and SLS pathways, and during storage in AD and AD+SLS pathways. For pathways that involve SLS, volatilization from manure liquids is more influential than from manure solids. As N in excreted manure increases, downstream NH3 emissions also increase. Inversely, as more organic N is available when reaching land application, NH3 emissions are reduced. Finally, N availability is sensitive to excreted N (mainly by milking cows), ammoniacal N (since it is more available), final availability of organic N, and N volatilization as it decreases its availability. NH3 emissions are reduced by 40% if manure is injected instead of being surface land-applied in the pathways with a digester. This reduction increases nitrogen availability by 50% in AD and AD+SLS, and 46% when compared to BC without injection. The overall effects on GHG emissions and DFF of these and other management practices will be analyzed in future work.

Finally, N availability is sensitive to excreted N (mainly by milking cows), ammoniacal N (since it is more available), final availability of organic N, and N volatilization as it decreases its availability. NH3 emissions are reduced by 40% if manure is injected instead of being surface land-applied in the pathways with a digester. This reduction increases nitrogen availability by 50% in AD and AD+SLS, and 46% when compared to BC without injection. The overall effects on GHG emissions and DFF of these and other management practices will be analyzed in future work.
64: <header>4.8 Conclusions and Implications </header>

The manure processing pathway has a substantial influence on the estimates of environmental impacts, which highlights the importance of assessing different sustainability indicators to identify trade-offs among pathways. This study uses a process based approach to develop manure specific inventory data and applies LCA techniques to quantify and compare GHGs, NH3 emissions, DFF, and nutrients between land applying manure, which is the most traditional disposal method in WI, with three other pathways that use SLS and AD technologies. Findings

show that AD pathways have the largest reductions in GHG emissions and DFF, but the largest increase in NH3 emissions. Reductions in GHG emissions come mostly from avoidance of CH4(b) and N2O(b) emissions during storage and land application, and reduction of fossil CO2 emissions due the production of electricity from biogas. The AD+SLS and AD pathways present the largest increase in NH3 emissions with 44% and 40% respectively, while the SLS pathway presented no change. This significant increase is a result of higher ammoniacal N in manure after both storage and digestion, the inexistence of a natural crust on top of the storage, and the land-spread application method (broadcast) since all of these factors create the conditions for N volatilization. These losses could be mitigated by adopting different management practices such as manure injection. When compared to grid electricity, the FERAD improved by a factor of 12 and the EROIAD by a factor that ranges from of 3 to 7 depending if heat is recovered in AD pathways. These ratios would have been higher by recovering the thermal energy that is currently lost during the energy generation process. Land applied total P and K are similar to excreted P and K for all pathways, with the difference that they are divided between the solid and liquid streams during SLS pathways. Even though the mineralization that occurs in the digester increases ammoniacal N (which is more available for crop production) during AD pathways, the overall net N availability after land application is very similar in all pathways.

When allocating the sustainability indicators to specific system outputs, it is demonstrated that adopting the traditional whole-system allocation approach introduces substantial variability to the results. This variability can be significantly reduced by applying a combined subdivision and allocation approach. A sensitivity analysis shows the importance of the N balance through manure management since it affects all sustainability indicators simultaneously. In addition, since C is a major component of manure TS, it is important to understand the dynamics of

manure C when it reaches the land and how to improve carbon sequestration to reduce GHG emissions. Besides direct emissions, such as CH4 and N2O during storage and land application, indirect emissions from NH3 emissions have a big influence on GHG emissions. Even though marginal changes do not have a major contribution to environmental impacts in the modeled pathways, sensitivity shows that an increase in synthetic fertilizers and bedding materials would increase DFF. This highlights the importance of promoting practices that increase nutrient availability since the production of these materials is SO energy intensive.

The approach adopted and the model developed in this paper can be applied to a broad range of sustainability indicators and systems that have not been studied in the LCA literature. This approach is useful to compare marginal changes among alternative pathways that are a result of improvement decisions and technologies, and to identify and quantify the environmental trade- offs from a system oriented perspective regardless of the difference in final products. This paper combines data from different sources and integrates multiple simulation models to fill the current knowledge gaps in the areas of dairy manure management, conventional LCA, and environmental sustainability to inform businesses, farmers, and governments and promote science-based responsible decision making. This paper is only a first step in the process of developing a more comprehensive study which will involve addressing some important challenges. First, more research is needed to characterize and understand GHG and NH3 emissions from digestate and separated manures. This study only differentiates N2O and NH3 emissions based on ammoniacal content, but other variables such as C:N ratio and particle size could influence GHG emissions. Second, the decision to include the environmental impacts related to the agricultural phase of manure needs to be analyzed. LCA studies have SO far considered that manure is a waste and thus, has no environmental impacts associated to its

production (all environmental impacts are assigned to the main product of milk). However, as manure gains more importance as a source of nutrients for crop production and becomes a major input for renewable energy generation, it will turn from being a waste to a valuable co-product of the dairy system. Considering manure as a co-product will add complexity and further challenges to the model due to the additional allocation and variables that will have to be included in the analysis. Third, alternative strategies should be analyzed to improve the environmental sustainability of manure processing pathways. For example, different manure management practices (i.e. alley scraper collection and land-injection), co-digestion of manure with other locally available biomass resources (i.e. corn stover and cheese whey), and exploring different biogas conversion technologies besides electric energy (i.e. compressed gas for transportation). Finally, economic and social considerations should be included to complete the three sustainability pillars that constitute a thorough Life Cycle Sustainability (LCSA) study.

[g] Addition of synthetic P and K are a result of losses during the sand recovery process and the installation of a cyclone. Synthetic N addition is a result of NH3 volatilization.</list>

0: Defining anaerobic digestion pathways: The effect of management practices, co- digestion strategies, and energy convesion processes on environmental impacts
1: This study applies life cycle assessment (LCA) techniques to evaluate the processes controlling the environmental impacts of current and potential anaerobic digestion (AD) pathways on a dairy farm setting representative of Wisconsin. Eight AD pathways are compared against a base-case (BC) pathway to understand the effect of management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient balances. For the BC, GHG emissions are 243.3 kg CO2-eq, NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg, DFF is 306.8 MJ, and N availability is 9.5 kg per GJ-1 of produced energy. Results show that the AD pathway has a substantial influence on the estimates of environmental impacts. GHG emissions range from 100 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1, , NH3 emissions range from 4 to 18 kg GJ-1, DFF range from 176 to 566 MJ GJ-1, and N availability ranges from 3 to 15 kg GJ-1 of produced energy. In addition, energy return on investment (EROIAD) and fossil energy ratios (FERAD) range from 0.9 to 1.4 and from 1.8 to 5.7. Injecting digestate is an effective management practice to reduce NH3 emissions, which at the same time increases N availability and reduces GHG emissions and DFF given that the manufacturing of purchased fertilizers is fossil energy intensive. Co-digesting manure with corn stover and switchgrass increases energy production. However, corn stover is more effective in reducing GHG emissions as this feedstock is less resource intensive in production. Finally,
recovering heat has proven to be an easy to adopt strategy that improves all sustainability indicators without implementing major operational changes.
2: This study applies life cycle assessment (LCA) techniques to evaluate the processes controlling the environmental impacts of current and potential anaerobic digestion (AD) pathways on a dairy farm setting representative of Wisconsin. Eight AD pathways are compared against a base-case (BC) pathway to understand the effect of management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient balances. For the BC, GHG emissions are 243.3 kg CO2-eq, NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg, DFF is 306.8 MJ, and N availability is 9.5 kg per GJ-1 of produced energy. GHG emissions range from 100 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1, , NH3 emissions range from 4 to 18 kg GJ-1, DFF range from 176 to 566 MJ GJ-1, and N availability ranges from 3 to 15 kg GJ-1 of produced energy. In addition, energy return on investment (EROIAD) and fossil energy ratios (FERAD) range from 0.9 to 1.4 and from 1.8 to 5.7. However, corn stover is more effective in reducing GHG emissions as this feedstock is less resource intensive in production. Finally,

3: In the last years, the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS2) of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA, 2007) has encouraged the production of liquid transportation biofuels, specifically corn and cellulosic ethanol, which have been the center of the biofuel discussion and academic research (EISA 2007). In recent years, the production of other types of biofuels including biogas produced through anaerobic digestion (AD) has received increased attention. AD is a natural occurring process in which microbial organisms break down organic material in the absence of oxygen to produce biogas, a mix of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). The resulting biogas can be further combusted to produce electricity or thermal energy for heating applications, upgraded to be injected into a natural gas pipeline, or compressed to be used as transportation fuel. The remaining fraction after digestion (known as digestate) can be used as fertilizer as it maintains the nutrient contents of the initial feedstock.

AD is not a new technology. Bacteria have been producing biogas naturally for millions of years and sewage treatment plants have been applying this concept for decades. More recently, AD has been applied to agricultural facilities to mitigate climate change and achieve energy independence. The main advantages that have positioned AD among the most promising technologies to produce sustainable energy are the flexibility in substrate utilization, relatively straightforward conversion process, reduction of emissions from waste streams, reduction of odor and pathogens, and potential to contribute to nutrient control strategies. Despite these advantages, biogas has been largely absent from the biofuels' debate. This is in part due to the

lack of comprehensive biogas studies that explore the benefits and sustainability of this versatile biofuel through its lifecycle. In addition, even though there are some general policies that include biogas as part of broader environmental and energy goals (Table S1 of the supplementary information), more specific policy frameworks and regulations are needed to boost the implementation of farm-based digesters.

Livestock manure is currently the main feedstock used in agricultural AD systems. As a result, states with well-established animal agriculture production hold the largest number of digesters in the U.S. Wisconsin (WI) is known as America's dairy land with nearly 1.3 million dairy COWS which produce approximately 4.7 million dry tons of manure annually. The volume of manure produced constitutes a major environmental concern but also an abundant biomass source for energy generation. Given the large quantities of manure generated, more than 30 digesters have been installed in dairy farms making WI the leading state in digester installations in the country (USEPA 2014). At the moment, electricity is the most common form of energy produced from biogas mostly due to electricity prices and available conversion technologies (Kramer 2009). Given the flexibility of biogas it is important to evaluate other end-use energy forms that have the potential for implementation. For example, the RFS2 includes biogas as a transportation biofuel which could eventually boost biogas production for this sector. Despite these opportunities the development of new anaerobic digesters in the state has been relatively slow and there are no studies available in the literature that consider a comprehensive environmental assessment of AD systems in WI from a life cycle perspective.

Many biomass feedstocks other than manure have biogas production potential. Mixing feedstocks (co-digestion) is a practice that has increased in agricultural systems that use this technology. Recent studies have shown that co-digestion can increase biogas yields according to

the feedstock composition combined in the digester (Comino et al. 2012; Westerholm et al. 2012; Marañón et al. 2012). Radloff et al. (2012) assessed the bioenergy feedstock potential in WI and concluded that corn stover is the second underutilized biomass source in the state after manure. The production of bioenergy from corn stover has been studied in WI, but primarily as a feedstock to produce cellulosic ethanol (Sinistore 2012). There is a natural coupling that exists between dairy manure and corn stover as feedstocks of AD given that both resources are readily available on dairy farms. Similarly, dedicated energy grasses have been considered as bioenergy sources for electricity, heat, and cellulosic ethanol due to their various benefits when compared to other crops (e.g. grasses require fewer fertilizers, can grow on less productive and marginal land areas, and have better energy ratios than biomass crops). As a result, there is potential to use these feedstocks for AD in the short-term as the cellulosic ethanol industry is not feasible yet.

Countries in Europe have been leading the implementation and evaluation of AD systems and their related environmental impacts (Börjesson and Berglund 2007; Appels et al. 2011; Poeschl et al. 2012). Price incentives for biogas production in these countries have justified the adoption of strategies that make these AD systems fundamentally different from those in WI. First, feedstocks in Europe are transported long distances to centralized large-scale digesters. Most agricultural digesters on WI are smaller in scale than those in Europe and are fed with feedstocks available on-farm. Second, food crops (e.g. corn and wheat) are used as AD feedstocks in Europe as they have high energy densities. In WI, digesters are fed with waste streams (mostly manure) that have low energy densities. Finally, on-farm management practices (e.g. digestate processing and disposal methods) can be different from one context to the other. It is important to account for these practices when evaluating the environmental impacts of an agricultural system as they have a significant influence on results (Thoma et al. 2013). For

example, AD increases the ammoniacal nitrogen (N) content in digestate through mineralization. In this form N is more available to crops but is also volatilized more easily. If digestate is not injected or incorporated, N availability can be reduced significantly through volatilization losses (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014).

There is still a limited number of studies assessing the lifecycle environmental impacts of biogas production, and even fewer studies looking at the impacts of different co-digestion and management practices in the U.S. This study aims at addressing these knowledge gaps by applying LCA techniques to quantify and compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient availability of different AD pathways. The analyzed pathways consider different manure management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes in order to pinpoint approaches that maximize energy production and minimize environmental impacts. Results from this assessment could guide the development of regulations specific to biogas and accelerate the implementation of farm-based digesters by providing useful information about their operation and impacts.

If digestate is not injected or incorporated, N availability can be reduced significantly through volatilization losses (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014).

There is still a limited number of studies assessing the lifecycle environmental impacts of biogas production, and even fewer studies looking at the impacts of different co-digestion and management practices in the U.S. This study aims at addressing these knowledge gaps by applying LCA techniques to quantify and compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient availability of different AD pathways. The analyzed pathways consider different manure management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes in order to pinpoint approaches that maximize energy production and minimize environmental impacts. Results from this assessment could guide the development of regulations specific to biogas and accelerate the implementation of farm-based digesters by providing useful information about their operation and impacts.
5: <header>5.2 Introduction </header>

In the last years, the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS2) of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA, 2007) has encouraged the production of liquid transportation biofuels, specifically corn and cellulosic ethanol, which have been the center of the biofuel discussion and academic research (EISA 2007). In recent years, the production of other types of biofuels including biogas produced through anaerobic digestion (AD) has received increased attention. AD is a natural occurring process in which microbial organisms break down organic material in the absence of oxygen to produce biogas, a mix of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2). The resulting biogas can be further combusted to produce electricity or thermal energy for heating applications, upgraded to be injected into a natural gas pipeline, or compressed to be used as transportation fuel. The remaining fraction after digestion (known as digestate) can be used as fertilizer as it maintains the nutrient contents of the initial feedstock.

AD is not a new technology. Bacteria have been producing biogas naturally for millions of years and sewage treatment plants have been applying this concept for decades. More recently, AD has been applied to agricultural facilities to mitigate climate change and achieve energy independence. The main advantages that have positioned AD among the most promising technologies to produce sustainable energy are the flexibility in substrate utilization, relatively straightforward conversion process, reduction of emissions from waste streams, reduction of odor and pathogens, and potential to contribute to nutrient control strategies. Despite these advantages, biogas has been largely absent from the biofuels' debate. This is in part due to the

lack of comprehensive biogas studies that explore the benefits and sustainability of this versatile biofuel through its lifecycle. In addition, even though there are some general policies that include biogas as part of broader environmental and energy goals (Table S1 of the supplementary information), more specific policy frameworks and regulations are needed to boost the implementation of farm-based digesters.

Livestock manure is currently the main feedstock used in agricultural AD systems. As a result, states with well-established animal agriculture production hold the largest number of digesters in the U.S. Wisconsin (WI) is known as America's dairy land with nearly 1.3 million dairy COWS which produce approximately 4.7 million dry tons of manure annually. The volume of manure produced constitutes a major environmental concern but also an abundant biomass source for energy generation. Given the large quantities of manure generated, more than 30 digesters have been installed in dairy farms making WI the leading state in digester installations in the country (USEPA 2014). At the moment, electricity is the most common form of energy produced from biogas mostly due to electricity prices and available conversion technologies (Kramer 2009). Given the flexibility of biogas it is important to evaluate other end-use energy forms that have the potential for implementation. For example, the RFS2 includes biogas as a transportation biofuel which could eventually boost biogas production for this sector. Despite these opportunities the development of new anaerobic digesters in the state has been relatively slow and there are no studies available in the literature that consider a comprehensive environmental assessment of AD systems in WI from a life cycle perspective.

Many biomass feedstocks other than manure have biogas production potential. Mixing feedstocks (co-digestion) is a practice that has increased in agricultural systems that use this technology. Recent studies have shown that co-digestion can increase biogas yields according to

the feedstock composition combined in the digester (Comino et al. 2012; Westerholm et al. 2012; Marañón et al. 2012). Radloff et al. (2012) assessed the bioenergy feedstock potential in WI and concluded that corn stover is the second underutilized biomass source in the state after manure. The production of bioenergy from corn stover has been studied in WI, but primarily as a feedstock to produce cellulosic ethanol (Sinistore 2012). There is a natural coupling that exists between dairy manure and corn stover as feedstocks of AD given that both resources are readily available on dairy farms. Similarly, dedicated energy grasses have been considered as bioenergy sources for electricity, heat, and cellulosic ethanol due to their various benefits when compared to other crops (e.g. grasses require fewer fertilizers, can grow on less productive and marginal land areas, and have better energy ratios than biomass crops). As a result, there is potential to use these feedstocks for AD in the short-term as the cellulosic ethanol industry is not feasible yet.

Countries in Europe have been leading the implementation and evaluation of AD systems and their related environmental impacts (Börjesson and Berglund 2007; Appels et al. 2011; Poeschl et al. 2012). Price incentives for biogas production in these countries have justified the adoption of strategies that make these AD systems fundamentally different from those in WI. First, feedstocks in Europe are transported long distances to centralized large-scale digesters. Most agricultural digesters on WI are smaller in scale than those in Europe and are fed with feedstocks available on-farm. Second, food crops (e.g. corn and wheat) are used as AD feedstocks in Europe as they have high energy densities. In WI, digesters are fed with waste streams (mostly manure) that have low energy densities. Finally, on-farm management practices (e.g. digestate processing and disposal methods) can be different from one context to the other. It is important to account for these practices when evaluating the environmental impacts of an agricultural system as they have a significant influence on results (Thoma et al. 2013). For

example, AD increases the ammoniacal nitrogen (N) content in digestate through mineralization. In this form N is more available to crops but is also volatilized more easily. If digestate is not injected or incorporated, N availability can be reduced significantly through volatilization losses (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014).

There is still a limited number of studies assessing the lifecycle environmental impacts of biogas production, and even fewer studies looking at the impacts of different co-digestion and management practices in the U.S. This study aims at addressing these knowledge gaps by applying LCA techniques to quantify and compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, ammonia (NH3) emissions, depletion of fossil fuels (DFF), and nutrient availability of different AD pathways. The analyzed pathways consider different manure management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes in order to pinpoint approaches that maximize energy production and minimize environmental impacts. Results from this assessment could guide the development of regulations specific to biogas and accelerate the implementation of farm-based digesters by providing useful information about their operation and impacts.

10: <header>5.3 Relation to Existing Literature </header>

Evaluating the benefits of AD systems has been of interest to researchers and scientists for decades. Studies have primarily targeted energy potential and biogas production focusing on specific aspects of the digester design and feedstocks performance. Gebremedhin et al. (2005) developed a heat transfer model to predict energy requirements to operate a plug-flow digester. Wu (2012) found that methane yield remained unchanged with increasing mixing power in complete-mix digesters, but that energy output increased with heat additions in plug-flow digesters. Comino et al. (2012) investigated biogas yield from co-digesting cattle manure with

cheese whey and found that up to 65% whey can be co-digested. Appels et al. (2011) reviewed several feedstocks used in AD and discussed their potential and challenges. The authors concluded that animal manure is one of the most suitable feedstocks for biogas production because of its N content, easy degradability, and low cost availability. The authors also concluded that cellulosic sources, such as crop residues, dedicated crops, and grasses can be degraded to up to 80% in AD, representing interesting feedstocks for this conversion technology.

These studies constitute valuable sources of information but their scope is limited for the design and implementation of biofuels' policies and to analyze operational trade-offs. Pimentel (1991) was one of the first to suggest that a lifecycle approach should be considered when evaluating a biofuel given that the production of biomass feedstocks is energy and resource intensive, and can affect the net energy and environmental performance of the biofuel that is being evaluated. This approach was further strengthened with the establishment of the RFS2, which defined life cycle assessment (LCA) as the methodology to estimate the environmental impacts and energy balance of biofuels. Cherubini (2010) outlined key steps when defining lifecycle greenhouse gas (GHG) balances of bioenergy systems that produce electricity, heat, and transportation biofuels from residues or crops. These steps include biomass source, cultivation and harvest, production and application of fertilizer, pre-treatment and transport, conversion, distribution, and combustion of the biofuel. The author suggests that when a crop residue is used for energy production, the auxiliary energy and material inputs of the agricultural phase can be assumed to be zero.

Biogas LCA studies are relatively new and many of them have focused on existing municipal wastewater and water treatment plants (Pasqualino et al. 2009; Vergara et al. 2011). With the development of more efficient and specialized digesters, the incorporation of agriculture based

feedstocks has received increased attention within the biogas LCA literature (Jury et al. 2010; Cherubini and Stromman 2011). In spite of this, most studies available are based on defined pathways or centered on specific aspects of the biogas lifecycle. Jury et al. (2010) focused on the final use of biogas and estimated reduced emissions in comparison to natural gas when upgrading and injecting biogas into natural gas pipelines. Patterson et al. (2011) quantified the environmental impacts of assembling and installing central and distributed infrastructures to produce and use biogas. De Vries et al. (2012) evaluated the environmental impacts related to a new manure processing technology that concentrated nutrients in the liquid fraction and generated biogas from the solid fraction.

Fewer studies within the biogas LCA literature have considered the environmental impacts of multiple production pathways and uses. Börjesson and Berglund (2006) was one of the first studies that analyzed different scenarios for feedstock production and energy generation. The paper estimated fuel-cycle emissions in Sweden and made emphasis on how the system responded to individual feedstocks, digester scales, and transportation distances. The authors found that operating the biogas plant demands more than one third of energy consumption and that AD systems are energy positive for short distance transport of raw materials. More recently, Poeschl et al. (2012) described lifecycle inventory (LCI) and lifecycle impact assessment (LCIA) of 18 impact categories of single-feedstock digestion and co-digestion systems in small and large biogas plants in Germany. The authors concluded that the most efficient substrates in terms of environmental impacts are straw and corn silage within single-feedstock scenarios and combining municipal solid waste with agricultural and food industry residues within co-digestion scenarios.

The situation and set-up of the AD industry in WI calls for a different definition of AD pathways than the ones applied in the studies mentioned above. Corn silage and other crops are the base feedstocks of those studies; whereas dairy manure is the base feedstock in WI. Moreover, the approach used in the lifecycle inventory stage by the studies mentioned above is based on average practices and emission factors (EF) as opposed to developing case specific factors. Finally, there has been no investigation on the effect of manure management practices and technology choices within the biogas LCA literature. As Appels et al. (2011) argue, there is a need to further extend existing AD models since operational and management decisions are currently done by a general rule of thumb. Manure management practices, such as the use of digested solids as bedding, or technology choices, such as the separation technology used, could lead to important changes in the system that result in differentiated environmental impacts. For example, using digestated solids for bedding avoids the production of other bedding materials (e.g. sand) and the need of their separation from manure prior to entering the digester. There is a need to conduct comprehensive assessments on AD systems considering environmental sustainability indicators and processing technologies according to local conditions and practices in the U.S.

15: <header>5.4.1 Scope </header>

Process-based and LCA methods are applied to evaluate GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, and nutrient availability. These sustainability indicators are evaluated through each step of current and potential AD pathways in a dairy farm system representative of WI. All pathways are modeled with the software GaBi 5 (PE International 2012) for a dairy farm with 1,000 lactating

cows, 605 growing heifers (<21 months), and 286 mature heifers and dry cows. The size of the farm has been defined as current commercial manure digesters are more economically feasible for large dairy operations and the average dairy size of farms holding digesters is approximately 2,000 animals in WI (Kramer 2009). As result, the more than 200 CAFOs in WI represent the greatest potential for anaerobic digestion in the short term.

AD pathways include the following unit processes: feedstock production, collection, and pre- treatment; biogas production, conditioning, and energy generation; digestate solid-liquid separation (SLS); storage of liquid and solid streams; and land application of liquid and solid streams. Pathways are defined based on management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion process. Figure 1 shows the system boundaries that encompass all unit- processes of the analyzed pathways starting with the production of raw materials and ending at the farm gate with biogas processed into its final energy form. The main product is electricity or compressed gas depending on the AD pathway. Time steps vary across the AD lifecycle (e.g. one year for corn stover and switchgrass production, one day for manure collection, and six months for manure storage). Since the main purpose of this system is energy production and biogas is generated on a daily basis, all calculations are averaged for one day. Animal husbandry and cultivation processes for cow feeding are not included in this analysis. Embedded and cumulative energy, NH3 and GHG emissions associated with the production of material and energy inputs (i.e. diesel and electricity) are included in the system boundaries; however, the production of capital goods (i.e. machinery and buildings) is excluded as its contribution to the measured outputs is marginal (Kim et al. 2009). The functional unit (FU) for this analysis is defined as 1 GJ of produced energy. This FU reflects the main function of the system, energy production, and allows for comparisons with other fossil and renewable energy LCA studies. No allocation of co-
products is applied given that the main objective of the paper is to compare marginal changes in environmental impacts among AD pathways.

This analysis considers marginal changes of material and energy inputs when comparing AD pathways to the BC. Additions or reductions of material inputs take place with synthetic fertilizers, when there are changes to nutrient availability reaching the soil (e.g. due ammonia emissions), and sand when solid digestate is used as the bedding material. Urea, diammonium phosphate, and potassium chloride are the purchased sources of N, P, and K to supplement crop production when needed. Availability of nutrients in digestate reaching the land is 80% for P and K, 100% for ammoniacal nitrogen, and 43% for organic nitrogen (25% the first year, 12% the second year, and 6% the third year) (Laboski and Peters 2012).

products is applied given that the main objective of the paper is to compare marginal changes in environmental impacts among AD pathways.

This analysis considers marginal changes of material and energy inputs when comparing AD pathways to the BC. Additions or reductions of material inputs take place with synthetic fertilizers, when there are changes to nutrient availability reaching the soil (e.g. due ammonia emissions), and sand when solid digestate is used as the bedding material. Urea, diammonium phosphate, and potassium chloride are the purchased sources of N, P, and K to supplement crop production when needed. Availability of nutrients in digestate reaching the land is 80% for P and K, 100% for ammoniacal nitrogen, and 43% for organic nitrogen (25% the first year, 12% the second year, and 6% the third year) (Laboski and Peters 2012).
18: <header>5.4.2 Environmental Sustainability Indicators </header>

NH3 emissions and nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) are reported in kilograms. GHG emissions are characterized for a 100-year time horizon and measured in kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalents (kg CO2-eq). Characterization factors used for gases other than CO2 are 298 kg CO2-eq for N2O and 25 kg CO2-eq for CH4 based on the CML 2001 method. N2O emissions resulting from NH3 emissions and leaching are estimated using IPCC's definition that 0.01 of volatilized N as NH3 is converted to N2O-N emissions and 0.0075 of leached N is converted to N2O-N emissions (IPCC 2006). Emissions from biomass are considered biotic emissions (CO2(b), N2O(b) , CH4(b)) and are evaluated separately from fossil fuel emissions (CO2(f), N2O(f), CH4(f)) to account for the CO2(b) recycling process that takes place during plant growth as it is assumed that the carbon contained in biomass has been previously captured as CO2.

DFF is defined as the energy consumed at the site plus the energy consumed in the production and delivery of that energy product. The fossil energy ratio (FER) and the energy return on investment (EROI) ratio can be used to determine the efficiency of a sustainable energy production. If the FER is < 1, the amount of usable energy is less than the fossil energy expended to obtain that energy. If the EROI ratio is < 1, the amount of usable energy is less than the total energy expended to obtain that energy (Equations 1 and 2).
"FER ="|Usable nergyout|(1) "FER ="|energyin|(1) EROI|Usable energyout =|(2) EROI|Total energyin|(2)

20: <header>5.4.3 Inventory Data and Description of AD Pathways and Unit-Processes </header>

Life cycle inventory (LCI) is developed using a process-based approach to capture material, energy, and emission flows for each unit-process of the AD pathways. These flows are related to system parameters and local conditions of WI (e.g. volatile solids, temperature) to maximize the representativeness of the model. Year-to-year temperature variation is captured by averaging daily data from five weather stations on each of the regions of WI for the years 2007-2011. In addition, the electricity matrix represents the mix of fuels that are part of the electric grid of WI (MER 2011). The necessary data for constructing the LCI includes various sources. First, data required to calculate material, energy, and emission inputs and outputs for manure AD are based on Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014). Second, data on switchgrass comes from the literature as switchgrass is not currently produced on a commercial scale in WI. Representative studies for switchgrass and corn stover production in WI include Myhre (2010); Cronin (2011); Sinistore (2012). Finally, verifiable databases have been used for material and energy inputs, including the
National Renewable Energy Laboratory U.S. LCI dataset, PE International (2012) Professional database, and EcoInvent (Ecoinvent 2007).

A single feedstock AD system is defined as the base-case pathway (BC) where manure is the only feedstock to produce on-farm electricity with no heat recovery. In the BC manure is collected with a diesel skid steer, digestate is separated with a screw press, digestate is land- applied by surface broadcast, and sand is used as cow bedding. Cow diet composition, manure excretion and characteristics, and biogas production rates used in the BC are described on Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014). A total of eight AD pathways are compared against the BC. Each pathway is defined by varying one unit-process of the BC while maintaining the rest constant (Table 1). Four pathways are modeled by changing the following management practices: manure collection with an electric alley scraper, digestate SLS with a centrifuge, digestate land application by injection, and digested solids used as cow bedding. Two pathways are modeled by co-digesting manure with corn stover and switchgrass. Finally, two pathways are modeled by producing electricity with heat recovery and compressed gas for transportation as final products. Attributional lifecycle concepts are used to evaluate each individual pathway and consequential lifecycle concepts are used to compare the marginal changes between the BC and the remaining pathways.

23: <header>Management practices </header>

Four AD pathways are compared against the BC based on changes to management practices during manure collection, digestate SLS, digestate land application, and digested solids use. The first AD pathway (AS) compared to the BC considers collecting manure with an electric alley scraper that requires 1.38 kWh ton manure based on a survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) on 178 dairy farms in WI. Even though the frequency of collection is increased with

the alley scraper when compared to the skid steer, it is assumed that GHG emission factors from the barn are not altered by the collection method. Moreira and Satter evaluated the effect of scraping frequency on NH3 emissions, and concluded that the frequency of scraping had little or no effect on NH3 emissions from the barn floor (Moreira and Satter 2006).

The second AD pathway (CTF) considers digestate SLS by centrifugation which requires 3.1 kWh ton of digestate (Moller et al. 2000). It is assumed that no biotic emissions occur during the separation process itself due to the short retention time. Separation efficiencies and characteristics of the solid and liquid streams after SLS are presented in Table S2. The third AD pathway (INJ) considers land-application of both solid and liquid digestate fractions by injection instead of surface broadcast. Diesel consumption for injection is calculated as 0.69 kg ton digestate based on Hadrich et al. (2010); Rotz et al. (2011). N2O(b)-N is emitted at a rate of 1.76% of applied NH3-N after ammonia emissions for the liquid fraction and 1.97% for the solid fraction since it assumed that solid digestate behaves as untreated solid manure (Chadwick et al. 2011). Several studies found that there are no changes in CH4(b) emissions from injection to surface broadcast; therefore, the same EF is assumed for both practices (Wulf et al. 2002; Rodhe et al. 2006; Perälä et al. 2006; Agnew et al. 2010). In the fourth AD pathway (BED), digested solids are used as cow bedding instead of being applied to the soil, which replaces sand as the bedding material. According to the survey conducted by Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2014) digested solids are replaced three times a week at an average rate of 5.5 kg cow-1 day in WI dairy farms that use this type of bedding. This quantity represents nearly 75% of the daily separated solids.

25: <header>Co-digestion strategies </header>
Two co-digestion systems are compared against the manure single digestion system described in the BC. A flexible approach when feeding the digester is adopted to increase the

volume of the AD system according to feedstock availability. The amount of additional feedstock that can be added to the digester is limited by two factors: yields that can be achieved on the farm, and total solids (TS) content of the additional feedstock. TS is a limiting factor since the systems requires 15% TS or less in order to maintain a liquid AD process (Liew et al. 2012).

The first co-digestion pathway (CS) considers mixing manure with corn stover. In this pathway, corn stover is removed as opposed to the BC where it remains on the field. Stover is harvested on an annual basis from continuous corn plantings where the grain is used for cow feed. A total of 321 hectares of corn are needed to supply the corn grain included in the dairy ration and a conservative corn stover removal rate of 3,400 kg ha is applied based on Sinistore (2012) who conducted a LCA of ethanol production from corn stover in WI. This number is consistent with James et al. (2010) under removal rates of 38%. Removal of nutrients and organic matter in corn plantings are minimized given that digestate is re-applied to the soil in spring and fall. Harvesting and baling of corn stover are done on an annual basis and require 6.6 kg of diesel ton (Sinistore 2012). Stover is ground before entering the digester to increase bacteria accessibility to volatile solids (VS), which requires 14.9 kWh of electricity ton-1. A total of 570 m³ are required to process the extra feedstock coming from corn stover on top of the existing 4,840 m³ required to process dairy manure under the assumptions of a 28 day hydraulic retention time (HRT) and a stover density of 147 kg per cubic meter (Kaliyan and Morey 2009). Corn stover estimates of 85% TS, 90% VS (of TS), and CH4 yield of 213 ml g -1 VS are selected by averaging the results of nine studies available in the literature (Table 2). These characteristics increase the TS content of the digester mixture to 9%, which is lower than the 15% needed for liquid digestion. It is assumed that N, P and K are removed at rates of 0.9, 13.3, and 7.5 kg ton-1. which are replaced either by digestate or purchased fertilizers (Bundy 1998).

The second co-digestion pathway (SG) considers mixing manure with switchgrass. A recent study by Ventura et al. (2012) estimated that there are 635 thousand hectares suitable for energy grass production in WI. Switchgrass is produced under the assumption that there is readily available land on the dairy farm. One year of establishment and 20 years of production before replanting are considered for this grass (Cronin 2011; Blengini et al. 2011). All material and energy inputs for switchgrass production are taken from Myhre (2010). During the establishment year, seeds are planted at a rate of 7.6 kg ha and herbicides glyphosate and 2,4- dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) are applied for weed control at rates of 1.9 and 1.4 1 ha-1. In the harvest years, N, P, and K fertilizers are applied at rates of 76, 9, and 104 kg ha-1 respectively, while 2,4-D, atrazine, and lime are applied at 1.41 ha-1, 0.8 kg ha-1. and 243 kg ha-1. It is assumed that the produced switchgrass volume is equal to that of corn stover to facilitate comparisons among these biomass feedstocks. The average yield of switchgrass for WI is 9.1 ton ha-1, which demands nearly 190 ha of land. 85% TS, 93.5% VS (of TS), and CH4 yield of 220 ml g-1 VS are determined for switchgrass by averaging the results of seven studies available in the literature (Table 2).

28: <header>Energy conversion </header>

This unit-process targets the transformation of biogas into its final energy form. The BC considers the production of electricity with an engine-generator-set with no heat recovery, given that the majority of AD systems installed in WI adopt this approach (Kramer 2009). Two AD pathways are analyzed based on variations to this energy conversion unit-process. The first of these pathways (CHP), considers combined heat and power at 35% electricity efficiency and 50% thermal efficiency (CHP 2008). CHP differentiates from BC by providing heat energy instead of combusting a fraction of the produced biogas to maintain the digester operating

temperature at 38°C. The final AD pathway (CBG) considers upgrading and compressing biogas to be used as a transportation fuel. It is assumed that vehicle fuel quality compressed biogas is 96% CH4 content. The upgrading process requires 1.6 MJ per cubic meter of biogas and the compression process 0.6 MJ per cubic meter of biogas. During both of these processes nearly 3.2% of the generated methane is lost through leakages in the system (Pertl et al. 2010).
A summary of the calculated material and energy inputs for each unit-process and pathway analyzed in this study can be found in Table 3.

30: <header>5.5 Results and Discussion </header>

Net energy production is 20.3 GJ for BC, AS, and INJ; 18.7 GJ for CTF; 27.7 GJ for BED; 25.2 GJ for CS; 28.7 GJ for SG; 26.5 GJ for CHP; and 82.3 GJ for CBG. Sustainability indicators are expressed per GJ of produced energy (electricity or compressed biogas). GHG emissions are presented in net terms, which considers that CO2(b) emitted throughout the stages of the AD pathways come from carbon uptake by corn stover, switchgrass, and the crops that constitute the dairy diet.
31: <header>5.5.4 Sustainability Indicators of the Base-Case (BC) Pathway </header>

Net GHG emissions for the BC are 243.3 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of produced electricity. N2O(b) accounts for 66% of emissions making it the major contributor to this sustainability indicator followed by CH4(b) accounting for 25%, and CO2(f) accounting for 9% coming from the combustion of diesel fuel. Nearly 63% of N20(b) is emitted during liquid digestate application and 41% from indirect emissions through NH3 volatilization. As opposed to dairy operations without AD systems, N2O(b) emissions are higher than CH4(b) emissions because the majority of

degradable VS are destroyed in the digester significantly reducing CH4(b) emissions in downstream processes. Consequently, CH4(b) is mostly emitted in the biogas production and electricity generation unit-process due to losses and leakages. In terms of unit-processes, liquid digestate application represents 50% of GHG emissions, followed by biogas production and electricity generation with 18%, liquid digestate storage with 17%, and solid digestate storage, solid digestate application, and manure collection with 5% each.

NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg NH3 GJ-1 of produced electricity. Fifty-four percent of these emissions come from liquid digestate storage, 28% from liquid digestate land application, 10% from the barn floor, 6% from digestate solid land application, and the rest from digestate solid storage. The conditions in storage do not promote the formation of a natural crust given the low TS content of the liquid digestate that reaches storage (due to both VS destruction in the digester and the separation process). The inexistence of this crust promotes NH3 emissions on top of the stored digestate through aeration. NH3 emissions from liquid digestate during the land application unit-process are also significant because digestate is surface broadcast in the BC. The effect of injection in NH3 emissions is examined in the INJ pathway. Liquid digestate (application and storage) is responsible for most NH3 emissions as ammoniacal N remains with the liquid stream after SLS.

DFF is 306.8 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy and comes only from the consumption of diesel fuel given that all electricity and heat requirements are provided by the AD system. Liquid digestate and land-application represent nearly 81% of DFF, followed by manure collection with 13%, and solid digestate land-application with 6%. Primary energy requirements for the BC are 1,570 MJ GJ-1 and consider the consumption of electricity, diesel fuel, and heat. Electricity requirements for sand recovery, biogas production and cleaning, SLS, and digestate agitation

during storage total 280 MJ GJ-1; diesel consumed during manure collection and land application is 306.8 MJ GJ-1; and heat needed to maintain the digester at 38 °C is 939 MJ GJ-1 of produced energy. To calculate the EROI ratio primary energy related to the addition of the digester in the dairy farm is determined by subtracting 507 MJ GJ1, which is the primary energy consumption of a dairy system without a digester (Aguirre-Villegas et al. 2014). Based on a total electricity production of 20.3 GJ-1 for the whole system, EROI is 0.94 and FER is 3.26 for the BC pathway.

N, P and, K are tracked throughout the unit processes to determine final nutrient availability. P and K are not affected by the unit-processes of the AD system, other than being partitioned between solid and liquid streams during SLS. P availability is 1.9 kg GJ-1 for land-applied liquid digestate and 0.7 kg GJ-1 for land-applied solid digestate. K availability is 14.3 kg GJ-1 for land- applied liquid stream and 1.6 kg GJ-1 for land-applied solid digestate. Nitrogen is affected by losses as NH3 and N2O throughout the system, and by changes in N form during the digestion process. N availability is 9.5 kg GJ-1 for land-applied liquid stream and 1.1 kg GJ-1 for land- applied solid digestate.
32: <header>5.5.4 Sustainability Indicators of the Base-Case (BC) Pathway </header>

Net GHG emissions for the BC are 243.3 kg CO2-eq GJ-1 of produced electricity. N2O(b) accounts for 66% of emissions making it the major contributor to this sustainability indicator followed by CH4(b) accounting for 25%, and CO2(f) accounting for 9% coming from the combustion of diesel fuel. Nearly 63% of N20(b) is emitted during liquid digestate application and 41% from indirect emissions through NH3 volatilization. As opposed to dairy operations without AD systems, N2O(b) emissions are higher than CH4(b) emissions because the majority of

degradable VS are destroyed in the digester significantly reducing CH4(b) emissions in downstream processes. Consequently, CH4(b) is mostly emitted in the biogas production and electricity generation unit-process due to losses and leakages. In terms of unit-processes, liquid digestate application represents 50% of GHG emissions, followed by biogas production and electricity generation with 18%, liquid digestate storage with 17%, and solid digestate storage, solid digestate application, and manure collection with 5% each.

NH3 emissions are 17.1 kg NH3 GJ-1 of produced electricity. Fifty-four percent of these emissions come from liquid digestate storage, 28% from liquid digestate land application, 10% from the barn floor, 6% from digestate solid land application, and the rest from digestate solid storage. The conditions in storage do not promote the formation of a natural crust given the low TS content of the liquid digestate that reaches storage (due to both VS destruction in the digester and the separation process). The inexistence of this crust promotes NH3 emissions on top of the stored digestate through aeration. NH3 emissions from liquid digestate during the land application unit-process are also significant because digestate is surface broadcast in the BC. The effect of injection in NH3 emissions is examined in the INJ pathway. Liquid digestate (application and storage) is responsible for most NH3 emissions as ammoniacal N remains with the liquid stream after SLS.

35: <header>5.5.5 Changes in Sustainability Indicators among AD pathways </header>

Sustainability indicators for each unit-process of the analyzed AD pathways are presented in Figures 2-5. Net GHG emissions range from 100 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1, NH3 emissions range from 4 to 18 kg GJ-1 DFF range from 176 to 566 MJ GJ-1, and N availability range from 3 to 15 kg GJ-1 of produced energy depending on the AD pathway. BED, and CHP decrease GHG emissions the most when compared to the BC with 27%, and 24% reductions respectively. Given that the FU is defined as produced energy, these reductions are mostly attributed to the higher energy efficiency of CHP, and the higher biogas production (due to a higher availability of

degradable VS from the digestate solids) in BED. BED also replaces sand with digested solids as the bedding material which avoids the production and transportation of this resource. CS reduces GHG emissions by 3.8% despite the increase due to corn stover collection and grinding and the additional purchased fertilizer needed to replace N lost through volatilization. This reduction reflects the higher electricity production rates obtained when co-digesting manure with corn stover. On the other hand, SG does not reduce GHG emissions given the increase in CH4(b) emissions from liquid digestate, which are a result of the additional VS from switchgrass that enter the storage.

INJ reduce GHG emissions by 3.1% despite the increase in N20(b) and emission from fossil fuels. N20(b) emissions are increased due to higher ammoniacal N reaching the soil as N20(b) emissions depend on ammoniacal N. Fossil emissions from the combustion of diesel fuel are also increased because digestate injection is more energy intense than surface broadcast methods. Net GHG emissions are reduced during this pathway for two reasons. First, indirect N20(b) emissions from NH3 volatilization are reduced. Second, as NH3 emissions are reduced during this pathway, there is more available N for crop production which reduces the need of purchasing N fertilizer. The AS pathway also reduces GHG emissions during collection because diesel fuel is replaced by electricity produced by the AD system. CTF is the only pathway that increases GHG emissions by 9.7%. The first source of this increase comes from higher electricity requirements of the centrifuge when compared to the screw press, which reduces overall electricity production. Second, the higher N concentrations in the solid stream increase N20(b) emissions, given that the EF for solid digestate is higher than the EF for liquid digestate.
All AD pathways are effective in reducing NH3 emissions with the exception of CTF. INJ reduces NH3 emissions by 37% due to the injection of both the liquid and solid digestate during

the land-application unit-process and avoided purchased fertilizer. Reductions of NH3 emissions from the remaining pathways are attributed to higher biogas production rates. The increase of NH3 emissions in CTF comes from both storage and land-application of solid digestate, given that more ammoniacal N is separated along with this fraction by centrifugation.

INJ and BED decrease DFF the most with 42% and 31% reductions. These reductions come from the avoided production of N fertilizer (urea) and sand bedding respectively, which shows the significant amounts of fossil energy involved in the manufacturing of these resources. AS also reduces DFF as it replaces the use of diesel with electricity generated by the AD system for the manure collection unit-process. Oppositely, CTF and CS increase DFF by 6.6%, and 6.2% respectively. DDF is increased in CS due to higher N fertilizer and diesel requirements, and in CTF due to higher electricity requirements for the centrifugation process which reduces overall energy production by the system. EROI ratios are increased by all pathways except for the CTF that reflects a 13% reduction. CHP increases EROI by 36%, showing the effectiveness of recovering heat from the electricity generation process. FER is increased in INJ by 74%, BED by 45%, CHP by 36%, and AS by 15%.

Given that the FU is produced energy, nutrient availability among pathways will change according to net energy production. Prior to dividing the sustainability indicators by the functional unit, P and K availability for the entire system present no change among AD pathways since both liquid and solid streams are land-applied in all pathways. N availability is affected mostly by losses through volatilization. As a result, N availability is increased in the INJ pathway by 42%.
Results for the CBG pathway show higher methane leakages during biogas compression and upgrading to transportation fuel quality than the losses from electricity production. Electricity

requirements for this pathway are provided with electricity from the WI grid, which has nearly 75% of its mix coming from fossil sources (i.e. coal and natural gas). The CBG pathway shows a 1.4 EROI, which is influenced by the energy intensity involved in producing WI grid electricity. Total results of GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, N availability, EROI, and FER for all AD pathways are presented in Table 4.

39: <header>5.5.5 Changes in Sustainability Indicators among AD pathways </header>

Sustainability indicators for each unit-process of the analyzed AD pathways are presented in Figures 2-5. Net GHG emissions range from 100 to 267 kg CO2-eq GJ-1, NH3 emissions range from 4 to 18 kg GJ-1 DFF range from 176 to 566 MJ GJ-1, and N availability range from 3 to 15 kg GJ-1 of produced energy depending on the AD pathway. BED, and CHP decrease GHG emissions the most when compared to the BC with 27%, and 24% reductions respectively. Given that the FU is defined as produced energy, these reductions are mostly attributed to the higher energy efficiency of CHP, and the higher biogas production (due to a higher availability of

degradable VS from the digestate solids) in BED. BED also replaces sand with digested solids as the bedding material which avoids the production and transportation of this resource. CS reduces GHG emissions by 3.8% despite the increase due to corn stover collection and grinding and the additional purchased fertilizer needed to replace N lost through volatilization. This reduction reflects the higher electricity production rates obtained when co-digesting manure with corn stover. On the other hand, SG does not reduce GHG emissions given the increase in CH4(b) emissions from liquid digestate, which are a result of the additional VS from switchgrass that enter the storage.

INJ reduce GHG emissions by 3.1% despite the increase in N20(b) and emission from fossil fuels. N20(b) emissions are increased due to higher ammoniacal N reaching the soil as N20(b) emissions depend on ammoniacal N. Fossil emissions from the combustion of diesel fuel are also increased because digestate injection is more energy intense than surface broadcast methods. Net GHG emissions are reduced during this pathway for two reasons. First, indirect N20(b) emissions from NH3 volatilization are reduced. Second, as NH3 emissions are reduced during this pathway, there is more available N for crop production which reduces the need of purchasing N fertilizer. The AS pathway also reduces GHG emissions during collection because diesel fuel is replaced by electricity produced by the AD system. CTF is the only pathway that increases GHG emissions by 9.7%. The first source of this increase comes from higher electricity requirements of the centrifuge when compared to the screw press, which reduces overall electricity production. Second, the higher N concentrations in the solid stream increase N20(b) emissions, given that the EF for solid digestate is higher than the EF for liquid digestate.
All AD pathways are effective in reducing NH3 emissions with the exception of CTF. INJ reduces NH3 emissions by 37% due to the injection of both the liquid and solid digestate during

the land-application unit-process and avoided purchased fertilizer. Reductions of NH3 emissions from the remaining pathways are attributed to higher biogas production rates. The increase of NH3 emissions in CTF comes from both storage and land-application of solid digestate, given that more ammoniacal N is separated along with this fraction by centrifugation.

INJ and BED decrease DFF the most with 42% and 31% reductions. These reductions come from the avoided production of N fertilizer (urea) and sand bedding respectively, which shows the significant amounts of fossil energy involved in the manufacturing of these resources. AS also reduces DFF as it replaces the use of diesel with electricity generated by the AD system for the manure collection unit-process. Oppositely, CTF and CS increase DFF by 6.6%, and 6.2% respectively. DDF is increased in CS due to higher N fertilizer and diesel requirements, and in CTF due to higher electricity requirements for the centrifugation process which reduces overall energy production by the system. EROI ratios are increased by all pathways except for the CTF that reflects a 13% reduction. CHP increases EROI by 36%, showing the effectiveness of recovering heat from the electricity generation process. FER is increased in INJ by 74%, BED by 45%, CHP by 36%, and AS by 15%.

Given that the FU is produced energy, nutrient availability among pathways will change according to net energy production. Prior to dividing the sustainability indicators by the functional unit, P and K availability for the entire system present no change among AD pathways since both liquid and solid streams are land-applied in all pathways. N availability is affected mostly by losses through volatilization. As a result, N availability is increased in the INJ pathway by 42%.
Results for the CBG pathway show higher methane leakages during biogas compression and upgrading to transportation fuel quality than the losses from electricity production. Electricity

requirements for this pathway are provided with electricity from the WI grid, which has nearly 75% of its mix coming from fossil sources (i.e. coal and natural gas). The CBG pathway shows a 1.4 EROI, which is influenced by the energy intensity involved in producing WI grid electricity. Total results of GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, DFF, N availability, EROI, and FER for all AD pathways are presented in Table 4.

41: <header>5.6 Conclusions and Implications </header>

Assessing the environmental impacts of different AD systems cannot be conducted with a one size fits all approach as the selected pathways have a substantial influence on results. GHG emissions can be reduced 31% by management practices alone, 24% if heat from the electricity generation process is recovered, and 4% by co-digesting manure with corn stover. Results also show that injection is an effective management practice to reduce NH3 emissions, and thus, to increase N availability of the digestate that reaches the soil after the digestion process. The increase in N availability has environmental benefits not only for GHG emissions due to avoided urea production and application, but also for DFF as manufacturing of fertilizer is very intensive in fossil energy resources. Replacing diesel fuel with electricity produced by the AD system promotes GHG emission reductions as manure is collected with an alley scraper instead of a skid-steer. Separating digestate through centrifugation is effective in concentrating P, K, and N in the solid stream, but is also more energy demanding than a screw press. Replacing sand with digested solids for cow bedding contributes to reduce DFF, NH3 emissions, and GHG emissions.

In terms of co-digestion strategies, results show that mixing manure with biomass feedstocks increases energy production. Co-digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emissions as this feedstock requires harvesting as opposed to switchgrass that needs

to be added to the already existing crop mix requiring additional planting as well as harvesting. On the other hand, SG is more effective to increase EROI and decrease DFF and NH3 emissions than CS due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock. Overall, all AD pathways increase EROI when compared to the BC except CTF as this pathway demands more energy than BC. Finally, results show that heating the digester with recovered heat from the generator improves all sustainability indicators, highlighting the potential of this pathway to maximize energy efficiency and reduce environmental impacts without adding major technical or economic challenges to the farmer.

This analysis has highlighted future work that needs to be addressed. The SG pathway assumes that there is available land to grow switchgrass, which might not be the case for the majority of dairy farms in WI. It is also assumed that the digestion system is flexible to add extra volume in the co-digestion scenarios, which might pose technical and economical limitations for the farmer. There may also be some synergistic effects when combining pathways that change management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion process that need to be analyzed. Additional pathways that explore management practices of liquid digestate storage are needed as this unit-process is a major contributor to GHG and NH3 emissions. Finally, there are additional readily available waste streams in WI (e.g. cheese whey) that could be included in the digester as biomass feedstocks.

WI internal energy supply is currently limited by the scarcity of fossil fuel reserves. The state has a clear competitive advantage for biogas production due to the availability and reliability of biomass resources, but there are some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of AD systems. Among some of the barriers are high initial investment, little information about ways to maximize profitability (i.e. co-digestion), lack of knowledge about environmental benefits, and

inexistence of specific regulations. The results of this study seek to provide useful information to farmers, researchers and policy makers in the process of overcoming these barriers. The range of biogas pathways could help current dairy farmers with AD systems to adopt new strategies or help new adopters to define specific pathways according to their priorities and needs. Moreover, the analyzed AD pathways could be included in existing policy frameworks or provide guidelines in the process of developing new climate and energy policies that adjust to the versatility of biogas at regional and national levels. This paper also seeks to provide a methodological framework when conducting AD LCA studies and serve as a reference point when including the agricultural phase of residues in the analysis.

Policy|Scope|Description - relation to biogas Revised Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS2) of the Energy Independence and Security Act (EISA, 2007)|Federal|Specifies yearly volumes of transportation renewable fuels that must be sold in the U.S., until reaching 36 billion gallons by 2022. Biogas falls under the category of undifferentiated advanced biofuels (5 billion gallons) that have to achieve 50% GHG emission reductions. U.S. EPA Clean Water Act: Wisconsin pollutant discharge elimination system (WPDES)|Federal|Applies to CAFOs and protects water quality by controlling and minimizing the potential of manure and process wastewater discharges into Wisconsin's waters. CAFOs may install digesters as a waste management tactic. U.S. EPA Clean Air Act: Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Title V Greenhouse Gas Tailoring Rule|Federal|Has included the regulation of GHG emissions from small and large stationary sources upon combustion. Dairy farms may install digesters as a mean to reduce GHG emissions. Wisconsin Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS)|State|Sets that 10% of the generated electricity by a utility has to come from renewable energy resources by 2015. Other policies and mechanisms for utilities|Federal|Net metering, feed-in-tariffs, and renewable energy certificates have been implemented in Wisconsin, but most had a voluntary approach and up to electricity utilities.
