1: Agriculture provides numerous benefits for society, but it also has some important environmental consequences. Cropland and pasture occupy nearly 40% of the earth's land surface (Foley et al. 2005) , use 3-8% of total energy consumption (FAO 2000), and emit approximately 14% of the total GHG emissions in the world (IPCC 2007). With an increasingly growing and wealthier population that demands more food and fuel, the pressure on the planet's ecosystems threatens to reach levels that cannot be sustained. Animal agriculture presents multiple challenges for sustainability. Air emissions from farms with ruminant animals introduce CH4 and N2O into the atmosphere, which are more potent than CO2. Dairy alone contributes 30% of agricultural GHG emissions and manure management is one of the most significant sources of these emissions.

Wisconsin (WI) has a well-established agricultural sector with the potential to produce bioenergy to reduce GHG emissions and achieve energy independence. The production of bioenergy must be done with caution to avoid competing with food production for land resources. The dairy industry will likely continue to dominate agricultural activities in WI, therefore the emerging bioenergy industry will have greater success when integrated into existing agricultural systems. This dissertation applies LCA principles and brings together different concepts, methods, and data sources to quantify and evaluate the environmental impacts related to dairy systems in WI and their integration with bioenergy systems.

System models like the ones developed in this dissertation can help farmers, researchers, and policy makers to identify synergies between dairy and bioenergy production. Moreover, this work provides methodological insights to improve some of the limitations of the LCA framework. These limitations include the uncertainties resulting from assumptions made during the lifecycle inventory stage, the definition of the system boundaries, and the use of different allocation methods. Due to this variability as well as to the specific characteristics of every system under study (e.g. climate, technology, management practices), this dissertation uses a pathway approach to evaluate environmental impacts. The methods used in this work can serve not only as a reference point for future LCA studies that integrate dairy and bioenergy, but can be expanded to other sectors and settings according to biomass resource availability, agricultural practices, and type of energy needed.

Chapter two uses a cradle-to-farm gate approach to determine GHG emissions, energy intensity, and direct land use of changing dairy diets to integrate co-products of the ethanol and biodiesel industries. Findings highlight that manure management is one of the main contributors to GHG emissions. Results also show that scenarios that maximize dry distillers grains with solubles in the dairy diet result in lower GHG emissions and energy intensity, but lead to higher land area requirements. The system boundary expansion approach applied in this study reduces ambiguities produced by allocation decisions at the feedstock and co-product levels, and provides a methodology that can be used in future studies that consider this integration.

Chapter three develops a novel method combining subdivision and allocation concepts to reduce the variability when partitioning the environmental impacts in a multi-functional system. This new method is applied to a system producing cheese, dried whey, and whey cream. After running several sensitivity tests, it is shown that the variability of results is significantly reduced.

The combination of subdivision and allocation more accurately reflects the inputs and outputs from the processes associated with the main product and co-products and can be easily replicated in other multifunctional systems.

Chapter four combines process-based and LCA techniques to test the environmental impacts of different manure processing pathways that include anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS). Findings suggest that AD has the largest reductions in GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels, but also the largest increases in ammonia emissions. Reduction of GHG emissions comes mostly from avoided CH4 emissions during manure storage as this gas is captured in the digester. Ammonia emissions are increased during storage and land application due to mineralization taking place through the digestion process. These high ammonia emissions are the reason why nitrogen availability rates are similar across pathways despite the fact that AD increases the ammoniacal nitrogen fraction in the digestate. Finally, the analysis shows that anaerobic digestion improves the energy return on investment (EROI) ratio by a factor up to 7 when compared to grid electricity.

Chapter five explores different management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes to reduce environmental impacts and increase energy production of anaerobic digestion processes. Results show that the combination of management practices achieves the greatest reduction in GHG emissions and that the production of biogas as a transportation fuel achieves the highest EROI ratio. Ammonia emissions are reduced by injection, which also increases nitrogen availability of the digestate reaching the soil. Co- digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emissions as this feedstock is already available on the farm. Co-digesting switchgrass with manure is more
effective to increase EROI and decrease depletion of fossil fuels and ammonia emissions due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock.

The widespread adoption of AD systems could have important implications not only for energy production, but also for fuel distribution. For example, the development of a network of digesters could create interesting opportunities to develop infrastructure for compressed natural gas fueling stations. Despite the benefits of AD, there are still some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of this technology. Among the most important are high initial investment levels, little information about ways to maximize profitability, lack of knowledge about non- economic benefits (e.g. GHG reduction), and inexistence of specific regulations. The results of this dissertation provide useful information to farmers, researchers, and policy makers in the process of overcoming these barriers.

These high ammonia emissions are the reason why nitrogen availability rates are similar across pathways despite the fact that AD increases the ammoniacal nitrogen fraction in the digestate. Finally, the analysis shows that anaerobic digestion improves the energy return on investment (EROI) ratio by a factor up to 7 when compared to grid electricity.

Chapter five explores different management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes to reduce environmental impacts and increase energy production of anaerobic digestion processes. Results show that the combination of management practices achieves the greatest reduction in GHG emissions and that the production of biogas as a transportation fuel achieves the highest EROI ratio. Ammonia emissions are reduced by injection, which also increases nitrogen availability of the digestate reaching the soil. Co- digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emissions as this feedstock is already available on the farm. Co-digesting switchgrass with manure is more
effective to increase EROI and decrease depletion of fossil fuels and ammonia emissions due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock.

The widespread adoption of AD systems could have important implications not only for energy production, but also for fuel distribution. For example, the development of a network of digesters could create interesting opportunities to develop infrastructure for compressed natural gas fueling stations. Despite the benefits of AD, there are still some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of this technology. Among the most important are high initial investment levels, little information about ways to maximize profitability, lack of knowledge about non- economic benefits (e.g. GHG reduction), and inexistence of specific regulations. The results of this dissertation provide useful information to farmers, researchers, and policy makers in the process of overcoming these barriers.
7: <header>6.1 Overview </header>

Agriculture provides numerous benefits for society, but it also has some important environmental consequences. Cropland and pasture occupy nearly 40% of the earth's land surface (Foley et al. 2005) , use 3-8% of total energy consumption (FAO 2000), and emit approximately 14% of the total GHG emissions in the world (IPCC 2007). With an increasingly growing and wealthier population that demands more food and fuel, the pressure on the planet's ecosystems threatens to reach levels that cannot be sustained. Animal agriculture presents multiple challenges for sustainability. Air emissions from farms with ruminant animals introduce CH4 and N2O into the atmosphere, which are more potent than CO2. Dairy alone contributes 30% of agricultural GHG emissions and manure management is one of the most significant sources of these emissions.

Wisconsin (WI) has a well-established agricultural sector with the potential to produce bioenergy to reduce GHG emissions and achieve energy independence. The production of bioenergy must be done with caution to avoid competing with food production for land resources. The dairy industry will likely continue to dominate agricultural activities in WI, therefore the emerging bioenergy industry will have greater success when integrated into existing agricultural systems. This dissertation applies LCA principles and brings together different concepts, methods, and data sources to quantify and evaluate the environmental impacts related to dairy systems in WI and their integration with bioenergy systems.

System models like the ones developed in this dissertation can help farmers, researchers, and policy makers to identify synergies between dairy and bioenergy production. Moreover, this work provides methodological insights to improve some of the limitations of the LCA framework. These limitations include the uncertainties resulting from assumptions made during the lifecycle inventory stage, the definition of the system boundaries, and the use of different allocation methods. Due to this variability as well as to the specific characteristics of every system under study (e.g. climate, technology, management practices), this dissertation uses a pathway approach to evaluate environmental impacts. The methods used in this work can serve not only as a reference point for future LCA studies that integrate dairy and bioenergy, but can be expanded to other sectors and settings according to biomass resource availability, agricultural practices, and type of energy needed.

Chapter two uses a cradle-to-farm gate approach to determine GHG emissions, energy intensity, and direct land use of changing dairy diets to integrate co-products of the ethanol and biodiesel industries. Findings highlight that manure management is one of the main contributors to GHG emissions. Results also show that scenarios that maximize dry distillers grains with solubles in the dairy diet result in lower GHG emissions and energy intensity, but lead to higher land area requirements. The system boundary expansion approach applied in this study reduces ambiguities produced by allocation decisions at the feedstock and co-product levels, and provides a methodology that can be used in future studies that consider this integration.

Chapter three develops a novel method combining subdivision and allocation concepts to reduce the variability when partitioning the environmental impacts in a multi-functional system. This new method is applied to a system producing cheese, dried whey, and whey cream. After running several sensitivity tests, it is shown that the variability of results is significantly reduced.

The combination of subdivision and allocation more accurately reflects the inputs and outputs from the processes associated with the main product and co-products and can be easily replicated in other multifunctional systems.

Chapter four combines process-based and LCA techniques to test the environmental impacts of different manure processing pathways that include anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS). Findings suggest that AD has the largest reductions in GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels, but also the largest increases in ammonia emissions. Reduction of GHG emissions comes mostly from avoided CH4 emissions during manure storage as this gas is captured in the digester. Ammonia emissions are increased during storage and land application due to mineralization taking place through the digestion process. These high ammonia emissions are the reason why nitrogen availability rates are similar across pathways despite the fact that AD increases the ammoniacal nitrogen fraction in the digestate. Finally, the analysis shows that anaerobic digestion improves the energy return on investment (EROI) ratio by a factor up to 7 when compared to grid electricity.

Chapter five explores different management practices, co-digestion strategies, and energy conversion processes to reduce environmental impacts and increase energy production of anaerobic digestion processes. Results show that the combination of management practices achieves the greatest reduction in GHG emissions and that the production of biogas as a transportation fuel achieves the highest EROI ratio. Ammonia emissions are reduced by injection, which also increases nitrogen availability of the digestate reaching the soil. Co- digesting corn stover with manure is an effective strategy to reduce GHG emissions as this feedstock is already available on the farm. Co-digesting switchgrass with manure is more
effective to increase EROI and decrease depletion of fossil fuels and ammonia emissions due to higher biogas production yields achieved with this feedstock.

The widespread adoption of AD systems could have important implications not only for energy production, but also for fuel distribution. For example, the development of a network of digesters could create interesting opportunities to develop infrastructure for compressed natural gas fueling stations. Despite the benefits of AD, there are still some barriers that limit the widespread adoption of this technology. Among the most important are high initial investment levels, little information about ways to maximize profitability, lack of knowledge about non- economic benefits (e.g. GHG reduction), and inexistence of specific regulations. The results of this dissertation provide useful information to farmers, researchers, and policy makers in the process of overcoming these barriers.
8: <header>6.2 Policy implications </header>

Events such as the 2010 BP oil spill and more frequent climate change related disasters prove that fossil fuels have externality costs that are currently transferred to society. It is a matter of time before these externalities are quantified and properly assigned to fossil fuels, creating incentives to produce cleaner energy products. Legislation has considered three approaches when placing an economic value on climate: carbon taxes, cap-and-trade systems, and low carbon fuel standards (LCFS). Carbon taxes and cap-and-trade systems are based on GHG emissions from direct combustion as opposed to LCFS, which are based on lifecycle emissions. To date, only LCFS have been implemented in the U.S., California being the first state. The California LCFS considers LCA pathways for transportation fossil fuels and biofuels and incorporates GHG emission results into the policy itself as a reference point for specific pathways. Success of this

initiative will generate the incentives for its widespread implementation across states. From a policy perspective, this dissertation has defined bioenergy pathways that could be included in existing policy frameworks, such as the LCFS. In addition, it provides guidelines in the process of developing new climate and energy policies at regional and national levels. A case study on how the results of this dissertation could be applied in the policy making process is presented in the Supplementary Information section.

13: <header>6.5 Supplementary Information: Case Study </header>
How can the results of this dissertation be applied in the policy making process: The inclusion of biofuel pathways in the Low Carbon Fuel Standard

The California LCFS is the first major public initiative to integrate lifecycle concepts and carbon emissions into a law in the U.S. In 2009, California presented a proposed regulation to implement the LCFS that requires a reduction of the carbon intensity of the transportation fuels used in California by an average of 10% by the year 2020. The LCFS will be applicable to all refineries, blenders, and producers of transportation fuels and will consider all emissions coming from direct and indirect sources associated with the full lifecycle of a transportation fuel (well- to-wheels for fossil fuels and field-to-wheels for biofuels). Basically, the LCFS requires that the overall carbon intensity of the regulated providers of transportation fuels (regulated parties) meet each year's carbon intensity levels for the pool of their transportation fuels (CARB 2009).

As opposed to volumetric mandates such as the Renewable Fuel Standard, the LCFS focuses on GHG emission reductions rather than a fixed production volume of a biofuel. This aspect will give fuel companies flexibility on how to bring down their carbon intensity by innovating their processes, using low carbon feestocks (e.g. biomass or wastes), and considering sustainable management farming (Sperling and Yeh 2010). The options mentioned in the standard include: blending with low carbon fuels, increasing the sales of low carbon fuels, and applying purchased or accumulated credits from previous years within the transportation sector. Credits will be based on the amount of fuels sold, carbon intensity of those fuels, and efficiency of the vehicles that use them (CARB 2009). One key aspect of the LCFS is that it will allow the trading and banking of emission credits. According to Sperling and Yeh (2010), this combination of regulatory and
market mechanisms is what makes LCFS more robust and durable than pure regulatory or market approaches such as the carbon tax and cap-and-trade proposals.

In the California LCFS, carbon intensity is calculated by LCA which characterizes fuel pathways rather than standardized fuels. The well-to-tank analysis of the LCFS is based on the Greenhouse Gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy Use in Transportation model known as GREET. The system boundaries of these pathways include two emission sources: direct GHG from producing and using the fuels, and indirect GHG that are also associated to that fuel. For example, corn ethanol can have different pathways (called sub-pathways) associated with its direct emissions depending on the steps that contribute to the total carbon intensity of the fuel. These steps are: energy consumption in the farm, management practices in the farm, crop yields, production process of the fuel (e.g. dry mill or wet mill); energy efficiency and fuel used in the plant; value of co-products, transportation, and final use.

According to CARB the carbon intensity values of different fuel pathways will be published in a "lookup table" SO producers and importers can easily identify the pathway that applies best to their respective fuel chains. The reference pathways will not be fixed as the Standard includes the options for modifications and additions of other pathways presented by the regulated parties themselves. This flexibility has the objective to encourage innovation and reductions in total GHG emissions across any stage of the fuel's lifecycle that will be classified by the LCA methodology (CARB 2009)

The main indirect sources of emissions in the LCFS come from land use changes from the production of crop-based biofuels as it was identified as the only significant source for additional GHG emissions (Andress et al. 2010). To assess the impact of those emissions, the LCFS staff used a global trade model called The Global Trade Analysis Project (GTAP), which has a long
history in modeling complex international economic effects. Basically, this model evaluates land use conversion related with the production of crops for biofuels at a worldwide level.

In addition to California, there are other States that are currently working on low carbon fuel standards such as Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Rohde Island, Vermont, and Minessota (Andress et al. 2010). When available, their results will be very valuable for discussions as they have different biofuel pathways, geographic characteristics, and priorities.

The California LCFS is the first major public initiative to integrate lifecycle concepts and carbon emissions into a law in the U.S. In 2009, California presented a proposed regulation to implement the LCFS that requires a reduction of the carbon intensity of the transportation fuels used in California by an average of 10% by the year 2020. The LCFS will be applicable to all refineries, blenders, and producers of transportation fuels and will consider all emissions coming from direct and indirect sources associated with the full lifecycle of a transportation fuel (well- to-wheels for fossil fuels and field-to-wheels for biofuels). Basically, the LCFS requires that the overall carbon intensity of the regulated providers of transportation fuels (regulated parties) meet each year's carbon intensity levels for the pool of their transportation fuels (CARB 2009).

As opposed to volumetric mandates such as the Renewable Fuel Standard, the LCFS focuses on GHG emission reductions rather than a fixed production volume of a biofuel. This aspect will give fuel companies flexibility on how to bring down their carbon intensity by innovating their processes, using low carbon feestocks (e.g. biomass or wastes), and considering sustainable management farming (Sperling and Yeh 2010). The options mentioned in the standard include: blending with low carbon fuels, increasing the sales of low carbon fuels, and applying purchased or accumulated credits from previous years within the transportation sector. Credits will be based on the amount of fuels sold, carbon intensity of those fuels, and efficiency of the vehicles that use them (CARB 2009). One key aspect of the LCFS is that it will allow the trading and banking of emission credits. According to Sperling and Yeh (2010), this combination of regulatory and
market mechanisms is what makes LCFS more robust and durable than pure regulatory or market approaches such as the carbon tax and cap-and-trade proposals.

In the California LCFS, carbon intensity is calculated by LCA which characterizes fuel pathways rather than standardized fuels. The well-to-tank analysis of the LCFS is based on the Greenhouse Gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy Use in Transportation model known as GREET. The system boundaries of these pathways include two emission sources: direct GHG from producing and using the fuels, and indirect GHG that are also associated to that fuel. For example, corn ethanol can have different pathways (called sub-pathways) associated with its direct emissions depending on the steps that contribute to the total carbon intensity of the fuel. These steps are: energy consumption in the farm, management practices in the farm, crop yields, production process of the fuel (e.g. dry mill or wet mill); energy efficiency and fuel used in the plant; value of co-products, transportation, and final use.

According to CARB the carbon intensity values of different fuel pathways will be published in a "lookup table" SO producers and importers can easily identify the pathway that applies best to their respective fuel chains. The reference pathways will not be fixed as the Standard includes the options for modifications and additions of other pathways presented by the regulated parties themselves. This flexibility has the objective to encourage innovation and reductions in total GHG emissions across any stage of the fuel's lifecycle that will be classified by the LCA methodology (CARB 2009)

The main indirect sources of emissions in the LCFS come from land use changes from the production of crop-based biofuels as it was identified as the only significant source for additional GHG emissions (Andress et al. 2010). To assess the impact of those emissions, the LCFS staff used a global trade model called The Global Trade Analysis Project (GTAP), which has a long
history in modeling complex international economic effects. Basically, this model evaluates land use conversion related with the production of crops for biofuels at a worldwide level.

In addition to California, there are other States that are currently working on low carbon fuel standards such as Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Rohde Island, Vermont, and Minessota (Andress et al. 2010). When available, their results will be very valuable for discussions as they have different biofuel pathways, geographic characteristics, and priorities.
